visits.text: Return toward Burqin then Altay, capital of the prefecture of the same name. The city is modern without great architectural interest, but it harbors an extraordinary fact: the birthplace of skiing worldwide. In 2005, 5,000-year-old skis were found in a cave in the neighboring mountains - predating Scandinavian Nordic skis by several millennia. Visit the small Altay Ski Museum, which presents replicas and the history of this discovery.
Stroll through General Park along the Kelan River: monument to pioneers, swans in season, locals practicing tai chi in the morning. A quiet hour to catch your breath after the mountain roads.

If you have time, Altay's central market is worth a visit: fragrant Hami melons, dried fruits (Turpan raisins, apricots, jujubes), Uyghur spices. Good place to stock up before the long road to the Ili valley.

activities.text: Try grilled whole Irtysh River fish, a regional specialty - often pike or pikeperch, locally caught. Several popular restaurants line the south bank. Expect 80-150 yuan for a good-sized fish.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Xinjiang - Kanas, Altai, and Ili Steppes in 16 Days

visits.text: On the road between Zhongwei and Bayanhot, stop at Moon Lake (Yueliang Hu) in the heart of the Tengger Desert. Covering 3 square kilometers, this freshwater lake has a peculiarity: its shape resembles China seen from above, and it's divided in two by reed beds—half freshwater, half saltwater. Rated 5A site, ideal for breaking up a long 5.5-hour desert drive. Allow one hour on site.

Transitional stop in Bayanhot (seat of Alxa Left Banner), the mandatory departure point for reaching Badain Jaran. Here you'll meet your 4x4 convoy and local guide—the desert expedition absolutely cannot be done independently. Take the opportunity to visit the Alxa Museum, which presents local Mongolian culture and the geology of the region's three great deserts.

Last "civilized" night before the great desert. Bayanhot is a quiet little Mongolian town with a lamaist temple (Yanfu Monastery) and a few shopping streets where you can find necessary equipment. Buy water, high-protection sunscreen, a scarf, a headlamp.

activities.text: Safety briefing with your guide for the Badain Jaran expedition: 3 days, 4 possible nights in yurts near interior lakes, no cell service, sand at 40°C by day and 5°C at night. Pack your bags: everything non-essential stays in Bayanhot.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner Mongolia: Steppes, Dunes, and Forgotten Empires

visits.text: Huangguoshu Waterfall, on the Baishui River, anchors a cluster of 18 cascades spread across 163 km². The main waterfall measures 67 meters high and 83.3 meters wide—the largest single-drop waterfall in Asia. The 17th-century traveler Xu Xiake described it as mist bursting from rock. Behind the curtain of water, Water Curtain Cave extends 134 meters with six natural windows, five chambers, and three springs. On sunny days, a rainbow forms above Rhinoceros Pool.

Doupotang Waterfall, 1 km upstream, is the widest in the group at 105 meters. It served as the filming location for the 1986 television series "Journey to the West," where monk Tang and his disciples cross the river. Before floods, it emits a low rumble that earned it the nickname "roaring waterfall."

Tianxingqiao scenic area, 7 km downstream, covers 4.5 km² of karst landscape where stones, trees, and water form natural compositions. Silver Chain Waterfall falls in parallel threads like a pearl necklace into a turquoise pool.

activities.text: Walk through Gaodang Stone Village, 6 km from the waterfalls: houses, bridges, roofs, and kitchen utensils carved from stone by the Buyi people. The village, founded during the Ming dynasty, forms a circle crossed by a river spanned by a five-arch bridge whose reflection draws a perfect circle.

days_plan.itin_title: Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian

places.name: Anyang

visits.text: Yinxu, in Anyang, is one of the world's most significant archaeological sites. It was the last capital of the Shang dynasty, occupied around 1300–1046 BC, rediscovered in 1899 thanks to oracle bones being sold as "dragon bones" to pharmacies. Over 160,000 bone fragments have been unearthed, bearing the earliest known Chinese inscriptions. Inscribed as a UNESCO site in 2006. Budget 3 hours to truly grasp it, ideally with an audio guide.

The Tomb of Fu Hao, within the palace compound, is the only intact Shang royal tomb ever found. Discovered in 1976, it belonged to the consort of King Wu Ding (around 1200 BC). Fu Hao was a queen, priestess, and general: oracle bones record that she led 13,000 soldiers into battle—the largest force ever documented. Nearly 1,928 objects were excavated: 468 bronzes, 755 jades, cowrie-shell currency, and human sacrifices.

The new Yinxu Museum, opened February 26, 2024, is a revelation. 1.8 million visitors in one year. It assembles exceptional Shang bronzes (including a replica of the Houmuwu Ding, the world's largest ancient bronze at 832 kg), original oracle bones, and war chariots. If you see only one thing in Anyang, make it this.

Right next door, the National Museum of Chinese Writing traces the evolution of characters, from oracle bones to Zhou bronzes to the Tang. Of the 4,300 characters identified on oracle bones, 1,600 have been deciphered so far. It's fascinating if you read or are learning Chinese—more arid otherwise.

activities.text: At the Yinxu Museum restaurant, they serve noodles decorated with oracle-bone characters printed in edible squid ink. Tourist gimmick, sure, but it's fun and unexpected after a morning of archaeology.

days_plan.itin_title: Spiritual Henan & Shaanxi – Tracing the Cradle of Chinese Civilization

map_keywords: National Museum of Chinese Writing Anyang

visits.text: The 4x4 convoy leaves Bayanhot early in the morning to plunge into the Badain Jaran Desert. Allow 5-6 hours of track to reach Badain Hu Lake, the heart of the desert. Inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list in July 2024, Badain Jaran is China's 3rd largest desert (49,000 sq km) and home to the world's tallest stationary dunes. Bilutu Peak tops 1,611 meters in altitude, with a relative height of 460 meters—the "Everest of sands."

On arrival, the contrast is striking: 144 lakes in incredible colors—turquoise, emerald, some pink or red—nestle between the mega-dunes. The name "Badain Jaran" means "mysterious lakes" in Mongolian. Many are saline (up to 330 g/L of salt), a few are freshwater. They're fed by underground springs from distant mountains.

Visit the Badain Jaran Temple, built in 1755 under the Qing Dynasty on the shore of a lake. Nicknamed the "Forbidden City of the Desert," it's a Tibetan Buddhist monastery miraculously preserved: its total isolation saved it from the Cultural Revolution. Inside, original murals, prayer wheels, and a few monks who still maintain daily rituals. 273 square meters, two stories.

Today, ascent of Bilutu Peak, the world's tallest stationary dune. The 4x4 drops you at the base; you climb on foot—2 hours of effort in soft sand, three steps forward for two steps back. At the summit, 360-degree panorama over the ocean of dunes and seven interior lakes. The crest is sharp as a razor blade.

Discover the magical Yinderitu Spring, a tiny salt lake at whose heart gushes a 3-square-meter reef pierced by 108 freshwater springs. Unexplained hydrological mystery: the water is sweet, rich in trace elements. Mongolian princes of Alxa used to have it transported by camel to their palace. Even today, two Germans come to drink it every year.

Nuoritu Lake, the largest and deepest in the desert (1.47 sq km, 28 meters deep). Reeds shelter surprising fauna: migrating swans, wild ducks, geese, mollusks, and even fish in moderately saline water. Picnic lunch in the shade of rare trees growing at the water's edge.

Final day in the desert. The 4x4 convoy takes you across the mega-dunes westward, toward the desert exit. Driving is extreme: 40-degree slopes, controlled sliding descents, stops to dig out vehicles stuck in sand. Hold your belongings tight—the 4x4 jolts hard.

Discover the least-visited landscapes: wind-sculpted stone forests, hidden oases with desert poplars (huyang) that can live 1,000 years, sometimes exposed fossils—Badain Jaran was once a seabed 100 million years ago. Your guide easily spots tracks of gazelles and foxes.

Exit the desert and arrive in Alxa Right Banner (Ehji-Ohlo), a small town marking the end of the Badain Jaran expedition. Allow about 6 hours of 4x4 from the desert heart. This is the time to thank your guide and the team—tips are expected, around 200 yuan per day per person for the lead guide.

activities.text: Check into the yurt camp by the lake, run by a Mongolian family. Sunset over the dunes—the light shifts in ten minutes from bright yellow to deep red to mauve. Silence is total, save for wind sculpting the sand. Simple Mongolian meal: boiled mutton, fermented milk, fire-baked bread.

Stargazing from a neighboring dune. No light pollution for hundreds of kilometers. The Milky Way, constellations, sometimes Starlink satellites in single file. Lie down in the still-warm sand, stay a good hour for your eyes to adapt.

Slide on the singing sands of Baoritaolegai, nicknamed the "kingdom of singing dunes." When you rush down the slope, the sand produces a thunderous roar exceeding 105 decibels—the most powerful in the world. Phenomenon linked to electrostatic effects between grains, still poorly understood.

Evening around the fire with Mongolian herders who tend the desert's camel flocks. Stories told by the guide, Mongolian vodka, camel cheese. Night falls at 9 PM in summer, followed by dry cold that quickly drops to 5°C. Slip into the yurt early.

Hot shower (finally!) at the Alxa Right Banner hotel, a luxury forgotten for three days. Hearty Chinese meal: vegetable noodles, dumplings, cold beer. Rest up—tomorrow is a big transit day to Jiayuguan.

Short walk around town to see the main square, the local lamaist temple, and the bazaar. If you encounter Mongolian herders in deel (traditional coat), greet them with "sain bain uu"—hello in Mongolian. A smile does the rest.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner Mongolia: Steppes, Dunes, and Forgotten Empires
Inner Mongolia: Steppes, Dunes, and Forgotten Empires
Inner Mongolia: Steppes, Dunes, and Forgotten Empires

visits.text: Baihaba is arguably Xinjiang's most beautiful village - National Geographic China ranked it number one in 2005. Perched on the Kazakhstan border, only 1.5 km from the line, it's off-limits to foreigners without a border permit (request 3 months in advance). A hundred Tuva and Kazakh cabins, ancient weeping willows along the river, pastures stretching forever. The atmosphere is even more pristine than Hemu - fewer tourists, few shops, horses grazing in the streets.

Don't miss the thousand-year-old willow in the village center, under which elders still gather. Locals say it was planted by a Tuva ancestor seven centuries ago; dendrochronological dating suggests rather 400-500 years, which remains exceptional.

Take the trail leading to the west hill for the overview. The village appears as a wooden nest wrapped in meadows, with the first Kazakh foothills as backdrop. At sunset, light gilds the roofs and the river glitters orange.

activities.text: Stay with a Tuva family - a few houses open one or two rooms, with wooden bed, wool quilt, and wood stove. Spartan comfort but strong human experience. In the morning, you share salted milk tea and fried doughnuts.

Walk early toward the border post (without crossing it, obviously): 1.5 km from the village, easy trail. You'll see the barbed wire fence marking the limit with Kazakhstan, and watchtowers on each side. Strange feeling of being at the end of Chinese territory.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Xinjiang - Kanas, Altai, and Ili Steppes in 16 Days

visits.text: Transitional stop in Baotou, a steel city on the northern bend of the Yellow River. Pause at Wudangzhao Monastery, 70 km northeast of the city. It's the largest Gelug branch Tibetan Buddhist monastery in Inner Mongolia, founded in 1749. Six grand halls, over 2,500 statues, still active. More authentic than Hohhot's temples, far less crowded.

Quick stop on the banks of the Yellow River to see the water's color: ochre, laden with silt from the Loess Plateau. The river makes its great northward bend here, forming the "Ordos Loop" that gives the Kubuqi Desert its distinctive shape (named "bowstring" in Mongolian).

days_plan.itin_title: Inner Mongolia: Steppes, Dunes, and Forgotten Empires

visits.text: Departing Markham, you cross the bridge over the Jinsha Jiang—the administrative border between TAR and Sichuan. From here on, no more Tibet permit required; you're officially in Sichuan, within the Tibetan autonomous prefecture of Garzê (Kham Tibet). Batang at 2,560 m is the lowest altitude since Shangri-La—your body will appreciate it.

Visit Batang Chode Monastery (Ba Chode), a large Gelugpa complex founded in the 15th century at 2,600 m. Destroyed during the Cultural Revolution and rebuilt in the 1990s, it houses around a hundred monks, morning chants at 7 AM. Far less visited than famous sites—you'll likely be alone.

Wander Batang valley, which produces Sichuan's finest apricots: in May-June, the blossoming trees are spectacular. The town retains authentic Tibetan character—cobbled alleys, painted rammed-earth houses, little concrete. You'll hear many residents speaking Kham Tibetan rather than Mandarin.

activities.text: On the road between Markham and Batang, ask your driver to stop at Maoya Grasslands: 4,200 m altitude, herds of yaks and sheep, nomad tents. The word maoya means in Tibetan "flat as a bronze mirror." The horizon is without relief, creating a unique sense of vastness.

Dine early and light: in Batang you're recovering from altitude after several days above 3,500 m—take advantage of it to sleep well. Tomorrow you climb back to Litang at 4,014 m; the contrast will be brutal.

days_plan.itin_title: Three Parallel Rivers & Eastern Tibet - 14-Day Yunnan to Kham Extension

visits.text: Bayanbulak means 'rich spring' in Mongolian - and the grassland delivers on that promise. China's second-largest, at 2,500 m altitude, it extends over 23,000 km² in the heart of the Tianshan. The Torghut people, a branch of the Oirat Mongols, have lived here since 1771, after an epic migration: leaving the Volga in January, 170,000 of them marched for 8 months to Xinjiang, only 70,000 arrived, the rest dead from cold, hunger, or Cossack attacks. Emperor Qianlong granted them these lands.

Swan Lake (Tianer Hu) is the world's highest swan reserve, at 2,400 m. Established in 1980, it's the only one in China. More than 6,000 whooper swans nest here from April to November, along with 120 bird species. The observation walkway is 200 m from the marsh. Bring binoculars, swans keep their distance.

Balun Kure Temple is a mobile Tibetan Buddhist sanctuary, vestige of the seven portable temples of the Torghuts during their migration. It's now the only one still preserved in China. Small but fascinating, it testifies to a Buddhism-shamanism syncretism unique to the Mongols.

Second day at Bayanbulak to enjoy the grassland during hours without tour buses. Wake before 6 AM to watch sunrise over the Kaidu: mist at water level, first orange rays, herds heading out to pasture. It's a different spectacle, quieter than sunset.

Visit a Torghut camp off the tourist trail. The family welcomes you into the yurt - 5 meters diameter, wooden lattice, sheep felt, top opening for smoke. Salted milk tea, fresh butter, dried cheese. Conversation happens in Torghut language, translated by your guide.

If the season permits, attend the Nadam Fair, annual Mongol festival held on the 4th day of the lunar calendar's 6th month (generally late June-early July). Traditional wrestling, horse races with 6-12 year old children, archery, dances. The best Torghut riders come from throughout the region.

activities.text: The Nine Meanders and Eighteen Bends of the Kaidu River are THE emblematic photo of Xinjiang. At sunset, nine reflections of the sun appear simultaneously in the river's curves - hence its nickname. The Basirik platform is the best viewpoint. Arrive 1.5 hours early to get a spot. Cult filming location for the 2019 movie 'Pegasus'.

Horseback ride on the grassland with a Torghut herder, about 80 yuan for 1 hour. You'll ride along the river, pass flocks of Bayanbulak black-headed sheep - a breed adapted to -45°C, recognized as a 'living fossil' of local pastoralism.

Taste Bayanbulak lamb - a breed raised above 3,000 m altitude, fine meat, very lean. Traditional Torghut cooking: whole on embers for 4 hours, then carved at table. Several yurt camps offer this feast by reservation for 6-8 people. Expect 800-1,200 yuan for a whole sheep.

Night in a yurt under the Milky Way. At this altitude and with zero light pollution, the sky is extraordinary. Nights stay cool even in July (5-10°C), yurts are heated with yak dung stoves. Unforgettable experience.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Xinjiang - Kanas, Altai, and Ili Steppes in 16 Days
Northern Xinjiang - Kanas, Altai, and Ili Steppes in 16 Days

places.name: Beijing

visits.text: The Forbidden City, built between 1406 and 1420 under Emperor Yongle, sprawls across 178 acres and contains 980 buildings connected by courtyards paved with 12 million bricks. Home to 24 emperors of the Ming and Qing dynasties for five centuries, it now houses the Palace Museum and its vast imperial collections—paintings, bronzes, ceramics, lacquerware. The yellow roofs—a color reserved for the emperor—crown an ensemble that's been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1987. Start at the Gate of Heavenly Peace, the monumental entrance topped with Mao Zedong's portrait since 1949, then cross through the three great ceremonial halls of the outer court.

Climb Jingshan (Coal Hill), an artificial mound rising 354 feet, built from earth excavated from the Forbidden City's moats. From the summit, you'll get a sweeping view over the entire expanse of golden palace roofs. This is where the last Ming emperor hanged himself in 1644 when an army of 400,000 peasants stormed Beijing.

The Temple of Heaven, built in 1420 under Emperor Yongle and expanded in the 16th century under Jiajing, covers 673 acres—four times the size of the Forbidden City. The Hall of Prayer for Good Harvests, a 125-foot-tall rotunda with a 98-foot diameter, rests on 28 wooden pillars assembled without a single nail or drop of cement. The four central pillars symbolize the seasons, the next twelve represent the months. The emperor came here twice a year to pray to Heaven for bountiful harvests. Struck by lightning in 1889, the hall was rebuilt identically the following year. It's been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1998.

Step into the hutongs of the Nanluoguxiang quarter, one of Beijing's oldest alleyways with over 800 years of history. These narrow passages date back to the Yuan dynasty (1271-1368)—the word 'hutong' likely comes from the Mongolian 'hottog,' meaning 'well.' The courtyard houses (siheyuan) are arranged around small courtyards planted with pomegranate trees, a symbol of fertility. Beijing had 3,216 of them in 1944; fewer than 1,000 remain today.

The Mutianyu section of the Great Wall, 45 miles from Beijing, was first built in the mid-6th century under the Northern Qi dynasty, then reconstructed in granite in 1569 by General Qi Jiguang during the Ming period. The restored stretch runs 7,380 feet and features 22 watchtowers—an unusual density, with a tower roughly every 330 feet. The walls reach 23 to 26 feet in height and 13 to 16 feet in width at the top. Unique feature: crenellations line both sides of the walkway, which is exceptional—on most other sections, they only protect the outer side. The Mutianyu Pass, with its three interconnected watchtowers (Zhengguantai), forms a defensive structure found nowhere else on the Wall.

The Summer Palace, designed in 1750 by Emperor Qianlong to celebrate his mother's 60th birthday, covers 716 acres—three-quarters of which are occupied by Kunming Lake. The emperor had 100,000 workers excavate and enlarge the lake, using the earth to raise Longevity Hill. Destroyed in 1860 by Franco-British troops during the Second Opium War, it was rebuilt in 1886 by Empress Cixi—who diverted funds meant for naval modernization. The Long Corridor, a covered gallery stretching 2,388 feet, is adorned with thousands of paintings on wood depicting landscapes, flowers, and scenes from Chinese literature. At the lake's edge, Cixi's marble boat, 118 feet long, remains moored on the north shore. It's been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1998.

The 798 Art District occupies a former industrial complex of 6.9 million square feet built in the 1950s by East German architects in Bauhaus style. Factory 718, intended for manufacturing military electronic components, was divided into six units in the 1960s—including Unit 798, which gave the district its name. After subsidies dried up in the early 1990s, the large workshops with high ceilings and north-facing skylights attracted artists drawn by cheap rents. Today there are over 200 galleries, including UCCA (107,000 square feet of exhibition space) and Tang Contemporary Art.

activities.text: Watch the changing of the guard at Tiananmen Gate and cross the world's largest public square (109 acres).
Rise early to watch local residents at the Temple of Heaven Park practicing tai chi, dancing, singing, and playing cards beneath centuries-old cypresses—an unchanging morning ritual.
Take a rickshaw ride through the Shichahai hutongs, Beijing's largest protected quarter, winding between lakes, weeping willows, and Qing dynasty mansions.

Take the cable car up to Tower 14, then hike the ramparts between Towers 14 and 20 (western section) with panoramic views over forested hills—vegetation covers over 96% of the area. You can descend by toboggan from Tower 6, a slide that winds through the forest.

Cross the Seventeen-Arch Bridge, built in 1750 under Qianlong: 490 feet long, it links the lakeshore to Nanhu Island and bears over 500 carved stone lions, each one different. From the bridge's midpoint, you can count nine arches on each side—the number nine symbolized imperial power.

days_plan.itin_title: Classic China: Beijing, Xi'an & Shanghai
Classic China: Beijing, Xi'an & Shanghai
Classic China: Beijing, Xi'an & Shanghai
Classic China: Beijing, Xi'an & Shanghai
Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie
Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie
Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie

map_keywords: Hall of Prayer for Good Harvests Beijing

visits.text: Stopover in Beitun, a pivot town built by the Bingtuan - the Xinjiang Production and Construction Corps, a paramilitary organization founded in 1954 to clear and colonize these lands. The small local Bingtuan museum tells this singular history: demobilized soldiers from the People's Liberation Army turned farmer-soldiers, massive irrigation projects, birth of state farms. It's a little-known page of Chinese history, and Beitun is one of the ten Bingtuan administrative divisions still active today.

Beitun Reservoir, north of the city, is fed by the Irtysh. Quick stroll along the banks, opportunity to spot migratory birds in spring and autumn. Good break to stretch your legs before continuing on to Burqin.

activities.text: Lunch in a Beitun workers' canteen - the surrounding state farms bring fresh produce: watermelons, Manas tomatoes, Altai lamb. Simple, cheap meal (30-50 yuan), authentic to Bingtuan culture.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Xinjiang - Kanas, Altai, and Ili Steppes in 16 Days

places.name: Benzilan

visits.text: Stop at the First Bend of the Yangtze viewpoint, one of China's most startling geographic marvels. The river, charging south, slams into bedrock and executes a 180° reversal northward. The official lookout sits 15 km north of Benzilan—it's the only spot with an unobstructed view of the entire loop; elsewhere you'll catch only half. Visit in spring or autumn for turquoise water; summer rains turn it muddy.

Climb 23 km north of Benzilan to Dongzhulin Monastery, clinging to the slope at 3,000 m. Founded in 1667 under Emperor Kangxi and named "Gaden Döndrubling" by the 5th Dalai Lama in 1676, it ranks among the thirteen great Gelugpa monasteries of Kham. The current structure, rebuilt in 1985 after Cultural Revolution destruction, rises four storeys with 82 carved wooden pillars. On the third floor, you'll find two Maitreya and Sakyamuni Buddhas standing 7 and 10 m tall. Far less crowded than Songzanlin.

Beyond Benzilan, you tackle the G214 toward Baima Pass (4,292 m), one of the highest crossings between Yunnan and Mount Meili. The vegetation shifts abruptly here: virgin spruce forests, rhododendrons, prayer flags. Pause at 4,000 m to watch for Himalayan griffons—this zone is one of the last refuges for red pandas and clouded leopards.

activities.text: In Benzilan village, try your hand at carving a Tibetan wooden bowl. This crucial Tea-Horse Road stopover was the last village before the snowy passes to Lhasa. Budget 1 to 2 hours, around 100 yuan—you leave with your own bowl.

days_plan.itin_title: Three Parallel Rivers & Eastern Tibet - 14-Day Yunnan to Kham Extension

map_keywords: Jinsha River First Bend Moon Bay

visits.text: Burqin marks the gateway to the north, the antechamber to Kanas. You'll arrive mid-afternoon from Beitun. Head for the Five-Colored River (Wucai Tan of Burqin), 24 km west, along the Irtysh. This is China's only river that flows into the Arctic Ocean. The clay cliffs display seven hues at sunset, and the contrast with the turquoise water is spectacular. Allow 1.5 hours on site.

Make a detour via the Irtysh bridge at the edge of town to see the river flowing north - a unique geographic fact in China, as all other major rivers flow east or south. Brief 20-minute stop, explanatory signs in Chinese and English.

Burqin's night market is one of the best in northern Xinjiang. Fresh fish caught from the Irtysh grilled whole (the pikeperch is delicious), lamb skewers, Kazakh cheese, kvas (Russian fermented drink). Noisy, bright, lively atmosphere.

activities.text: If you arrive early enough, walk down to the Irtysh bank for a peaceful stroll. Local fishermen cast their lines in the evening - strike up a conversation, they're curious about foreigners and often offer tea.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Xinjiang - Kanas, Altai, and Ili Steppes in 16 Days

visits.text: Chaka Salt Lake ("salt lake" in Tibetan), at 3,059 meters elevation, covers 105 km²—ten times the area of Hangzhou's West Lake. Its salt layer reaches 5 meters deep in places. In calm weather, the lake's surface reflects the sky and snowy Qilian Mountains like a perfect mirror, earning it the nickname "Mirror of the Sky." National Tourism Geographic Magazine ranked it among the "55 places to see in a lifetime."

The old salt railway tracks, built in the 1950s to transport production, extend into the lake. Now abandoned, they serve as a photographic promenade. A tourist train runs between different areas of the site. Salt exploitation in the region dates back over 3,000 years, to the era of the Qiang people.

activities.text: Photography at sunrise or sunset on Chaka Lake—the best times for the mirror effect are before 9am and after 5pm, when the wind is low and light rakes across the surface. Wear brightly colored clothing (red, white, yellow) for reflections. Don't walk barefoot on the salt crust (cuts).

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou

visits.text: You've reached the gates of Changbaishan, in Manchu "Ever-White Mountain," inscribed in 2024 on the UNESCO Global Geoparks list. The massif is a volcano whose last major eruption dates to 946 - one of the most violent in the past 2,000 years, with ash found as far as Japan. Now dormant, it's under constant surveillance from the Tianchi observatory since 1999. Base yourself in Erdaobaihe, the small base town at the foot of the north slope.

The Changbai Waterfall is today's attraction. Water from Heaven Lake flows through a single natural outlet, the Erdaobai River, and plunges 68 meters - one of the world's highest volcanic waterfalls. In winter, it continues to flow partially, framed by enormous blue ice formations. The access trail crosses a valley of hot springs that steam at -25°C. Allow two hours round trip from the north entrance.

At the Changbai Hot Springs, near the waterfall, water gushes out at over 80°C. Vendors dunk eggs in wire baskets and sell them for 10 yuan each - the yolk cooks hard, the white stays oddly milky due to the water's mineral composition. A curiosity to taste.

Decisive day: heading to Tianchi, Heaven Lake. From the north gate, you first take a shuttle then a mandatory 4x4 for the final 16 kilometers to the summit. The lake sits in a caldera 5 km in diameter, at 2,189 m altitude. Maximum depth 384 m. In winter, it's entirely frozen under a flat white ice sheet, and is visible only about 50 days per year - weather changes brutally here. If the sky is clear, the panorama is unforgettable.

On the way back, stop at the Underground Forest (Dixia Senlin). A volcanic fissure 60 m deep shelters a forest of centuries-old pines and birches below the plateau. The trail descends via wooden stairs to the bottom. Surprising geological view: the collapse dates back to the 946 eruption.

The Changbaishan Grand Canyon, open on the west slope but visible from certain points in the north zone, measures 70 km long and up to 100 m deep. Carved by tectonic faults and erosion, it's sculpted with towers of volcanic rock. In winter, it's whitened by snow and particularly photogenic in late afternoon.

Today, switch to Changbaishan's west slope (allow 100 km of road from the north slope). The atmosphere changes completely: fewer tourists, more austere volcanic landscapes, access to Tianchi via 1,442 stone steps. The effort is earned - the panorama here is broader than the north side, with a plunging view of the lake and, on clear days, of North Korean peaks.

The Yalu Grand Canyon (not to be confused with the Changbaishan Grand Canyon further north) begins here. Over 10 km, the river has carved a gorge 200 m wide and 170 m deep in volcanic rock. The wooden trail follows the cliff. In winter, the river below marks the border with North Korea - you're just a few meters from the other side.

Jinjiang Waterfall, on the west slope, plunges 50 m in free fall then cascades another 20 m. Much less known than Changbai's waterfall, it's more powerful and stretches 70 m wide. In winter, it partially freezes into impressive blue ice columns.

activities.text: Soak in the baths at the thermal spa at the mountain's base after your visit. Several establishments (Pureland Hot Spring, Wanda Resort) offer outdoor pools where steam envelops everything. Picture the scene: -25°C in the air, water at 40°C, snow on the surrounding firs. Expect 200 to 400 yuan depending on the complex.

In the evening, sample the Manchu-Korean hotpot typical of the region: deer meat, wild mushrooms, fresh ginseng (Jilin is China's leading producer) and potato noodles. Erdaobaihe's restaurants are simple but warm. This is also where you drink the local ginseng baijiu, to be consumed in moderation.

If you ski, the Wanda Changbaishan complex, on the north slope, offers about thirty runs fed by abundant natural snow. The season runs from November to March. Day pass around 400 yuan, complete rental available on-site. It's now China's most modern ski resort, quieter and less crowded than Yabuli.

For non-skiers, head out by snowmobile through the boreal forest around Erdaobaihe. Several operators offer one-hour outings with a guide. You traverse taigas, sometimes crossing deer and wild boar tracks. Expect 300 to 500 yuan per session.

Take a break in a traditional Manchu guesthouse in Songjianghe or Erdaobaihe. These wooden houses have kang (bed-stoves heated by the kitchen hearth) where families have slept for centuries in the northeast. They serve suancai (fermented cabbage) and pao mo (pork stew). A night here costs around 250 yuan, and it's probably the most authentic experience of the trip.

For the brave, some local agencies organize the "Yalu River Winter Drifting" in winter: descent by inflatable boat on alternating frozen and open sections. Spectacular but reserved for thrill-seekers. Allow 2 hours and about 300 yuan.

days_plan.itin_title: Winter Manchuria - snow, sacred volcano and North Korean border
Winter Manchuria - snow, sacred volcano and North Korean border
Winter Manchuria - snow, sacred volcano and North Korean border

visits.text: Juzizhou Island stretches 5 km down the middle of the Xiang River, and it was here that Mao wrote his 1925 poem *Changsha*. A colossal head of young Mao was planted there in 2009, something to see or skip depending on your appetite for state mythology. Go in late afternoon, sun low over the water, sparse crowds. Allow 1h30 including the walk from the north entrance.

The Taiping Quarter is unabashedly kitsch: a pedestrian street rebuilt from scratch, red lanterns, frying steam clouds. It's also your best base for first contact with *xiang* cuisine, the spiciest of China's eight great cuisines. Ask for 微辣 (*wēi là*, mildly spicy) if you want to survive. Try the red-braised pork, Mao's favorite dish.

A 2,200-year-old mummy with joints still flexible: that's Lady Dai, pulled from the Mawangdui tomb sealed in 168 BC and excavated in 1972. The centerpiece of the Hunan Museum, free with WeChat reservation, closed Mondays. Add the embroidered silks, silk manuscripts, black lacquerware from the same tomb. Allow a full half-day, no less.

Pozi Street is a surviving market alley in the heart of old Changsha, miraculously spared by the city's massive construction campaigns. Narrow sidewalks, doorstep restaurants, baozi steam in the morning. Good photo of the calligraphic signboards. Allow 1h for a leisurely crossing, early morning ideal before the lunch crowd.

Lines of fifty people in front of a Cha Yan Yue Se counter? This is Changsha, capital of modern Chinese *milk tea* and home to Hunan TV, the country's most-watched channel. Continental pop culture is manufactured here. Spot the young crowd in streetwear on Huangxing Road. A cup costs 15-25 yuan, 30-min wait, worth trying once for the local experience.

activities.text: Dine early at a local *xiang cai* brasserie in the Taiping Quarter. On the menu: fried fermented tofu (stinky tofu), red-braised pork, spicy green beans. If the heat scares you, say 不要辣 (*bù yào là*, no spice). Jet lag will catch up around 9 p.m., don't linger after the meal. Rest is mandatory, two weeks of walking begin tomorrow.

Book the Hunan Museum on WeChat 48h in advance (free but daily quota). Present your passport at the entrance. English audio guide available, 20 yuan. The Mawangdui section is in the basement - crowds concentrate there, rush in at 9 a.m. opening. Natural exit toward Lantern Festival Park.

If the political angle interests you, allow a half-day for Mao's Memorial in Shaoshan, 1h30 drive from Changsha. Birth house, lotus pond, museum. Very ideologically charged, instructive on the construction of the cult. Otherwise, stay in town and walk Hexi toward the river at dusk, view of illuminated Juzizhou. For Shaoshan, full-day taxi 600 yuan.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi
Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi

visits.text: Be on-site at 5 p.m. to watch the dismantling of the 18 boats composing Guangji Bridge's central section—it's the spectacle. Built from 1171 onward under the Southern Song, this bridge (also called Xiangzi) has spanned the Han River for 850 years. It's one of China's Four Great Ancient Bridges, considered one of the world's first bascule bridges. Its 24 stone piers once supported as many pavilion-markets. Nighttime illumination at 8 p.m.

Sit for ten minutes in the courtyard of Kaiyuan Temple, founded in 738 under Tang Emperor Xuanzong—it's one of southern China's largest Buddhist monasteries and its atmosphere remains genuinely religious: monks in saffron robes, worshippers in meditation. Its wooden halls and ancient statues distinguish it from typical museum-temples. Free entry, an hour suffices, but the silence is worth the detour.

Walk up Paifang Street, the commemorative archway street aligning 23 Ming and Qing portals over 1.9 km. Each archway honors a local dignitary, an upright official, or a virtuous widow. Destroyed during the Cultural Revolution, they were rebuilt between 2006 and 2009 according to original plans. The street is lined with calligraphy shops, Chaozhou embroidery, and snack restaurants—it's the old town's backbone.

activities.text: Teochew (Chaozhou) cuisine is one of China's eight great regional cuisines and the south's most refined. Must-tries: braised goose in soy sauce, hand-beaten beef balls pounded for hours (texture bounces like rubber), rice porridge with seafood, and ceremonial Kung Fu tea served in tiny cups. Pat Gua Street is the snack epicenter. Expect 60-100 yuan per person.

Teochew opera, 580 years old, is one of China's oldest opera styles still alive. Elaborate makeup, hand-embroidered costumes, nasal singing over zither and percussion—it's demanding for European ears, but unforgettable. Check for performances at Paifang Street or the municipal theater, often Friday and Saturday evenings. Modest entry, 30-80 yuan.

days_plan.itin_title: Subtropical Southern China in 12 Days: Canton, Hakka Diaspora & Hong Kong Peninsula

places.name: Chengdu

visits.text: The Chengdu Research Base of Giant Panda Breeding, founded in 1987 from six rescued, sick and starving pandas, now spans 3 km² of forested hills and bamboo groves just 10 km from downtown. It's home to the world's largest captive giant panda population—over 200 individuals living in enclosures that recreate their natural habitat. Arrive early: pandas are most active during morning feeding and nap through the afternoon. The nursery lets you watch newborns in incubators (best in autumn-winter). The Giant Panda Museum, opened in 1993, displays more than 800 documents, fossils and models—it's the world's only museum entirely dedicated to this species.

Wuhou Temple (Wǔhóu Cí), originally built in 223 CE, is the only temple in China that jointly honors an emperor and his prime minister: Liu Bei, founder of the Shu Kingdom, and Zhuge Liang, the legendary strategist of the Three Kingdoms period (220-280). The current complex, rebuilt in 1672 under Emperor Kangxi, covers 37,000 m². Liu Bei Hall houses his 3-meter gilt statue flanked by his generals Guan Yu and Zhang Fei. The Stele of Three Perfections, erected in 809, stands 3.67 m tall—it combines text by Minister Pei Du, calligraphy by Liu Gongquan, and a eulogy to Zhuge Liang. Behind the temple, the Huiling Mausoleum (Liu Bei's tomb) has remained intact for over 1,700 years, never violated or excavated.

Jinli Ancient Street, adjacent to Wuhou Temple, recreates Qing Dynasty commercial architecture across roughly 550 meters. Its wooden houses with gray tiles and red eaves shelter shops selling Sichuan handicrafts (Shu embroidery, shadow puppets, Sichuan opera masks) and street stalls serving local classics: dan dan noodles (sesame noodles with chili oil), mapo tofu, and mala skewers. After dark, red lanterns light up and the atmosphere transforms—this is when to try san da pao, glutinous rice balls launched against a metal plate that bounce into soybean flour.

The Kuanzhai Alleys (Kuānzhǎi Xiàngzi), right in downtown Chengdu, are the last remnants of the Manchu city of Shaocheng, built in 1718 under Emperor Kangxi to house soldiers of the Eight Banners after the campaign against the Dzungars in Tibet. Of the original 42 lanes, only three survive: Wide Lane (Kuan, 7-8 m wide, reserved for officers who traveled by carriage), Narrow Lane (Zhai, 5 m, a Sino-Western mix tied to late-Qing foreign residents), and Well Lane (Jing, named after a well dug under Kangxi). The 45 interior courtyards (sìhéyuàn) were restored between 2004 and 2008. Here you sip covered-bowl tea (gàiwǎn chá) from bamboo chairs while watching Sichuan opera face-changing performances (biànliǎn).

People's Park (Rénmín Gōngyuán), a few minutes from Kuanzhai Alleys, is the heart of everyday Chengdu life. Under bamboo and ginkgo trees, retirees practice tai chi, play cards and mahjong, dance in couples, or fly kites. Open-air teahouses set up tables along the artificial lake—order jasmine tea and watch the ballet of hot-water pourers with their long-spouted kettles. On Sundays, an improvised "marriage corner" sees parents display profiles of their single children on placards.

Qingyang Palace (Qīngyáng Gōng), 1.2 km from People's Park, is one of Sichuan's oldest and most important Taoist temples. Founded during the Tang Dynasty, it takes its name from two bronze goats (qīngyáng) placed before the main hall—tradition says touching them brings good luck. The main building, the Hall of Three Purities (Sānqīng Diàn), is a nail-free wooden structure supported by massive columns.

The Leshan Giant Buddha (Lèshān Dàfó), 126 km south of Chengdu, is the world's tallest stone Buddha statue: 71 meters high, carved into the red sandstone cliff of Mount Lingyun at the confluence of the Min, Dadu, and Qingyi rivers. Construction began in 713 during the Tang Dynasty, initiated by monk Haitong who wanted to calm the dangerous waters of the confluence. When funds were threatened by corrupt officials, Haitong gouged out his own eyes to prove his sincerity—the extortionists fled. The monk died before completion; his disciples continued the work for 90 years until 803. Rock debris thrown into the river during construction actually altered the currents, making navigation safer. The head measures 14.7 m high, the ears 7 m, the fingers 8.3 m, and the feet can accommodate a hundred people. The hair consists of 1,021 spiral buns, each individually set into the rock. Descend via the Nine Bends Plank Road (Jiǔqū Zhàndào), a 278-step path carved into the cliff that leads from the top of the head to the feet of the colossus. UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1996.

Mount Qingcheng (Qīngchéng Shān), 68 km west of Chengdu, is one of the birthplaces of Taoism in China. This is where Zhang Daoling supposedly founded the Taoist School of Five Pecks of Rice in the 2nd century CE. The main path climbs through dense forest of cedars and bamboo, connecting about ten Taoist temples and pavilions up to the 1,260-meter summit. A cable car can shorten the ascent. Jianfu Temple at the mountain's base dates to the Tang Dynasty (built in 730). The site is a UNESCO World Heritage Site along with the Dujiangyan Irrigation System.

The Dujiangyan Irrigation System (Dūjiāngyàn), 3 km from the town of the same name, is the world's oldest irrigation system still in operation. Built in 256 BCE by Governor Li Bing and his son under the State of Qin, it diverts the waters of the Min River without a dam, through three complementary structures: a levee (Yuzui) that divides the current, a diversion channel (Baopingkou) tunneled through the mountain, and a spillway (Feishayan) that regulates floods. This system still irrigates over 5,300 km² of agricultural plains today and enabled Sichuan to become China's "rice basket." UNESCO listed in 2000.

activities.text: Sichuan cooking class in a downtown workshop. Learn to balance Sichuan spices—numbing Sichuan pepper (huājiāo) that tingles the tongue, dried chili, and fermented broad bean paste (dòubàn jiàng) from Pixian. Dishes taught vary: kung pao chicken, mapo tofu, or steamed dumplings. Allow 2 to 3 hours, tasting included.

Hike Mount Qingcheng via the main trail (ascent about 2h, descent 1h30). The path crosses suspension bridges, tunnels carved into rock, and clearings where Taoist monks sell green tea grown on the slopes. Option to shorten via cable car and ferry across Yuecheng Lake.

map_keywords: Chengdu Research Base of Giant Panda Breeding

visits.text: Hongyadong (Hóngyádòng), clinging to the cliff overlooking the Jialing River, is an 11-story, 75-meter-high complex built in the style of traditional stilt houses (diàojiǎolóu) of Bayu culture. The site dates back over 2,300 years—it was originally a fortified gate and major commercial wharf on the river. The current complex, completed in 2006, reproduces this characteristic wooden stilt architecture of southwestern China, adapted to mountainous terrain and floods. Peculiarity: the 1st floor and the 11th floor each open onto different streets at different elevations. In the evening, red lanterns illuminate the structure and its reflection in the river—an image often compared to the spirit world in the film Spirited Away.

Ciqikou (Cíqìkǒu), "the porcelain port," is a town over 1,000 years old on the bank of the Jialing River in Shapingba District. During the Ming and Qing dynasties, it was a center of porcelain production and trade. Its cobbled lanes bordered by two-story wooden houses preserve the atmosphere of an old riverside town. You'll find hand-pulled caramel candy workshops (máhuā), traditional teahouses, and handicraft shops. On weekends, artists play traditional Sichuan music in interior courtyards.

Liziba (Lǐzǐbà) monorail station became famous for a simple reason: Line 2 of the monorail passes through a 19-story residential building between the 6th and 8th floors. This isn't an accident—the building was designed simultaneously with the line, opened in 2005, to adapt to Chongqing's extreme topography, a city built on steep hills between two rivers. The monorail enters and exits the building every few minutes with surprisingly little noise thanks to soundproofing systems.

The Yangtze River Cableway (Chángjiāng Suǒdào), opened in 1987, is the only urban cableway still in service crossing the Yangtze. The cabin soars over the river for about 1,166 meters, linking Yuzhong District to the south bank (Nan'an). The crossing takes 4 minutes and offers a bird's-eye view of the river, bridges, and Chongqing's skyline. It's a relic from the era when Chongqing, "the mountain city," used cableways as daily public transport before the metro was built.

Chaotianmen Dock (Cháotiānmén), at the tip of the Yuzhong Peninsula, marks the exact confluence of the Yangtze and Jialing rivers. This is the wharf where Three Gorges cruises depart. The square offers a panoramic view of the confluence where the Jialing's green waters meet the Yangtze's brown waters—the demarcation line is visible in clear weather. At night, illuminated boats and Jiangbei's skyscrapers compose Chongqing's nocturnal panorama.

activities.text: Chongqing hot pot (huǒguō) tasting, the city's absolute specialty. The bright red broth, saturated with chili, Sichuan pepper, and beef tallow, boils in a pot at the center of the table. Each diner plunges thin meats, tripe, vegetables, and tofu into it. The local version is reputed spicier and fattier than Chengdu's. In the Jiefangbei or Nanbin Lu districts, specialized restaurants serve until late into the night.

Late afternoon or evening boarding at Chaotianmen wharf for the Three Gorges cruise toward Yichang (4 days / 3 nights downstream). The ship typically casts off around 9 PM. From the upper deck, Chongqing's nighttime skyline scrolls past as the vessel enters the Yangtze.

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest China: Sichuan, Chongqing & the Yangtze Gorges
Southwest China: Sichuan, Chongqing & the Yangtze Gorges

visits.text: Cizhong's Catholic church is an improbable historical object: built in 1867 by the Paris Foreign Missions, burned down in 1905 during the Tibetan Uprising (two French missionaries killed), then rebuilt in 1909 in the current village. Architecture half European, half Chinese—push open the door, the nave is Romanesque, the bell tower capped with a Chinese pavilion. Designated a historical monument in 1987. Sunday Mass in Tibetan still draws around a hundred faithful.

Circle the church: surrounding it, you'll find vineyards planted by the missionaries in 1867. Grape variety: "Rose Honey," introduced from France via Burma, extinct in Europe after the phylloxera epidemic of the late 19th century. Cizhong is now the only place on earth where this variety is still cultivated. Two families produce wine: Wu Hongxing's (next to the church) and the church authority itself.

Stroll the village lanes (roughly 200 families, 60% Catholic Tibetan/Naxi/Lisu, 40% Buddhist). The two communities live in harmony: Christmas and Tibetan New Year are celebrated together. Spot crosses on lintels beside prayer flags—you won't see that anywhere else in China.

activities.text: Knock on Wu Hongxing's door, next to the church, for a tasting. Two cuvées await: young Rose Honey (light, fruity) and late-harvest Rose Honey (syrupy, bouquet of rose and honey—hence the name). Budget 50-100 yuan for tasting, option to buy a bottle to take home.

If you land on a Saturday evening, attend Mass in Tibetan at 7 PM. Father Yao, a Han Chinese, has said Mass in Tibetan since 2008: liturgical chants in Tibetan minor mode, women in traditional chuba, candles in the nave. One hour, stay discreet, keep a scarf to cover your head (for women).

days_plan.itin_title: Three Parallel Rivers & Eastern Tibet - 14-Day Yunnan to Kham Extension

visits.text: After a pass at 4,500m, you plunge toward Danba—a 1,800m descent into the Dadu River basin. The town, stretched along the river at 1,800m, is your gateway to the Jiarong villages. Drop your bags, grab a coffee, climb to the Suopo viewpoint across the valley. The density of stone towers is breathtaking: over 80 towers in Suopo alone, the tallest reaching 67m.

Climb next to Jiaju village, terraced up the slope—white and red houses with wooden roofs, barley terraces, prayer flags. National Geographic named it "China's most beautiful village" in 2005. Take the stone paths, ask permission before entering courtyards, buy wild honey from residents. Budget 2-3 hours.

Head to Zhonglu, quieter than Jiaju, where you'll discover 81 watchtowers and 149 Tibetan families scattered across 5 km². Stay overnight if you can: several tower-houses rent rooms to travelers (200-300 yuan per night, meals included). Dawn here is magical—mist rises from the Dadu, towers emerge one by one.

Dive into Suopo, which holds the world's densest tower forest—175 towers in 13 hamlets. You'll find rare 13-angled structures, unique in the world. Built by the Jiarong during the ancient Kingdom of Women, they served for defense, storing wealth, sometimes also for worship. Many lean dangerously, like a local Leaning Tower of Pisa.

activities.text: Climb inside a real Jiarong tower. You'll ascend interior ladders across 4 or 5 levels to the top at 30-40m—several families in Suopo rent access to their tower for 50 yuan. Built between 800 and 1,200 years ago from stone and dried mud, without any modern pillars—they've survived multiple earthquakes. Plunging view over the Dadu.

If you arrive in October, Danba celebrates the Jiarong Harvest Festival. Women bring out traditional costumes—embroidered robes, silver belts, headdresses adorned with coral. Songs, dances, horse races in the meadows. The villages of Jiaju and Zhonglu are the most active. Ask your guesthouse for exact dates—they shift according to the Tibetan lunar calendar.

Try the hike toward sacred Mount Moerduo (5,105m), the Jiarong's guardian mountain. You can do the full circuit in 3 days, or a half-day loop from Geshizha—count on 4-5 hours, 800m elevation gain. You'll cross paths with pilgrims on koras (circumambulation) and cairns with prayer flags. Stay on the trails—this is sacred ground.

Evening embroidery workshop with a local family. Danba embroidery is inscribed on China's intangible heritage list, and several village women offer introductory workshops—one hour for 100 yuan, you leave with a small embroidered motif on fabric. Floral motifs symbolize Gasang, sacred flowers of Jiarong culture.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Western Sichuan - Kham, Aba, and Forgotten Valleys in 17 days
Deep Western Sichuan - Kham, Aba, and Forgotten Valleys in 17 days

places.name: Dandong

visits.text: The Yalu River Broken Bridge (Yalu Jiang Duan Qiao) is Dandong's star attraction. Built by the Japanese in 1911 to connect occupied Korea to the Eurasian rail network, it was bombed by over 100 American B-29s on November 8, 1950 during the Korean War. Today, only four spans remain on the Chinese side, riddled with shrapnel holes. You walk to the end, and there, the bridge stops abruptly above the river. Across: Sinuiju, North Korea. Entry 30 yuan.

At 25 km northeast, the Hushan (Tiger Mountain) Great Wall marks the known eastern terminus of the wall. Rebuilt in the 1990s on Ming foundations, it climbs a steep ridge. From the summit, you see North Korean villages across an arm of the Yalu just a few meters wide - the spot is called "Yibu Kua," "one step to cross." In winter, the walk is slippery but uncrowded.

The Memorial Hall of the War to Resist US Aggression and Aid Korea is worth a detour, even if the historical interpretation is very slanted. China recalls that approximately 197,000 Chinese "volunteers" died in the conflict, including Mao Anying, Mao Zedong's eldest son. Exhibits show weapons, uniforms and period photographs. Plan at least 1.5 hours.

activities.text: Board a tourist boat at the foot of the Broken Bridge to cruise along the North Korean shore at close range. Guides point out official buildings, the idle Ferris wheel, sometimes a few soldiers. Drones banned, binoculars tolerated. The cruise lasts about 30 minutes and costs 40 yuan. It's probably the shortest trip abroad you'll ever take - without setting foot on land.

In the evening, dine on the Yalu promenade at a North Korean restaurant staffed by personnel sent from Pyongyang (several exist in Dandong). The waitresses sing and dance around 8 PM. The menu features barbecue, kimchi and cold soup. It's a strange and unique experience: the smiles are genuine, the context much less so.

days_plan.itin_title: Winter Manchuria - snow, sacred volcano and North Korean border

map_keywords: Memorial Hall War Resist US Aggression Dandong

visits.text: Daocheng (3,750m) is your gateway to Yading. The town itself remains modest—a few streets, a monastery, hotels springing up fast. Stock up on water, chocolate, and altitude pills (ibuprofen + Diamox). You'll stay tonight at Riwa (Shangri-La in Chinese), 30 km further, at the reserve entrance.

Enter Yading Nature Reserve and discover 1,457 km² protecting three sacred peaks: Chenrezig (6,032m, Buddha of compassion), Jambeyang (5,958m, wisdom), Chanadorje (5,958m, power). For Tibetans, circling them is a kora ensuring liberation. At 2,900m, Riwa, the departure base, is lower—good for acclimatizing.

Take the mandatory shuttle (120 yuan round-trip) to Chonggu Si, a 13th-century monastery at the foot of Chenrezig at 3,900m. From there, follow a marked trail to Luorong Plateau (4,150m, 6 km, 2h) facing all three summits. It's one of China's most powerful panoramas. Drink, walk slowly, stay at least an hour to absorb it.

Rise early to see first light hit Chenrezig. You'll watch the summit turn gold for five minutes, then fade back to white. Photographers: position at Chonggu Si by 6:45am in September. Pilgrims arrive on koras around the monastery at the same hour—worth the trip alone.

Hike to Pearl Lake (Zhenzhu Hai), at 4,080m, more accessible than Milk Lake. You follow a 1.5 km boardwalk from Chonggu Si. The water is a raw, almost unreal green, reflecting Chenrezig on clear days. Sit down, open a thermos of tea, watch high-altitude ravens circling. This is where you understand why Joseph Rock, the Austrian botanist, spoke of "Shangri-La" in 1928.

Step through the gate of Chonggu Si, founded in the 13th century—you enter the park's only religious structure. Tibetan Kagyupa, modest, magnificent in its setting. Three monks still reside here. Light a butter lamp, lay a kata, circle the temple clockwise. The white, yellow, blue, red, and green prayer flags represent the five elements.

activities.text: If you're well acclimatized and skies are clear, attempt Five-Color Lake (Wuse Hai, 4,600m) and Pearl Lake (Zhenzhu Hai, 4,500m). From Luorong, follow a boardwalk trail for 5 km—it climbs steeply, count on 3-4 hours one-way. These sacred lakes nestle in glacial cirques, icy turquoise waters. Many pilgrims circle them on koras.

For the very fit: attempt the short kora around Chenrezig. You'll cover 14 km, 600m elevation gain, 5-6 hours. You follow Tibetan pilgrims circling while reciting mantras, prostrating every three steps for the most devout. Trail not marked for foreigners—local guide recommended (300-400 yuan/day).

Evening in Riwa: several guesthouses offer themed Tibetan dinners with Khampa song and dance performances. It's touristy, yes, but the artists are genuinely from the region and the costumes traditional. Budget 100-150 yuan for dinner-show. Otherwise, a simple bowl of momos and Tibetan beer at a guesthouse works just as well.

days_plan.itin_title: From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days
Deep Western Sichuan - Kham, Aba, and Forgotten Valleys in 17 days
Deep Western Sichuan - Kham, Aba, and Forgotten Valleys in 17 days

visits.text: High-speed train Beijing→Datong (~2h). The Yungang Grottoes, 16 km from Datong: 252 caves and 51,000 Buddhist statues carved into sandstone between 460 and 525 under the Northern Wei. The complex stretches 1 km along the cliff face of Mount Wuzhou. The five Tan Yao caves (nos. 16-20) each shelter a colossal Buddha representing a Tuoba emperor—an act of repentance following anti-Buddhist persecutions. Cave no. 20: 13.7 m Buddha in meditation. Cave no. 5: largest statue at the site (17 m). The style blends Persian, Greco-Buddhist Gandharan, Indian and Chinese influences. UNESCO since 2001.
Caves nos. 5 and 6, the best preserved. Cave no. 6 features a central pagoda adorned with scenes from the life of Buddha. After the capital relocated to Luoyang in 494, work gradually ceased.

The Hanging Monastery Xuankong, 65 km from Datong, built in 491 under the Northern Wei. Plastered 50 m high on the Jinlong canyon cliff face, opposite Mount Hengshan (sacred Taoist mountain). The structure is held by horizontal beams driven into the rock—the red wooden pillars are purely aesthetic. Forty pavilions linked by vertiginous walkways, 80 statues. The only monastery in China honoring Buddhism, Taoism and Confucianism—Sakyamuni, Lao Tzu and Confucius side by side.

The Nine Dragons Screen of Datong (1392), in front of the palace of Emperor Ming Hongwu's thirteenth son. Measuring 45.5 m long and 8 m high, covered in glazed tiles. The oldest and largest of China's three existing Nine Dragons screens.

activities.text: Ascent into the monastery via stone stairway carved into the cliff, crossing the Changxian bridge between the north and south sections—plunging view over the canyon.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie
Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie

visits.text: Red Yao never cut their hair: some women have 2 meters of it, rolled into buns under red headdress. Dazhai, Yao village at heart of Jinkeng zone, is more rural than Ping'an, fewer shops, more walking. Optional cable car to platform no.3, otherwise hike Dazhai-no.2-no.1 about 4h. Entry included in Longji ticket.

Tiantouzhai stretches on the ridge in stone stairways, Yao hamlet perched above the terraces. Panoramic platforms over spiral rice paddies. Quieter, less touristy than lower Dazhai. Allow 1h going from Dazhai, steep but well-marked trail. Clear view on clear days, frequent mist after 3 p.m. in wet season.

Golden Buddha Summit offers Jinkeng zone's highest platform, platform no.3. Accessible by cable car (135 yuan round trip) or 2h hike. 360° view over terraces in all directions, open summit when sky permits. Covered pavilion if mist rises. Allow 1h on site.

activities.text: If your legs hold, cross via the ridge between Ping'an and Dazhai: 5-6h trek, one of southern China's great trails. Paved trail, sustained elevation gain, continuous view over rice paddies on both sides. Water, snacks, 8 a.m. start. Driver-guide picks up at Dazhai late afternoon, luggage transferred by car during walk.

Attend Yao hair washing ceremony at Huangluo village, Guinness-listed as world's longest hair village. Folklorized for tourists but authentic costumes and techniques. Allow 1h. Entry 80-100 yuan. Afternoon show only, verify times by season. Photos allowed for tip.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi

visits.text: Dive into Dehang valley, a Miao village far less touristy than Xijiang, still populated by farmers in traditional costume. You'll discover Dehang Grand Canyon plunging into a karst valley with 300 m cliffs. Liusha Waterfall - "flowing sand" - falls in a fine veil over 216 m. Paved trails in good condition, few railings, caution with children. Entry 80 yuan.

Stop in the village center, which preserves traditional Miao organization: communal house at center, concentric alleys, covered wooden bridges. You'll see the drum tower used for festivals: Miao women still play there during the Drum Festival, celebrated in the fourth lunar month (May). If your visit coincides, it's the experience of a lifetime.

If you don't fear heights, cross the First Step Bridge (Yibu) - a single 15 m long rock laid across a narrow gap 200 m above ground, originally without railings. It was an evacuation route for inhabitants during attacks. Now secured by metal walkways, but the effect remains striking.

activities.text: If you arrive on market day (mornings, twice monthly according to lunar calendar), head to the central square. Miao women in embroidered costumes, medicinal plant sellers, fabric stalls, live black pigs. Raw and magnificent atmosphere, 100% local. Ask your host for exact dates.

Dine at a local home - several Miao families rent rooms and serve traditional meals: river fish in black soy sauce, sticky rice steamed in bamboo leaves, warm rice wine. Budget 80 yuan for dinner. Book through your inn. HSR train next morning from Jishou (45 minutes by taxi) to Changsha for return flight.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Hubei & Hunan - 14 days through Wudang, Shennongjia and Miao valleys

visits.text: The Shaolin Temple was founded in 495 under Emperor Xiaowen of the Northern Wei to welcome the Indian monk Batuo. In 527, Bodhidharma arrived, reputedly meditating nine years facing a cliff in a cave on Mount Song and founding Chan (Zen) Buddhism along with the foundations of Shaolin martial arts. Inscribed as a UNESCO site in 2010, the complex covers 57,600 m² but remains highly touristic: arrive at opening (8 am) to visit before the tour buses.

300 meters west of the main temple, the Pagoda Forest (Talin) gathers over 240 funerary stupas of eminent monks, dating from the 8th century (Tang) to the 19th (Qing). It's China's largest pagoda cemetery, peaceful and photogenic. Each stupa tells the life of the interred monk: the number of tiers reflects his rank, calligraphy adorns the base.

High up at 1,360 meters elevation, the Damo (Bodhidharma) Cave must be earned: 30 minutes on foot or 5 minutes by cable car from Shifang Temple. The cave is small, empty, but this is where it all began, according to tradition. The view over the Shaoshi Valley is splendid on clear days.

Songyang Academy, at the southern foot of Mount Song, is one of the four great Confucian academies of the Song dynasty. Founded in 484 under the Northern Wei, it hosted the brothers Cheng Hao and Cheng Yi, founders of Neo-Confucianism. But its true stars are two giant cypress trees: according to tradition, they were named "Great Generals" by Emperor Han Wudi himself over 2,000 years ago—among the oldest trees in China.

Zhongyue Temple, 4 km east of Dengfeng, is the largest and best-preserved of the five imperial temples dedicated to sacred mountains. Rebuilt mainly under the Qing, it retains its original Han layout with a succession of 11 courtyards spanning nearly a kilometer. It's a birthplace of Chinese Taoism, still vibrant: the monks practice here.

The Gaocheng Astronomical Observatory, 15 km southeast of Dengfeng, is China's oldest preserved observatory. Built in 1276 under the Yuan by astronomer Guo Shoujing on Kublai Khan's orders, it was used to measure the shadow of the gnomon: standing 12.62 meters tall, it was one of 27 observatories in the imperial network. The Shoushi calendar computed here remained in use until the end of the Ming.

On the way, make a quick stop at Songyue Pagoda (520), the oldest brick pagoda in China. Twelve sides, fifteen tiers, 40 meters tall, in the middle of the forest. It's stood for 1,500 years. Many miss it because it's off the standard circuits.

activities.text: Watch a kung fu demonstration at the Shaolin Wushu Training Center, right at the complex entrance. Three performances daily (10 am, 11:30 am, 2 pm), 30 minutes each. The techniques are choreographed for tourists, but the young monk-students (ages 8–15) are genuinely talented. Avoid weekends and Chinese holidays if you want to see anything.

For those who want to go deeper, several schools in the Dengfeng valley (Tagou, Shaolin Epo) offer 1- to 3-hour kung fu introductory classes with a senior monk, for around 200–400 yuan. It's touristic but sincere, and far more instructive than the performances.

If you're up for a hike, climb Junji Peak on Mount Song (1,491 m), starting from Zhongyue Temple on foot. Count on 4 to 5 hours round-trip, marked trail, quiet on weekdays. It's one of the five sacred Taoist mountains, and the least known: you'll cross paths with Chinese pilgrims rather than foreign tourists.

days_plan.itin_title: Spiritual Henan & Shaanxi – Tracing the Cradle of Chinese Civilization
Spiritual Henan & Shaanxi – Tracing the Cradle of Chinese Civilization

visits.text: Spend a night in Deqin before crossing the TAR border tomorrow. The town, at 3,400 m, isn't spectacular in itself, but its position makes it the logical stop between Cizhong and Yanjing: the road ahead, along the Mekong, offers no decent lodging before Yanjing. Use the time to fuel up, withdraw cash (few ATMs beyond here accept foreign cards), and double-check your Tibet permit with your driver one last time.

Make a stop at Yongzhi village, 30 km north of Deqin, where a trail descends to the bridge over the Mekong. It's one of the last panoramic views of the valley before entering the closed Tibetan zone. Budget 30 minutes for the descent and climb back up—photos guaranteed.

days_plan.itin_title: Three Parallel Rivers & Eastern Tibet - 14-Day Yunnan to Kham Extension

visits.text: Detian stretches 200 m wide and 70 m high, transborder waterfall shared with Vietnam (Vietnamese side: Ban Gioc). Brown waters in rainy season, clear in winter. Very little known to Westerners, real surprise. Approached from viewpoint then lower basin, two complementary angles. Allow 2h on site. Entry 80 yuan.

Imposing transborder waterfall, Detian lines up 200 m width over 70 m height. Flow varies with rains, spectacular in July-August, more modest in winter, but karst peak framing holds all year. View from Chinese bank wider than Vietnamese side.

Marker 53 marks China-Vietnam border at waterfall's foot. You can approach and place one foot on each side - but don't cross the marker, sensitive border. Discreet but real surveillance. No games, no humorous attempts. Vietnamese merchant next door will sell coffee to passersby of both nationalities, that's all.

activities.text: Take a bamboo raft right under the waterfall: embarkment at main viewpoint foot, 60 yuan per person, 20 min ride. Rafts pass just under border, plunging view over both countries. Stay seated, precarious balance. Life jacket provided. Avoid rainy days, browner water and unstable rafts.

If you arrive early, descend to lower basin first in early afternoon, frontal light on waterfall. Upper viewpoint late afternoon, soft backlight, rainbow possible. Lodging Detian Resort or Daxin town, equivalent distance. Zhuang restaurants at neighboring village, local cuisine 50-80 yuan per person.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi

visits.text: The Mogao Caves (Mògāo Kū), 25 km southeast of Dunhuang, constitute the world's largest, richest, and oldest Buddhist cave art complex. Carved starting in 366 AD—when the monk Le Zun had a vision of a thousand Buddhas bathed in golden light on the cliff face—the caves were expanded over ten centuries until the 14th century. 492 painted caves survive, containing 45,000 m² of murals and over 2,000 polychrome sculptures. Cave 96, the most spectacular, holds a 35.5-meter-tall Buddha. Cave 17, called the "Library Cave," discovered in 1900 by a Taoist monk, contained over 50,000 manuscripts, silk paintings, and textiles sealed for nearly 1,000 years—the greatest medieval East Asian document discovery. UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1987. Advance booking required.

The Singing Sand Dunes of Mingsha (Míngshā Shān), along the southern edge of the Dunhuang oasis, stretch 40 km long and rise over 250 meters high. Their name comes from the deep rumble the sand produces when it slides down slopes in dry weather—an acoustic phenomenon linked to the particular grain size of the sand particles. At the dunes' foot, Crescent Moon Lake (Yuèyá Quán) is a natural crescent-shaped spring 218 meters long and about 50 meters wide, fed by an underground water table that prevents sand from covering it. Historical texts mention this spring since the Han dynasty (2nd century BC).

Yumenguan Pass (Yùmén Guān), 90 km northwest of Dunhuang, was one of two gateways out of the Chinese empire toward the western routes. Its name, "Jade Gate Pass," comes from jade from Khotan that passed through this post. Only a square rammed-earth fort remains, 24 meters per side and 10 meters high, isolated in the Gobi Desert. Tang dynasty poets made it a symbol of frontier garrison solitude.

activities.text: Ride a camel across the Mingsha sand dunes as Silk Road merchants did for millennia. The singing sands produce an eerie humming sound when grains shift in the wind.
Camel ride around the Crescent Moon Lake oasis.
Free evening to watch sunset over the dunes.

Camel ride through the Mingsha dunes at sunset. Camel caravans follow a marked circuit from the dunes' base up toward the ridges. The descent can be done on sand sleds. In the evening, the dunes take on orange then purple hues.

days_plan.itin_title: The Silk Road Overland: Xi'an to Istanbul via Uzbekistan & Turkmenistan
China's Silk Road: Xi'an to Kashgar via the Hexi Corridor
China's Silk Road: Xi'an to Kashgar via the Hexi Corridor

visits.text: Plunge into Enshi Grand Canyon, which seriously rivals Zhangjiajie with fewer crowds. You'll cross the star section, "One Line of Sky" (Yixiantian), a 200 m deep vertical fissure only 2 m wide in places - you pass between two cliffs with just a strip of sky above. You'll also see the Stone Pillar, a 150 m monolith balanced on end. Plan a full day: 8am-5pm open, 200 yuan with cable car included.

Stop at Enshi Tujia Folk Village, which recreates at large scale the traditional habitat of diaojiaolou - wooden stilt houses built without a single metal nail. You'll watch weaving demonstrations, the Baishou dance ("hundred hands dance"), and taste Tujia cuisine. It's a bit staged but helps understand the architecture before visiting more remote real villages. Entry 95 yuan.

Push on to the old Yunyang salt wells, north of Enshi, which have exploited saline springs since the Han era that brought wealth to the region. You'll find a small local museum presenting traditional evaporation techniques. Rarely visited, but illuminates why this isolated region was strategic for 2,000 years.

activities.text: For thrill-seekers, Enshi's suspended glass bridge crosses the canyon at 718 m altitude - one of the world's highest. 488 m long, entirely transparent floor. Terrifying and magnificent. Included in park ticket. Skip if you fear heights.

In the evening, catch a Tujia performance at a family inn - polyphonic songs, white tiger dances (Tujia totem animal), ancestral cooking demonstration. More authentic than the big tourist spectacles. Budget 80 yuan, 90 minutes, at several downtown guesthouses.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Hubei & Hunan - 14 days through Wudang, Shennongjia and Miao valleys

visits.text: Two twin rocky pinnacles, each crowned by a temple, separated by a deep crevasse, linked by a narrow stone bridge: this is the Golden Summit of the Red Cloud (Hongyun Jinding) of Fanjingshan. Buddha Temple and Maitreya Temple seem to levitate when the sea of mist rises around 4 p.m. Guizhou's most widely distributed photo.

Fanjingshan peaks at 2,572 m, UNESCO-listed in 2018 for exceptional biodiversity: endemic Guizhou golden monkey, Abies fanjingshanensis fir, giant salamander. Sacred Buddhist sanctuary, one of China's five Maitreya mountains. Still little visited by Westerners. East entrance via Jiangkou, ticket 110 yuan plus cable car 160 yuan.

Ten meters high, a summit much wider than the base, balanced for millions of years: the Mushroom Rock (Mogu Shi) visibly defies gravity, improbable geological formation of Fanjingshan. 30 min walk from summit cable car, allow 1h round trip. Frequent mist, humid trail, caution. No railing, stay behind safety line.

activities.text: Allow a long stairway after the cable car to reach the Golden Summit stone bridge, several thousand steps. The ordeal is real, especially in humid heat. Departure at 7 a.m., return 2-3 p.m. on the sea of mist. Good shoes, water, snacks. Cable car closed in case of strong wind or snow (rare). Opening 07:00-15:00 last ascent.

If weather cuts bridge access at altitude, cable car remains operable to mid-height and lower botanical trail remains accessible. The trip's buffer day serves precisely for this case. Check weather the day before with local tourist office (limited English, ask hotel). Plan B: day in Tongren and its Tujia museum.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi

visits.text: Drop your bags at Feilai Si, perched at 3,470 m facing Mount Meili—one of Tibetan Buddhism's great pilgrimage sites. The temple was built in 1614 during the Ming dynasty, and legend says a meditating Buddha saw another Buddha fly from India and land here, hence the name (Flying Temple). It's tiny, but it's the antechamber to Kawagebo. Book a room facing the mountain—the view from bed is worth every yuan.

Look up at Mount Meili (Kawa Karpo in Tibetan), unfurling 13 peaks above 6,000 m. The principal summit, Kawagebo, tops out at 6,740 m—Yunnan's highest peak and one of the eight most sacred mountains in Tibetan Buddhism. In 1991, a Sino-Japanese expedition lost all 17 members in an avalanche: since 2001, Chinese law has banned climbing to respect the mountain's sacred status. No one has ever stood on the summit.

If clouds are blocking Feilai Si at sunrise, ask your driver to race 15 km south to the Wunongding viewing platform ("Foggy Peak"). The angle captures a wider panorama of all 13 peaks, and you'll see the 13 white Tibetan stupas aligned facing the massif. Far less crowded at dawn, and sometimes light breaks through here when it doesn't at Feilai Si.

Follow the road 26 km south to Mingyong village, gateway to China's lowest-altitude glacier. The glacier descends from Kawagebo to 2,600 m—one of the rare oceanic monsoon glaciers in the world. Stretching 11.7 km, it crosses five vegetation zones, from tundra to deciduous forest. The access trail starts from the village (8 km, 4-hour round trip) and reaches Taizi Temple at 2,720 m. Don't step onto the ice—it's unstable.

Stop repeatedly between Feilai Si and Mingyong to photograph the Lancang Jiang (Mekong) valley. The elevation drop between the road and the river reaches 4,700 m opposite Kawagebo—you're crossing one of the world's deepest gorges, part of the UNESCO "Three Parallel Rivers" site inscribed in 2003.

At the glacier's foot, 2,720 m elevation, you'll encounter Taizi Temple marking the end of the observation trail. A small Tibetan Buddhist temple, stationed here to bless the sacred glacier. Pilgrims burn juniper as an offering to Kawagebo—you can buy a bundle at the temple for 5 yuan and join the ritual.

Attack the Sacred Waterfall (Shen Pu, 3,700 m), easier than Glacier Lake: 12 km round trip, 600 m elevation gain, 4 to 6 hours. The trail departs Lower Yubeng, follows the river, runs through a valley strung with prayer flags. For Tibetan pilgrims, the water falling here is sacred—they circle three times beneath the cascade to purify themselves of sins. You can do the same, but the water's 4-5°C.

Exit via the Ninong trail, more scenic than the Xidang route you took in: 18 km descent, 5 to 6 hours, hugging the Mekong through an impressive canyon. Once in Ninong, catch a shuttle back to your 4x4 at Feilai Si (1 hour). Your legs will hurt tomorrow—guaranteed.

Between the waterfall and Lower Yubeng, you'll pass several small altars and chortens dotting the path. Pilgrims leave a stone, a flag, a ribbon—you can do the same if you wish, it's an accepted gesture.

activities.text: Turn in early: tomorrow means a 5:30 AM wake-up for "Golden Sunlight on the Sacred Mountain" (Ri Zhao Jin Shan in Chinese), the phenomenon where the first golden ray strikes Kawagebo's summit and descends across all 13 peaks in a matter of minutes. It lasts only 5 to 10 minutes. The mountain reveals itself rarely: 30-50% visibility in dry season, 10-20% in summer.

Pack down jacket, gloves, and beanie for dawn: at 3,470 m, it easily hits -5°C before sunrise, even in June. Several hotels rent oxygen bottles for 30 yuan—grab one if you're feeling headaches or nausea upon arrival.

Take your time on the Mingyong trail: light shifts every hour, and you might cross paths with Tibetan pilgrims making the kora (ritual circumambulation) around Kawagebo. The full pilgrimage lasts 14 days, 240 km, crossing several passes at 4,800 m.

Back in Feilai Si, grab butter tea at one of the many Tibetan guesthouses lining the main street. It's a chance to chat with yak drivers who haul supplies up to Yubeng—you're heading there tomorrow for the trek, so get their advice on trail conditions.
Evening in Feilai Si: hot shower (ultimate luxury after three days in guesthouses), hot meal. Hotels offer hot-stone massages for 100-150 yuan—do it, your calves will thank you.

days_plan.itin_title: Three Parallel Rivers & Eastern Tibet - 14-Day Yunnan to Kham Extension
Three Parallel Rivers & Eastern Tibet - 14-Day Yunnan to Kham Extension
Three Parallel Rivers & Eastern Tibet - 14-Day Yunnan to Kham Extension

visits.text: Very touristy by day, Fenghuang truly awakens in the evening, when buses leave and lanterns light up along the Tuojiang. Wooden stilt houses, stone bridges, paved alleys: Phoenix town remains one of Hunan's best-preserved ancient villages. Tujia-Miao Autonomous Prefecture of Xiangxi. Allow 1h for the first crossing of both banks.

Shen Congwen, one of 20th-century China's great writers, was born here.
