His house-museum is small, moving: photos, manuscripts, work table. *Border Town* (边城), his best-known novel, takes place between Fenghuang and the Tuojiang River. A short book to read before the trip. Allow 45 min on site. Entry 10 yuan.

East Gate, Qing bricks on red sandstone base, guards the northeast entrance to the old town. 18th-century watchtower, paid climb (10 yuan) for rooftop view. Allow 20 min. Nearby, the contemporary Tujia painter Huang Yongyu's workshop-museum, Fenghuang native and still living, opens when the artist isn't in residence.

activities.text: Wake before 8 a.m. to see women beating laundry on Tuojiang rocks, daily life scene before tour buses arrive. Walk from the north bank, cross stone bridges, take bamboo walkways. Morning light is best for photographing stilt houses. Allow 1h30, riverside coffee afterwards.

Xiangxi Miao still wear silver headdresses in surrounding villages. Half-day excursion to Miaowangzhai or Shanjiang (30-45 min drive), to see the crafts - silverwork, black-blue embroidery - outside the central tourist décor. Allow 250-350 yuan for half-day driver. Try the *sour fish soup* for lunch on site.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie
Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi

visits.text: Step into the Ip Man Museum, opened in 2002 within the ancestral temple complex. Foshan is the birthplace of Wing Chun, the martial art of Bruce Lee and his master Ip Man, and this small museum traces the grandmaster's life through his garments, wooden training weapons, and period photographs. If you've seen the Ip Man films, it's a pilgrimage; if not, the Wing Chun introduction is well worth the visit. Allow one hour.

Look up inside Foshan Ancestral Temple, founded in 1078 under the Northern Song. The polychrome Shiwan ceramic figurines adorning the roofs are among the province's finest. Dedicated to Beidi, Emperor of the North, the temple also houses the Wanfu Theater, Guangdong's oldest still-active stage. Lion dance demonstrations take place on weekends. An hour and a half suffices—don't miss the stele courtyard.

Stroll along Lingnan Tiandi, Foshan's renovated old quarter where traditional Cantonese arcade houses, interior courtyards, and the former Jian family mansion (circa 1860)—late-Qing tobacco magnates—still stand. Now blending cafés, design shops, and artisan workshops, the district strikes a coherent balance between heritage and contemporary life. Allow an hour for a late-afternoon wander.

activities.text: Immerse yourself in the Shiwan ceramic kilns, firing continuously for over 1,000 years. The Nanfeng Ancient Kiln, in continuous operation since the Ming dynasty (circa 1506), remains one of the world's oldest dragon kilns still in service. You'll see artisans throwing, modeling, and painting the polychrome figurines sold across southern China. Buy a figurine directly from the workshop—quality incomparable to airport souvenirs.

Reserve dinner at a traditional Cantonese restaurant in Foshan: roast char siu pork, fish soup with rice noodles, and the region's oyster fritters stand out for rusticity closer to the countryside than to Guangzhou's refinement. The area around the ancestral temple concentrates the best addresses—expect 70-100 yuan per person. Family atmosphere, few tourists, gentle prices.

days_plan.itin_title: Subtropical Southern China in 12 Days: Canton, Hakka Diaspora & Hong Kong Peninsula

visits.text: Reserve your day for Mount Taimushan in Fuding, which deserves its nickname 'Mount Tai in the Sea.' A 92 km² granite massif overlooking the East China Sea directly, considered one of the most beautiful sacred Taoist mountains. 'Tai Mu' means 'Great Mother,' referring to a local goddess. Over 360 rock formations have been named: Turtle Raising Its Head, Lying Tiger, Buddha Riding Lion.

Squeeze through Yixiantian, the 'One Line of Sky,' the massif's most famous passage. The fissure measures 60 meters long and only 30 cm wide at the narrowest point. Above, the sky appears as a thin blue line. For the claustrophobic, a bypass is possible. Sensations guaranteed.

Launch yourself on the Jiulilonghu trail (Nine Dragons Drinking)—one of the massif's most beautiful sections: caves, waterfalls, stone bridges, sea views. Allow 3-4 hours, 600-meter elevation gain. At the summit, Pinglian Temple offers hot tea to hikers.

activities.text: Taste Fuding white tea, one of Fujian's rarest specialties. The region produces 'Bai Hao Yin Zhen' (Silver Needle with White Down)—China's most prestigious white tea. Several producers offer tastings in villages around Taimushan: Diantou, Panxi. Fuding white tea is distinguished by its softness and remarkable aging. Count on 150-300 yuan for a guided tasting.

Relax in the evening at Taimushan Hot Springs. Several establishments offer open-air pools with views of the granite peaks. Water flows naturally at 65°C and is filtered to 38-42°C in the pools. Count on 150-250 yuan for a 2-hour session. Perfect after a day's hike.

days_plan.itin_title: Coastal Fujian - Beyond the Tulou, 11 Days on the Maritime Silk Road

visits.text: Walk through Furong on foot: it's China's only ancient village literally built above a 60 m high, 40 m wide waterfall. The You River crosses the old town before plunging into the void. The town was called Wangcun until 2007, when it was renamed after the 1986 film "Hibiscus Town" shot here. Over 2,000 years of history - founded in 202 BC under the Western Han. Take the trail passing behind the water curtain: unforgettable, but you'll get soaked.

Enter next the Tusi Palace, which testifies to the reign of hereditary Tujia chiefs under the Yuan, Ming and Qing dynasties. You'll tour a wooden stilt complex, fine carvings, a reconstructed audience hall. The Tusi ruled their fiefs in near-autonomy, in exchange for annual tribute to the emperor. The system lasted until the 18th century. Budget 1h on site.

Walk down Wuli cobbled street, over 2,300 years old, running 800 m between the diaojiaolou. Cobblestones polished by centuries, artisan workshops, rice tofu shops (the film's specialty). Taste mi doufu - white rice-based tofu, served in spicy sauce with black vinegar. A few yuan per bowl, everywhere on the street.

activities.text: Stay overnight: at night, the waterfall and diaojiaolou light up - an entirely free spectacle that day-trippers miss. Choose an inn with a terrace facing the waterfall. Rates between 200 and 500 yuan per night depending on view. Teahouse open late on the main street.

Early morning, descend the stone steps to the You River, below the waterfall. Board a short flat-bottom boat ride (50 yuan, 30 minutes) along the cliff base. Striking view of the village's stilts from below. Tujia fishermen still work there.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Hubei & Hunan - 14 days through Wudang, Shennongjia and Miao valleys
Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi

places.name: Fuzhou

visits.text: Welcome to Fuzhou, capital of Fujian, founded over 2,200 years ago. Sanfang Qixiang—'Three Lanes and Seven Alleys'—covers 38.35 hectares in the city's heart and preserves more than 200 Ming and Qing mandarin houses. The quarter dates to the 3rd century; its herringbone layout has remained intact since the Tang era. Nicknamed 'Imperial China's Beverly Hills,' it housed over 400 historical figures. Free entry to the lanes; admission charged for certain houses.

Enter Lin Zexu's residence at 127 Nanhou Street. This is the story of a man who changed China: an honest mandarin, Lin Zexu ordered the destruction of 1,188 tons of British opium at Humen in 1839—a gesture that triggered the First Opium War and the cession of Hong Kong. You'll see a full-scale model of the destruction, letters, and calligraphy. Closed Mondays.

Detour to Yan Fu's residence at 55 Yijin Lane. Yan Fu (1854-1921) translated Huxley's 'Evolution and Ethics' into classical Chinese—introducing Darwin—as well as works by Adam Smith, Spencer, and Mill, revolutionizing modern Chinese thought. The 600 m² house blends mandarin architecture with Western touches, typical of the late Qing. Original library, manuscripts, photographs.

To catch your breath, slip into Bing Xin's former residence at 23 Langguan Alley. A major 20th-century novelist and poet, she spent her childhood here. White walls, black tiles, tiny garden, frangipani courtyard. A preserved scholarly atmosphere. Ideal for a quiet break in the middle of the tourist quarter.

Start the day at Xichan Temple, founded under the Liang dynasty in 561 CE—one of Fuzhou's most active Buddhist temples. Its five main pavilions house a 1,500 kg bronze bell cast in 1611 and a banyan tree over 1,000 years old whose roots form a vegetal dome. Monks still sell Xichan tea here, cultivated on temple lands since the 17th century. 20 yuan admission.

Climb Mount Yushan, which dominates downtown at 58 meters altitude. Its Tianjun Temple and white Wuta Pagoda (941 CE) offer a panorama over Fuzhou's rooftops. At the summit, a jade Mazu statue recalls the city's maritime identity. It's also a promenade spot where locals practice tai chi in the morning.

Allow two hours for the Fujian Museum in West Lake Park. You'll see the province's archaeological artifacts: Song ceramics, scale models of junks, jade snuff bottles. Remarkable section on 'sea god worship' with ancient Mazu statues. Free admission; closed Mondays.

Continue to West Lake Park (Xihu Park), created in 282 CE—over 1,700 years ago. You cross lakes, arched bridges, red wooden pavilions, flower-filled kiosks—one of China's oldest urban gardens, a model for later imperial gardens. Rent a boat on the lake (30 yuan): it's a Sunday morning classic.

Return to Fuzhou to close the loop. Head to Fuzhou Arsenal Cultural Park in Mawei, which recounts the birth of modern China's navy. Founded in 1866 by Zuo Zongtang, the arsenal built China's first steam-powered warships. You'll see scale models, plans, period photos, and a cannon recovered from the bay. Moving for understanding the end of imperial China.

Push on to Luoxing Pagoda at the mouth of the Min River, a seamark since the 13th century. Built under the Song in 1271, it rises 31.5 meters and marks the port entrance. Marco Polo would have seen it from his ship. The surrounding park offers a tranquil walk with views of modern container ships—symbolic of Fuzhou's long sea connection.

If time permits, climb Mount Wushan, Yushan's twin in the old city. It houses the black Wuta Pagoda (941 CE, like Yushan's white one). According to tradition, the two pagodas are the city's twin 'sentinels.' Short but nice rooftop view. Often overlooked in tours.

activities.text: For dinner, head to Anjia Yan, an institution since 1876 specializing in yan, Fuzhou's signature dumpling. The distinctive feature: the dough is made from pounded pork meat, not flour. The dumpling is then stuffed with pork and served in broth. Count on 30-50 yuan for a large bowl. Combine with Fuzhou fish balls (yuwan), a local specialty.

To end the evening, aim for the Water Pavilion Theatre (Shuixie Tai) on Yijin Lane. Built over 400 years ago under the Ming Wanli dynasty, it's Fuzhou's only water-stage pavilion—remarkable acoustics thanks to the pool beneath the floor. Some evenings feature Min opera or Pinghua, the art of sung storytelling. Variable program; check on-site.

Taste 'Buddha Jumps Over the Wall' (Fotiaoqiang), Fuzhou's iconic dish. This slow-cooked stew contains 18 ingredients including sea cucumber, shark fin, abalone, ginseng, pigeon—cooked for hours in a clay pot. The name comes from a legend: the aroma would have made a Buddhist monk jump over the monastery wall to taste it. Count on 200-400 yuan per person in a good restaurant.

Late afternoon, stroll Yantai Hill (Yantai Shan) on the south bank of the Min River. The quarter preserves 19th-century foreign concessions: consulates, churches, English and American mansions testify to Fuzhou's opening in 1842 after the Treaty of Nanking. Shanghai-like atmosphere but more discreet.

Before your flight, board a night cruise on the Min River. The boat departs from Taijiang Wharf around 7 PM, crosses illuminated bridges (Sanxian Bridge, May Third Bridge), runs along Yantai Hill, and circles the central island. Duration 1 hour, ticket 100 yuan. Perfect view of modern Fuzhou and its lit skyline.

For your final meal, visit a Min cuisine specialist: split fish steamed, yan dumplings in broth, Fuzhou red vinegar. Restaurants Anjia or Juchunyuan (an institution since 1865) are the references. Count on 200-400 yuan per person for a full menu. Changle Airport is 1 hour by taxi (about 100 yuan).

days_plan.itin_title: Coastal Fujian - Beyond the Tulou, 11 Days on the Maritime Silk Road
Coastal Fujian - Beyond the Tulou, 11 Days on the Maritime Silk Road
Coastal Fujian - Beyond the Tulou, 11 Days on the Maritime Silk Road

map_keywords: Sanfang Qixiang Three Lanes Seven Alleys Fuzhou

places.name: Guangzhou

visits.text: Arrive early on Shamian Island, before couples in Chinese wedding gowns flood the tree-lined avenues. A British and French concession from 1859 onward, Shamian today unfolds beneath century-old banyan trees, among pastel-colored colonial buildings, the former Catholic Church of Our Lady of Lourdes, and the old HSBC building. It's Guangzhou's most striking contrast: 0.3 km² of hushed calm pressed against a metropolis of 18 million souls.

Descend into the burial chamber of Zhao Mo, second king of Nanyue, preserved intact beneath the museum built atop it. The tomb was discovered by chance in 1983 during construction work. Dating from 122 BC, it contained a jade burial suit sewn with silk thread, over 1,000 artifacts, and even the skeletons of sacrificed concubines. Allow two hours—it's one of the most eloquent archaeological sites in southern China.

The Chen Clan Ancestral Hall dates to 1894. Seventy-two Chen lineages across Guangdong pooled funds to build this immense ancestral temple, now a museum of Cantonese folk arts. Look up at the ceramic roof friezes: hundreds of polychrome figurines depict entire Chinese operas. The wood carvings and brick sculptures rank among the finest in China. An hour is enough, but you'll revisit it ten times in your mind.

Sit for ten minutes in the courtyard of the Temple of the Six Banyan Trees, founded in 537 and now wedged between two skyscrapers—it still draws monks and worshippers. Its Flower Pagoda, 57 meters tall and rebuilt in 1097, is Guangzhou's silent emblem. The poet Su Dongpo gave it its name in 1100 after seeing six banyan trees in the courtyard. Modest entry fee, contemplative atmosphere—perfect after the bustle of the Chen Hall.

Climb the five-story Zhenhai Pagoda in Yuexiu Park, built in 1380—it offers the finest historic view over the city. The park also shelters the Statue of the Five Rams, Guangzhou's founding myth: five immortals descended from heaven on rams bearing grain to end a famine. Locals gather here to dance, sing, and fly kites. Free entry, allow two hours.

Wander Yongqingfang without a plan, get lost—it's a chance to see how Guangzhou reinvents its past without turning it into a museum. This traditional Liwan district was renovated between 2016 and 2020: gray-brick qilou arcade houses, cobbled lanes, artisan workshops, design boutiques. Bruce Lee lived here as a child; his family home is open to visitors on a neighboring street. Allow 1.5 hours.

Pause beneath the glass panels on Beijing Road: at your feet, authentic Tang dynasty paving stones layered atop those from Song, Ming, and Qing—eleven strata of pavement spanning 1,300 years. The street is one of China's oldest commercial arteries, now pedestrianized and crowded with contemporary brands, but these archaeological windows are unmissable.

activities.text: Dim sum was born in Guangzhou: the yum cha (tea drinking) tradition has made Cantonese brunch a 2,000-year-old ritual. Step into Panxi or Guangzhou Restaurant to taste har gao (shrimp dumplings), siu mai, char siu bao, and chicken feet in black bean sauce. Choose your dishes from circulating carts. Expect 80 to 150 yuan per person. Arrive before 11 a.m. to secure a table.

Ascend Canton Tower at sunset. At 604 meters, it ranks among the world's tallest towers, and its twisted-hourglass silhouette has become the city's icon. The observation deck at 488 meters offers 360° views of the Pearl River and the skyscraper forests of Zhujiang New Town. For the brave: the Sky Drop, the world's highest thrill ride certified by Guinness. Book online to skip the line.

The Pearl River night cruise runs about an hour. You board at Tianzi Pier or Dashatou and glide beneath illuminated bridges, facing Canton Tower as it shifts colors every few minutes. It's touristy, yes, but the skyline spectacle from the water is impossible to replicate on foot. Expect 100 to 150 yuan, departures between 7 p.m. and 9 p.m.

In the evening, head to Bingsheng or Pearl River Restaurant to taste Guangdong's most emblematic dishes: crispy-skin chicken (spit-roasted then flash-fried in boiling oil), roast Shenjing goose, seafood congee, choy sum greens in oyster sauce. Cantonese cuisine prizes extreme freshness and minimal cooking. Reserve ahead, especially on weekends.

days_plan.itin_title: Subtropical Southern China in 12 Days: Canton, Hakka Diaspora & Hong Kong Peninsula
Subtropical Southern China in 12 Days: Canton, Hakka Diaspora & Hong Kong Peninsula

map_keywords: Museum of the Mausoleum of the Nanyue King Guangzhou
Temple of the Six Banyan Trees Guangzhou

visits.text: Reed Flute Cave (Lúdíyán), 5 km northwest of downtown, takes its name from the reeds that once grew at its entrance—perfect for making bamboo flutes. Carved by karst erosion roughly 700,000 years ago, the cave extends 240 meters deep with 500 meters of galleries open to visitors. Seventy-seven ink inscriptions on the walls date back to the Tang dynasty (618–907), making this one of China's oldest documented tourist sites. The central hall, the Crystal Palace of the Dragon King, measures 93 meters wide and 18 meters high. During World War II, over a thousand Guilin residents sheltered here during Japanese bombing raids.

Elephant Trunk Hill (Xiàngbíshān), where the Taohua River meets the Li River, is Guilin's emblem. This 55-meter limestone pinnacle, formed 360 million years ago on an ancient seabed, depicts an elephant dipping its trunk into the water. Between trunk and body opens the Shuiyue Cave (Moon in the Water), 12 meters high and 9.5 meters wide—at night, its arch and reflection form a perfect circle on the river. At the summit stands Puxian Pagoda, built during the Ming dynasty (1368–1644), rising about 14 meters. Legend tells of a celestial elephant sent by the Jade Emperor who fell in love with Guilin and refused to return; enraged, the Emperor pierced it with a sword, transforming it into stone.

Solitary Beauty Peak (Dúxiù Fēng) and the Jingjiang Princes' Palace occupy the exact center of Guilin. The palace was built between 1372 and 1392 for Zhu Shouqian, great-nephew of the Ming dynasty founder—its construction predates Beijing's Forbidden City. Fourteen princes across twelve generations lived here for 257 years. Under the Qing, the palace became an imperial examination center where over 590 scholars trained. The peak itself—a 66-meter limestone pillar formed 350 million years ago—bears 136 rock inscriptions on its flanks, the most famous carved over 800 years ago: "Guilin's scenery is the finest under heaven." Climb 306 steps to the summit for a 360° view over the city and Li River.

Seven Star Park (Qīxīng Gōngyuán), Guilin's largest at 120 hectares, takes its name from seven karst peaks arranged like the Big Dipper. A destination since the Sui and Tang dynasties (6th–10th century), it shelters the Seven Star Cave, whose galleries stretch over 1.5 km through Putuo Mountain—an ancient underground river roughly one million years old. Entry is through the Flower Bridge (Huā Qiáo), Guilin's oldest bridge, built during the Song dynasty (960–1279). At the foot of Crescent Mountain, the Guihai Forest of Steles assembles 218 engraved tablets from the Tang through Qing dynasties, considered China's second-largest stele collection after Xi'an.

Daxu old town (Dàxū), 15 km southeast of Guilin on the Li River's banks, is one of Guangxi's four best-preserved ancient towns. Founded in 200 BCE, it was an important trading post on the Li River. Its lanes paved with river pebbles are lined with wooden and stone houses from the Ming and Qing dynasties. The covered market, still active, sells fermented tofu, medicinal herbs, and local basketwork.

activities.text: Evening stroll along the Two Rivers and Four Lakes circuit (Liǎng Jiāng Sì Hú). The Sun and Moon Pagodas, rising from Shanhu Lake, light up after dark—the Sun Pagoda, cast in bronze, stands 41 meters tall across 9 stories. The route follows willow-lined and banyan-shaded banks, offering views of illuminated karst peaks in the heart of the city.

Tasting Guilin rice noodles (Guìlín mǐfěn) at a downtown shop. The local recipe uses a pork and beef broth slowly simmered with spices, served over fresh rice noodles with braised meat and pickled vegetables. Every neighborhood has its variant—try the smoked horse version (mǎròu mǐfěn), a local specialty since the Qin dynasty.

Final shopping and tasting at Guilin's central market: Guangxi tea, Flower Bridge hot pepper sauce, osmanthus cakes (guìhuā gāo)—the osmanthus flower is Guilin's emblem, whose name literally means "osmanthus forest."

days_plan.itin_title: Karst Heartlands: Guilin, Yangshuo & Longji Rice Terraces
Karst Heartlands: Guilin, Yangshuo & Longji Rice Terraces
Karst Heartlands: Guilin, Yangshuo & Longji Rice Terraces
Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi
Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi

visits.text: The walled town of Qingyan, 29 km south of Guiyang, was built in 1378 as a Ming military outpost to control the empire's southwestern frontier. Constructed entirely from local limestone—walls, streets, houses—it sprawls across 3 km² and preserves four monumental gates rising 9.5 meters high. A rare feature in China: Buddhism, Taoism, Catholicism, and Protestantism have coexisted within its walls since a French missionary arrived in 1851.

Step through the gate of Ciyun Temple in Qingyan, where stone carvings on column bases rank among the finest in Guiyang. Wanshou Palace, a former Jiangxi merchant's trading house converted into a Taoist temple in 1778, preserves a theater stage adorned with bas-reliefs depicting historical legends.

Jiaxiu Tower, in central Guiyang, stands on a rock in the middle of the Nanming River. This three-story Ming dynasty wooden structure with upturned eaves served as a study and meditation retreat for scholars. It remains the city's most recognizable symbol.

activities.text: Street food tasting in Qingyan's alleyways: braised pork trotters in soy sauce (a local specialty since the Qing dynasty), Qingyan tofu, and rice cake porridge rank among Guizhou's most celebrated snacks.

days_plan.itin_title: Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian
Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi

visits.text: En route to Gyantse, Kamba La Pass (4,794m) offers the first view of Yamdrok Lake (羊卓雍错)—a 630km² sacred turquoise lake encircled by snow-capped mountains. It's one of Tibet's three great sacred lakes along with Namtso and Manasarovar. Stop for 20 minutes for photos from the pass.

Pelkor Chode Monastery (白居寺, 3,900m) is unique in Tibet: it unites under one roof the three major Tibetan Buddhism schools—Gelugpa, Sakyapa, and Kadampa—a feat of inter-sect tolerance founded in 1418 by Prince Rabten Kunzang Phak. Its assembly hall houses an 8-meter bronze Sakyamuni statue. The adjoining Kumbum is its masterpiece.

Gyantse's Kumbum (十万佛塔) is a 32-meter stupa built between 1418 and 1427, the only stupa of this type in all of Tibet. Its 9 stories house 77 chapels, 108 doors, and more than 10,000 Buddha images. Climb chapel by chapel clockwise: the 15th-century frescoes, in Newar-Tibetan style, are among the finest in all Himalayan Buddhist art. On the 4th floor, four pairs of eyes watch the four cardinal directions.

activities.text: Ascend Gyantse Dzong (14th-century medieval fortress) dominating the town from a rocky spur. In 1904, Younghusband's troops bombarded it during the British expedition to Tibet. The museum inside relates this colonial chapter from the Tibetan viewpoint. Splendid view over the town and valley.

days_plan.itin_title: From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days

visits.text: Welcome to Haikou, Hainan's capital and your gateway to the tropical island. First stop: Qilou Street in the historic quarter. Over 600 arcade houses in Sino-European style, built between 1820 and 1940 by overseas Chinese returning from Singapore, Penang, and Saigon. The Sipai Building, the oldest, dates back to the Southern Song dynasty (12th century). Baroque, Indian, Arab, and Asian architecture blended together - unique in China.

Five kilometers west of downtown, don't miss Hai Rui's Tomb, honoring a Ming dynasty mandarin born in Haikou in 1514 who became a symbol of Confucian integrity. Hai Rui dared criticize Emperor Jiajing in a famous 1566 letter, was imprisoned, then rehabilitated. Mao Zedong made him an official hero. The stone mausoleum dates from 1589, restored under the Qing and again in 1996. Quiet, shaded, rarely crowded.

Stop by the Temple of the Five Officials (Wugong Ci), 2 km southeast, honoring five mandarins exiled to Hainan under the Tang and Song dynasties for standing up to the imperial court. The current temple dates from 1889, built of red lacquered wood and framed by century-old banyans. It's the island's oldest temple. Combined with Hai Rui's Tomb, allow half a day for the historical sites.

Visit the Hainan Provincial Museum, free admission, opened in 2008. You'll find 60,000 pieces covering the island's history - shipwrecks loaded with ceramics from the Maritime Silk Road, Li and Miao minority costumes, tropical forest fauna. Good context before the rest of your journey. Closed Mondays.

Head to Shishan Volcanic Geopark, 15 km southwest of Haikou. You'll find over 40 inactive craters, including Mahushan, formed roughly 13,000 years ago - geologically recent. You can descend into the caldera via basalt stairs, among ferns and strangler figs. UNESCO Global Geopark site. Allow 2 hours on-site, early morning before the heat hits.

Don't miss Dongzhai Mangrove Reserve, east of Haikou: China's largest mangrove forest, covering over 3,300 hectares, with more than 200 species of migratory birds. Boat tour through the aerial roots, stop at the observation station. Fragile ecosystem: no touching shellfish or crabs. Very few foreign tourists, unlike the rest of the island.

Holiday Beach, Haikou's urban beach, is free and open to all. You'll find blonde sand, warm water (24-26°C in winter), coconut palms, street vendors selling fresh coconuts in the background. Not the island's most beautiful coastline - save Sanya for that - but ideal for a late afternoon rest before continuing your journey. Sunset over the Qiongzhou Strait.

Return to Haikou in two stages. First 80 km by road from Wuzhishan to Sanya HSR station (about 1h30), as Wuzhishan isn't served by the circular rail line. Then 1h30 express train to Haikou East, along the southeast coast. The complete Hainan loop totals 653 km - the world's only circular high-speed railway, western section completed in 2015. Views of rice paddies and coconut groves from the carriage.

Upon arrival, end your stay with a stop at Haikou International Duty-Free Shopping Complex. It's the world's largest duty-free store since opening in 2022 - 280,000 m², over 800 international brands. For foreigners, it's a chance to buy alcohol, perfumes and cosmetics tax-free before the return flight.

Before Meilan airport (25 minutes from downtown), take a final walk through Qilou arcade street, grab a last Hainan coffee on a terrace. Wenchang chicken rice, noodle soup, green mango with chili - the classics for a last meal. Meilan airport has direct connections to most major Chinese cities, plus Hong Kong, Singapore, Bangkok and Seoul.

activities.text: Dive into Haikou cuisine at lunch. Head to Bo'ai Street for authentic Hainan noodle soup (Hainan fen) or chicken rice (Wenchang chicken rice), the island's most iconic dish, exported all the way to Singapore. Next door, a tropical fruit market: mangosteens, rambutans, dragon fruit. Also try the iced qing bu liang tea, a mix of beans, taro, and shaved ice.

In the evening, stroll through Qilou Old Street after dark, when the arcades light up. Craft shops, café terraces, contemporary art exhibitions in former consulates. Slow atmosphere, somewhere between Macau and Singapore. Perfect for a cocktail or iced tea on a terrace.

Book a Hainanese cooking class with a local family (via schools like Cookly or Haikou Cooking Class). On the menu: Wenchang chicken, three-sauce lamb, sago dessert. Wenchang chicken requires slow poaching, crispy skin and white meat, served with ginger-garlic sauce. It's Hainan's national dish and the basis for Singapore's famous Hainan Chicken Rice.

Spend the evening on Haidian Island, connected to downtown by three bridges. You'll find the student and professional quarter, with rooftop bars, food trucks, and vibrant alleys. Sample Hainan coffee (Singapore style, strong, sweet, condensed milk) and watch cruise ships enter the harbor.

If your flight leaves in the evening, stop at Mission Hills Haikou, the world's largest golf complex with ten courses designed by Jack Nicklaus, Greg Norman, Annika Sörenstam and others. Greens at 600-1,200 yuan on weekdays, booking required. Spa and hot springs on-site for non-golfers.

days_plan.itin_title: Tropical Hainan: 10 Days Around China's Southern Island
Tropical Hainan: 10 Days Around China's Southern Island
Tropical Hainan: 10 Days Around China's Southern Island

visits.text: West Lake (西湖) has been UNESCO-listed since 2011 - one of the rare designed landscapes to receive this recognition. It covers 6.39 km², punctuated by three artificial islands built during the Song Dynasty (960-1279). The Bai Causeway, 1.5 km long, bears the name of the Tang poet who had the lake dredged when he was magistrate of Hangzhou in the 9th century. Marco Polo described the city as "the most beautiful in the world."

Leifeng Pagoda (雷峰塔) rises on the lake's south shore, rebuilt in 2002 as an identical replica of the Song original (977 CE) that collapsed in 1924. The new tower incorporates the ancient foundations, visible on the ground floor through a glass floor. From the top, the view over the lake and Su Causeway is Hangzhou's finest.

Baochu Hill (宝叔岭) north of the lake offers the most classic climb - 45 minutes from the water's edge to Baochu Pagoda (renovated in 1933). From the summit, the entire lake spreads beneath your feet: the five causeways, the islands, and the hills on the opposite shore.

Lingyin Temple (灵隐寺), founded in 326 CE by Indian monk Huili, is one of China's ten largest Buddhist temples - reconstructed sixteen times. The adjacent Feilai Peak (飞来峰, Peak that Flew Here) is covered with 470 rock carvings made between the 10th and 14th centuries, including a particularly remarkable bas-relief Laughing Buddha.

If you leave early this morning, the Longjing tea plantations (龙井) 4 km west of the lake await you. Longjing (Dragon Well) is China's most prized green tea - leaves hand-flattened in cast-iron woks. In April, it's Mingqian season, the first harvest before the Qingming Festival, the finest and most expensive.

Su Causeway (苏堤), 2.8 km long, crosses the lake from shore to shore with six arched bridges and weeping willows. Built under the governance of poet Su Dongpo in the 11th century with silt from dredging the lake, it's inscribed within the UNESCO perimeter. The late afternoon walk is one of Hangzhou's most pleasant.

activities.text: Rent a bike and do the full West Lake loop (15 km). The bike paths are flat, well-marked, follow the water almost everywhere. It's the best way to see the lake without getting stuck with walking groups.

Dine on the lake shores in the Nanshan Road area. Try: Dongpo pork (pork braised in rice wine, recipe attributed to poet Su Dongpo, magistrate here in the 11th century), West Lake vinegar fish, and cooked lotus.

Longjing tea tasting at a producer's in Longjing or Meijiawu villages. Several teahouses set among the tea bushes offer guided tastings with views over the cultivation rows. Much cheaper than in the city.

Take a boat ride on the lake to reach Three Pools Mirroring the Moon Island (三潭印月) - three small stone towers built in the water during the Song Dynasty. The crossing takes 20 minutes from Hubin pier.

days_plan.itin_title: Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui
Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

places.name: Harbin

visits.text: Welcome to Harbin, the former "Moscow of the Orient." Founded in 1898 by Russians to build the Transmanchurian Railway, the city welcomed 200,000 White Russians after the 1917 revolution. Start with Saint Sophia Cathedral, Byzantine, built in 1907 and reconstructed in red brick in 1932. Decommissioned since 1958, it now houses an architecture museum. In winter, its snow-dusted onion domes give the impression of being in Saint Petersburg.

Central Street (Zhongyang Dajie) stretches 1.4 km and preserves dozens of Art Nouveau, Russian Baroque and Neoclassical buildings from the 1900s-1930s. Paved with granite blocks, it's pedestrianized. Taste Madieer ice cream (from the local Russian brand, serving stick ice cream at -25°C on the street since 1906) and Dalieba Russian bread, sold in 1 kg loaves.

Zhaolin Park hosts the Ice Lantern Festival, the world's oldest ice lantern exhibition (since 1963). Smaller than Ice and Snow World, but more intimate, with about 300 sculptures lit from within. Open 9 AM to 9 PM. Ideal for a first immersion evening before the big park tomorrow.

The big day: Harbin Ice and Snow World, the world's largest ice theme park. The 27th edition covers 1.2 million m² with over 2,000 sculptures, some taller than 14 stories. The 2026 festival opened January 5 and runs through late February. Ice blocks are carved from the frozen Songhua. Entry 328 yuan. The park is open 10 AM to 10 PM. Arrive around 3 PM to see the sculptures in daylight, then the illumination at sunset.

In the morning, cross the Songhua to Sun Island for the International Snow Sculpture Expo. Over 260 sculptures, some taller than 20 m, carved by artists from around the world. No night lighting here: must see in daylight. Open 9 AM-5 PM, entry 198 yuan.

On Sun Island, detour through Volga Manor, a park-village reconstructed in Russian style with a wooden Orthodox church, izbas, troikas. Kitsch but photogenic under snow. Many Chinese weddings take photos here with Russian costumes rented on-site.

Return to Harbin to close the journey with the city's darkest and most moving pages. The Unit 731 Museum, 25 km from the center, occupies the former site of the Japanese army's bacteriological experimentation laboratory (1936-1945). Over 3,000 prisoners - Chinese, Russian, Korean - were used as guinea pigs. The museum, modernized in 2015, is solemn and essential. Free admission, closed Mondays.

The New Synagogue Museum tells the story of the 20,000 Jews who lived in Harbin between 1898 and 1950. Fleeing Russian pogroms then Nazi Germany, they built schools, newspapers and synagogues. The new synagogue of 1921, in yellow brick, now houses the museum. A largely forgotten history. Admission 25 yuan.

If energy permits, take a final daylight tour of Zhongyang Dajie to spot details you missed: carved facades, mosaics of the Modern Hotel (1906), amber fossil souvenir shops (Manchuria is one of the world's major sources). Buy a final dalieba for the road.

activities.text: Walk on the frozen Songhua in the evening. The river between the two banks forms a kilometer of solid ice where locals set up amusement rides, sleds pulled by Mongolian horses, inner tube slides, and even traditional ice wrestling stands. All free or nearly so. You understand why Harbin is nicknamed the "Ice City."

Russian dinner: the Lucia or Russia Coffee & Food restaurant on Zhongyang Dajie serve borscht, piroshki, Chicken Kiev and local salmon caviar. Expect 150-250 yuan per person. Red velvet atmosphere, samovar and pianist: it's as kitsch as it is charming.

Inside Ice and Snow World, tackle the 521 m long ice slide, the park's largest. Free with admission but sometimes a long queue. Guaranteed thrills: you descend at full speed, hood flying, in a channel carved in ice. Kids and adults alike love it.

Taste grilled lamb skewers from street stands, a Hui Muslim specialty very common in the northeast. The Daoli district around Zhongyang Dajie is full of them. With a Harbin beer (the local brand has brewed since 1900, founded by Russians), it's the ideal post-festival supper.

Before departure, lunch at a real Harbin inn: guo bao rou (sweet and sour fried pork, invented in Harbin for Russian diplomats in 1907) and northeast jiaozi (dumplings). The Laochuanji restaurant, in the center, is an institution. Expect 100 yuan.

Transfer to Harbin Taiping International Airport (HRB), 35 km from the center, about 45 minutes by taxi. Direct flights to Beijing, Shanghai, Seoul and several Russian cities. One last Manchu detail before takeoff: grab a Russian coffee at the airport, a still-thriving local legacy.

days_plan.itin_title: Winter Manchuria - snow, sacred volcano and North Korean border
Winter Manchuria - snow, sacred volcano and North Korean border
Winter Manchuria - snow, sacred volcano and North Korean border

map_keywords: Ice Lantern Garden Party Zhaolin Park Harbin
Sun Island International Snow Sculpture Expo Harbin

visits.text: Hemu is the other jewel of Kanas. A Tuva village of log cabins, nestled in a birch valley, 60 km from the lake. The Tuvas - a Turko-Mongol people, 2,500 individuals total in the region - claim descent from Genghis Khan's soldiers left here in 1219 during the western campaign. Their cabins, unique in China, resemble those of Siberia: steep roofs, smoke curling above chimneys. Stroll early morning through the lanes, before the buses arrive at 10 AM.

Climb to the east hill viewpoint behind the village. Thirty minutes of steep ascent, but the reward is worth the effort: plunging view over all the roofs, the Hemu River winding through birches, and snowy peaks in the background. This is the iconic shot of northern Xinjiang.

Visit the small Tuva ethnographic museum in the village center. Traditional costumes, throat-singing instruments (the khoomei), archival photos from the 1950s when the region was definitively attached to China. Half an hour suffices, but it provides context.

Walk upstream along the Hemu River for 2-3 km. The trail follows the water, passes meadows of wildflowers in June (columbines, gentians, edelweiss), and opens onto deserted banks. Perfect picnic spot with naan bread, local cheese, and fruit bought in the village.

activities.text: If you're here in October, you'll catch the 'golden forest': the birches yellow all at once, the cabins vanish in a yellow tide. This is the season when Chinese photographers flock here - book accommodation six months in advance.

Horseback ride with a Tuva herder, from 1 hour to half a day. Altay horses are small, sturdy, perfectly adapted to the terrain. Expect 100-150 yuan per hour. Ask if you can see the reindeer - a few families still raise them, extremely rare in China.

Evening of Tuva throat singing in a yurt or cabin open to visitors. Khoomei produces two simultaneous notes - a deep drone, a high whistled melody. Spectacular and very ancient. Some local singers give demonstrations for 50-100 yuan. Inquire in the village.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Xinjiang - Kanas, Altai, and Ili Steppes in 16 Days

visits.text: You arrive in Hohhot, capital of Inner Mongolia, by high-speed train from Beijing (about 2.5 hours). Head first to Dazhao Temple, founded in 1579 by Tümed Mongol leader Altan Khan—it's the oldest and largest Tibetan Buddhist monastery in the city, and its original construction. Inside, the 2.55-meter silver Buddha consecrated by the 3rd Dalai Lama in 1586 has remained intact for over four centuries. The complex covers 30,000 square meters. Enter from the quieter pedestrian street on the south side.

Five minutes on foot from Dazhao stands Xilitu Zhao Temple, built during the Longqing and Wanli reigns. More discreet, more alive—monks still chant here every morning. Push through the gate before 9 AM when the air is still cool and the courtyards empty. The murals in the main hall are superb, minimally restored, with their original pigments still vibrant.

Built between 1727 and 1732 under Emperor Yongzheng, the Five Pagoda Temple (Wuta Si) is what remains of the grand Cideng Si complex, now vanished. The main structure, 16 meters tall, supports five stone pagodas covered with 1,563 small Buddha figures. Walk behind the pagoda: on the north wall you'll find the only known astronomical map carved in Mongolian script, cataloguing over 1,550 stars. Diameter 144.5 cm, unique in the world.

The Inner Mongolia Museum houses over 100,000 pieces, including the famous dinosaur skeletons from the region—one of the most impressive Mamenchisaurus specimens on display in China. A good grounding before heading out to the steppe. Allow two hours; closed Mondays.

Seven kilometers south of the center, the Tomb of Wang Zhaojun. Legend tells that this Han imperial concubine was sent in diplomatic marriage to the Xiongnu khan in 33 BC, sealing peace between the two peoples. The mound is immense, covered in spring green grass—hence its nickname, the Green Tomb. More than the tomb itself, it's the park and statues that warrant the visit.

activities.text: For dinner, head to Saishang Old Street or the Islamic quarter near the Great Mosque. Try the mutton shaomai (Hohhot's specialty), salty milk tea (suutei tsai), and nai pi flatbread. Locals eat fast, standing, in noisy canteens.

Evening stroll through the Hui quarter around Hohhot's Great Mosque, built in the late 18th century in a Ming-style Sino-Islamic architecture unique in China. The minaret-pavilion crowned with a Chinese roof is an architectural curiosity. The neighborhood comes alive after dark with dried fruit merchants and Uyghur bread bakers.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner Mongolia: Steppes, Dunes, and Forgotten Empires

places.name: Hong Kong

visits.text: Flight Guilin→Hong Kong. Victoria Peak (Sky Terrace 428 m). Peak Tram (1888), climbs in 6 min. Trail of 3.5 km around the peak.
Tsim Sha Tsui Promenade, Avenue of Stars. A Symphony of Lights every evening at 8pm—over 40 illuminated buildings.
Tian Tan Big Buddha on Lantau (1993, 34 m, 250 tonnes, 268 steps). Po Lin Monastery (1920s), vegetarian lunch of the monks.
Tai O fishing village (30 min by bus). Stilt houses, dried shrimp paste.
Mong Kok in Kowloon: flower, goldfish, bird markets and Ladies' Market. One of the world's most densely populated districts.
Man Mo Temple, Hollywood Road (mid-19th century). Suspended incense coils burning for weeks.
Mid-Levels Escalator (800 m, 135 m elevation, 1993). World's longest outdoor covered escalator.

activities.text: Star Ferry (founded 1880s, company since 1898). Crossing Tsim Sha Tsui-Central in 8-10 min.
Ngong Ping 360 cable car (25 min) from Tung Chung. Crystal Cabin option (glass floor).
Evening at Temple Street Night Market: dim sum, wonton, egg waffle, fortune tellers, street Cantonese opera.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie
Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie
Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie

map_keywords: Tsim Sha Tsui Promenade Hong Kong
Tian Tan Buddha Lantau Hong Kong
Tai O Fishing Village Hong Kong
Temple Street Night Market Hong Kong

visits.text: Hongcun (宏村), founded in 1131 during the Song Dynasty by the Wang family, is designed in the shape of an ox. The Moon Pond (月沼) at the center, dug in 1403, is the village's most photographed setting: white-walled, gray-roofed Hui houses reflect in the still water. This is where Ang Lee filmed several scenes of Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon (2000). UNESCO-listed the same year.

Hongcun's 150 Ming-Qing residences are the region's finest collection of Hui architecture. Lexu Hall and Jingde Tang are open to visitors: carved bracket roof frames, light-well courtyards (天井), blue stone paving. Explore the side lanes for houses still inhabited.

activities.text: Set yourself up by the Moon Pond before 7am with a sketchbook or camera. Chinese amateur painters are there before you - genuine open-air artistic culture at Hongcun. It's also the hour when residents go about their business before the influx of tour groups.

days_plan.itin_title: Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

visits.text: Lotus Peak (莲花峰) reaches 1,864 m - Huangshan's highest summit. The mountain was named by imperial decree in 747 CE; legend has it that Yellow Emperor Huangdi came here seeking the elixir of immortality 2,600 years BCE. Since the 16th century, it's been the favorite subject of the Shanshui painting school. UNESCO-listed in 1990.

White Goose Ridge (白鹅岭) is directly accessible from the Yungu cable car on the east side. From there, a trail connects to Bright Summit in 2 hours via the ridges. It's along this route that you'll find Huangshan's most famous pines: Welcoming Guest Pine, Couple Pine, Umbrella Pine - each has a name, a silhouette from classical painting.

Bright Summit (光明顶, 1,860 m) is the logistical hub for summit stays: this is where hotels, the weather center, and high-altitude restaurants concentrate. Book the West Sea Hotel several weeks in advance. The flat summit allows a clear 360° view even in light mist. Bring warm clothing even in May - 10°C at night is common.

The West Sea Grand Canyon (西海大峡谷) is Huangshan's most spectacular hike - a 5-hour loop that descends into the gorges then ascends by monorail. The site's most dramatic granite formations concentrate here, with stone columns emerging from the clouds. The West Sea Pavilion (built in 1935) offers the widest view over the entire canyon.
Huangshan Hot Springs (黄山温泉), at the foot of the mountain on the Yungu side, let you decompress after two days of hiking. The water emerges at 42°C and the outdoor pools face the forest.

activities.text: Take the Yungu cable car on the east side then hike to connect Bright Summit, Lotus Peak, and White Goose Ridge - count 5 to 6 hours for this classic circuit. The trails are in excellent condition, with steps carved into the rock on steep passages.
Set yourself up at sunset on Bright Summit or Celestial Capital Peak. The late-day light tints the granite peaks from gold to orange - Huangshan's other magic hour, besides sunrise.

Wake at 5am for sunrise from Bright Summit or Celestial Capital Peak. The sea of clouds below the ridges is the spectacle that defines Huangshan - it's why Ming painters would set up here for months. Best period: November to May, after rain.
Descend the mountain by cable car after sunrise, retrieve your luggage at Tangkou, and drive toward Hongcun (25 km, 40 min). Lunch in Tangkou village before departure.

days_plan.itin_title: Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui
Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

visits.text: Conquer Mount Hua (Huashan), one of China's five sacred mountains. The western peak rises to 2,154 meters, accessible via cable car or challenging hiking trails including the infamous 'Soldier's Path' with 4,000 steep steps carved into sheer cliffs.

Experience the North Peak via the Gorge Trail or the more demanding routes that 'break the legs' with endless stairs. As you ascend, spectacular panoramas unfold across the surrounding plains and distant peaks.

Discover ancient temples and shrines perched on precipitous ledges throughout the mountain. Taoist monks have practiced here for millennia, drawn by the mountain's spiritual power and breathtaking natural beauty.

activities.text: Challenge yourself on the famous plank walk, narrow wooden boards bolted to vertical cliff faces offering heart-stopping views. This iconic attraction draws adventurers from around the world.

days_plan.itin_title: The Silk Road Overland: Xi'an to Istanbul via Uzbekistan & Turkmenistan

visits.text: You're now in another world: the Ili valley, an immense fertile plain in southwestern Xinjiang. Flight Altay-Yining (2h) or drive via Urumqi for those with time. If you arrive in June, rush to Huocheng and its lavender fields - the world's second-largest production area after Provence, about 4,000 hectares cultivated by state farms since the 1960s. Flowering runs from June 10 to July 10, peaking around June 25.

The steppes of Xinyuan district, farther east, are among China's vastest. Cavalcades of Ili horses (famous breed since the Han dynasty, raised for the imperial cavalry), Kazakh yurts, sheep flocks as far as the eye can see. Stop at Naraty Grassland (250 km from Yining) for half a day if the itinerary allows.

Along the route, the Ili River (Yili) flows in wide meanders between poplars. It's one of the rare Chinese rivers that empties abroad - in Kazakhstan, into Lake Balkhash. The bridges make for beautiful sunset photos.

activities.text: Lavender distillery in Huocheng: tour the fields and factory, sample essential oils, soaps, and infusions. Most Chinese lavender cosmetics come from here. Allow 1.5 hours for a complete guided tour.

If you stay with a Kazakh family in a yurt (offered by several base camps around Naraty), share the evening meal: besh barmak (flat noodles with mutton), tangy fresh cheese, butter tea. Kazakhs still largely live from seasonal herding in the valley.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Xinjiang - Kanas, Altai, and Ili Steppes in 16 Days

visits.text: Jianshui's Confucian Temple was founded in 1285 under the Yuan dynasty. With 114 buildings spread over 7.5 hectares, it's considered China's third-largest after those in Qufu and Beijing. Unlike those two, it still functions as a school - you'll encounter high school students in the same courtyards as tourists.

Chaoyang Gate, built in 1389 under the Ming dynasty, looks exactly like the Tiananmen tower in Beijing - it was constructed four years before it. From the top, you have a direct view down the old town's main axis. Free entry.

The Shuanglong 17-arch bridge spans Lake Yilong over 148 meters. Built in 1839, it remains Yunnan's longest multi-arch bridge. Late in the day, the raking light on the lake's reflections is particularly beautiful.

Tuanshan lies 13 km west of Jianshui: a village whose Ming-Qing residences have remained intact since the 17th century. The courtyard houses (siheyuan) are in excellent condition - some have been converted to guesthouses. The guided tour included with entry lasts 45 minutes.

The small Xianglu stone bridge at Tuanshan's entrance dates from the same Ming period. The water is clear and village women still come to wash laundry in the morning - a daily scene unchanged for generations.

On the way back to Jianshui in the afternoon, stop at the pottery kilns in the northern quarter. These active workshops have produced unglazed purple ceramics for centuries - wood-firing in direct-flame kilns gives pieces their characteristic metallic luster.

Transit stop: the Yuanyang→Jianshui route descends 3 hours of switchback passes from 1,800 m. Arrival early afternoon. One last pass by Chaoyang Gate at sunset - the light on Ming brick differs from morning.

activities.text: Jianshui purple pottery is made from naturally brown-violet local clay, unglazed, dating back to the Song dynasty. Visit an active workshop in the Xichuang quarter to see throwing and sand-polishing - some offer a 2-hour introduction for 80 to 120 RMB.

Don't leave without eating Jianshui grilled tofu - sold on skewers throughout the old town, topped with green chili and salt. It's Yunnan's most famous street specialty. The best stands open from 5 PM near Chaoyang Gate.
Have lunch in a Tuanshan house-restaurant: residents serve Bai and Han dishes cooked over wood fires in their own kitchens. No menu - they bring you what's ready. Count on 50 to 80 RMB per person.
Evening stroll through old Jianshui: Linchuan Lu pedestrian street comes alive after 7 PM with skewer stands, mahjong players, and sesame candy vendors. Less touristy than the main street.
Duck noodles (鸭子米线) dinner before tomorrow morning's train to Kunming - broth simmered 8 hours, served with local rice noodles. It's Jianshui's farewell dish.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Yunnan - beyond the classic circuit
Deep Yunnan - beyond the classic circuit
Deep Yunnan - beyond the classic circuit

visits.text: Before leaving Alxa territory, make a 14-km detour southwest of Mandela town for the Mandela Mountain petroglyphs. Over 18 square kilometers, more than 6,000 rock carvings have been chiseled into basalt rocks by successive nomadic peoples over 5,000 years: hunting scenes, shamans, herds, war chariots. One of the world's largest rock art ensembles, and a perfect stop to break the 5-hour drive to Jiayuguan.

You leave Inner Mongolia to enter Gansu and arrive in Jiayuguan, the western terminus of the Ming Great Wall. Jiayuguan Fortress, built in 1372, marks the western limit of historical Chinese empire: past this gate lay the "barbarian west," meaning the Silk Road. Three concentric enclosures, two gate-towers, 11-meter-high walls. The fortress is intact—one of the best-preserved along the entire Wall.

Nine kilometers north, the "Overhanging Great Wall" (Xuanbi Changcheng) climbs steeply up the flank of the Heishan Mountains. Built in 1539, it closes the northern approach to Jiayuguan Pass. Ascent of 400 steps, immense panorama over desert and snow-capped mountains. The contrast between white peaks and yellow sands is striking.

To the south, the "First Beacon Tower of the Great Wall" (Diyi Dun) plunges steeply into the Taolai River canyon. It's the very first structure of the Wall in the west, erected in 1539—symbolically, the wall begins here; over 6,000 km later it ends at the Bohai Sea. Transparent glass platform above the precipice, for the brave.

Your journey ends here—Jiayuguan is also the classic starting point of the Chinese Silk Road toward Dunhuang, Turpan, and Kashgar. Direct trains to Lanzhou, Xi'an, or Urumqi, or flights from the local airport. Before you leave, have tea on the ramparts—the sunset over the fortress is worth every memory.

activities.text: Final meal in a Muslim restaurant in Jiayuguan's Hui quarter: hand-pulled beef noodles, cumin lamb, wood-fired skewers. Gansu cuisine marks the transition to Uyghur flavors of the Silk Road. Try the desert apples, crisp and sweet, a local specialty.

If time permits, visit the Great Wall Museum in Jiayuguan, tracing 2,000 years of Chinese fortification history. Models, plans, archaeological objects. Well done, not too long. Air-conditioned, which helps when it's 35°C outside.

days_plan.itin_title: China's Silk Road: Xi'an to Kashgar via the Hexi Corridor
Inner Mongolia: Steppes, Dunes, and Forgotten Empires

visits.text: Flight Changbaishan-Changchun then express train to Jilin (40 minutes). You come for a single phenomenon: wusong, or Jilin rime, ranked among China's Four Natural Wonders with the Three Gorges, Yunnan's Stone Forest and Guilin's landscapes. It forms because the Fengman power plant upstream releases water at 4°C that prevents the Songhua River from freezing - the water vapor it releases crystallizes on riverside trees at -20°C or below.

Wusong Island (Wusongdao), 40 km north of Jilin, is the epicenter of the phenomenon. Get up early, really early: the golden light of sunrise on the frosted willows is unmatched. The villages of Hantun and Zengtong, on the banks, have traditional Manchu farms that rent heated kang beds. Around 11 AM, the frost begins to fall from the branches in a rain of crystals: it's the "rime fall."

Beishan Park, in the city itself, offers a panoramic view of the Songhua and the line of frosted trees from its mixed Buddhist and Taoist temples. It's a walking spot for locals in down jackets. Short climb up icy stairs to the summit where several pavilions welcome incense offerings.

Wula, former capital of one of the pre-Qing Manchu kingdoms, can be visited in half a day 30 km north. The ramparts of the old Manchu town are partly preserved, and a museum recounts the history of the Jurchen, ancestors of the Manchus. It's an un-touristy site where you can see still-living Manchu shamanistic traditions.

Jilin's Catholic Church, built by French missionaries in 1917, surprises in the middle of a Chinese city. Neo-Gothic style, two 45 m spires, original stained glass. Still active, it welcomes the local Catholic community. Peculiarity: it survived the Cultural Revolution nearly intact, temporarily converted into a warehouse.

Jilin's Confucius Temple (Wenmiao) dates to 1907. It's one of China's four great Confucian temples, unknown to foreigners. The red wooden buildings are striking under snow. Inside, an exhibition on the imperial examination system - over 1,300 years of institution.

activities.text: Go ice-skating on the Songhua. Several kilometers of ice are developed in season right inside the city, with skate rentals, wooden sleds for children and even bicycle-sleds. It's free or nearly so (10 yuan for rental), packed on weekends, and terribly fun. Open as soon as the ice exceeds 30 cm thickness, generally mid-December.

Taste the frozen specialties of Dongbei in Jilin's street markets: frozen pears (dong li), frozen persimmons (dong shi), frozen tofu in broth. Locals keep fruit outside all winter in bags hung from the balcony - the freeze is their freezer. Biting into a pear at -20°C is like crunching raw sorbet.

Songhua Lake ski complex (Vanke Songhua Lake Resort), 25 km southeast, is one of the northeast's most appreciated. Voted China's best resort five times in recent years. 30 runs, abundant natural snow, family atmosphere and fair prices (day pass around 350 yuan, ski rental 150 yuan).

To end the day, plunge into a northern hot spring. The Shengbeihu spa, in the heights north of Jilin, offers outdoor pools with views over the frozen valley. Local peculiarity: you drink warm baijiu between soaks, an unapologetic Manchu tradition. Expect 200 yuan.

days_plan.itin_title: Winter Manchuria - snow, sacred volcano and North Korean border
Winter Manchuria - snow, sacred volcano and North Korean border

visits.text: Welcome to Jinan, the City of 733 Springs. Start at Baotu, the most powerful of the 72 named springs. Water gushes in three jets from an underground karst cavity, sometimes shooting up to 26 meters high. Emperor Qianlong, who visited the site in the 18th century, bestowed upon it the title of "First Spring Under Heaven" and used its water for his tea. The current park dates from 1956. Enter through the south gate, take time for tea at Wangheting Pavilion, and exit through the north gate to reach the lake.

Head to Daming Lake, fed precisely by these springs. It's the only urban lake in China formed this way. Emperor Qianlong had his summer residence on an island here. Today the shores host cafés, parks, and a large marble block engraved with a 1929 text by Mao Zedong. A one-hour boat ride connects Daming to the Black Tiger and Five Dragon Pool spring clusters. Practical tip: the boat tour combines several sites while avoiding walking in summer heat.

Black Tiger Spring is Jinan's second-largest spring by flow rate. Water spurts through three tiger heads carved in stone, and the wind rushing into the cavity produces a deep rumble that gave the site its name. Around it, the cluster includes 14 named springs including Pipa and Jiunv. Don't miss watching locals come to fill their jugs with spring water—a daily ritual in Jinan. Access is free and the site remains pleasant even late in the day.

To finish, climb Thousand Buddha Mountain south of the city. The trail reveals Buddhist caves carved during the Sui Dynasty (581-618), with hundreds of buddhas sculpted into the cliff face. At the summit, panoramic views over Jinan and, on clear days, Mount Tai to the south. Allow 1.5 hours for a leisurely ascent. Tip: prefer late afternoon when the slanting light illuminates the sculptures and the heat subsides.

activities.text: In the evening, dive into the Furong Street quarter. This pedestrian alley in old Jinan packs street food stalls, craft shops, and traditional buildings. Try jianbing (savory crepes), youxuan (fried onion flatbread), and soybean paste noodles—local specialties. Budget 50 yuan for a proper standing meal among the stalls.

If you arrive late morning, slip into the Shandong Museum (east side of the city). Here are preserved the Yinqueshan bamboo slips discovered in Linyi in 1972—the oldest known manuscripts of Sun Tzu's Art of War. An ideal appetizer for understanding Shandong's historical depth before heading south.

days_plan.itin_title: Shandong: 11 Days from Confucius to Mount Tai and the German Coast

visits.text: The English word *china* (porcelain) comes from here. Jingdezhen has been producing since the year 1004 and supplied the Ming and Qing imperial courts for six centuries. The *blue and white* you know as Delft, Meissen and other European copycats starts from this city. The technique of pure kaolin was invented on site. Every showcase in the world's great museums has a piece born in Jingdezhen.

Opened in 2020, the Imperial Kiln Museum designed by Studio Zhu-Pei lines up brick vaults inspired by ancient kilns. Remarkable architecture, collection of fragments found on the very site. Free entry, WeChat reservation mandatory, closed Mondays. Allow 1h30. Outer courtyard overlooks the antiques market.

At Taoyangli, you hear the potter's wheel from the entrance. The Ancient Kiln Folk Museum occupies a vast park where artisans work live. You can climb into a reconstructed Qing dragon kiln, buy a piece directly from the workshop. Ticket 53 yuan. Allow 3-4h. Demonstrations stop at 4 p.m., plan for early afternoon.

activities.text: Aim for the Taoxichuan market on Saturday or Sunday evening for contemporary ceramics, Tuesday and Friday for vintage pieces. Factory district converted to artist studios - the city's alternative quarter. If you want to bring back a real piece, it's here, not in airport shops. Allow 200-2000 yuan depending on the piece.

Book Imperial Kiln Museum entry on WeChat the day before, daily quota (free but limited). Passport scanned at entrance. English audio guide available, 30 yuan. Museum bookstore sells bilingual editions on Chinese porcelain history, interesting as intellectual souvenirs. Avoid Monday (closed).

days_plan.itin_title: Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi

visits.text: Jinghong's Tropical Botanical Garden covers 1,125 hectares and houses over 13,000 plant species. Founded in 1959, it's China's largest botanical garden. The giant fern greenhouse and the dipterocarp forest alone are worth 2 hours.

Wat Manfeilong (八角亭), 65 km from Jinghong, dates from the 13th century and remains one of Yunnan's best-preserved Theravada Buddhist sites. Nine white stupas surround a central stupa - the style is clearly Thai. Shoulders and knees covered required.

Jinghong's Mekong waterfront comes alive in the evening: strollers, Dai pétanque players, and grill vendors on the bank. Here you realize you're a few hundred kilometers from Laos - the Mekong flows directly toward Luang Prabang.

Return to Jinghong from Mengla (3-hour drive). Wat Pajie (勐泐大佛寺), rebuilt in 1997 on the foundations of a 14th-century Ming temple, dominates the city from a hill. The walk up takes 20 minutes and offers a view over the Mekong plain.
The Dai Ethnicity Museum in the ethnocultural park traces the history of the Möng Mao kingdom (9th-17th century) and presents ancient Dai scripts, similar to Khmer and Thai. Entry 50 RMB.

activities.text: Explore Zhuque Street night market where Dai vendors sell fried insects, palm larvae, and smoked duck with local spices. Even if you don't eat the insects, the atmosphere is that of a Southeast Asian border market.

Rent an electric bike (50 RMB/day) to ride along the Mekong to Manting Dai village, 3 km from downtown: coconut palms, golden temples, saffron-robed monks. The path follows the riverbank and is completely flat.
Dai Thai massage in Jinghong before the departure flight: several salons on Menglong Lu street offer massages with local herbs (lemongrass, wild ginger) for 100 to 150 RMB per hour.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Yunnan - beyond the classic circuit
Deep Yunnan - beyond the classic circuit

visits.text: Huanglong ("Yellow Dragon") is a 700 km² UNESCO site in the Min Mountains. Its main attraction: 3.6 km of yellow travertine terraces formed by calcium deposits from a hot spring, creating hundreds of tiered pools filled with turquoise, blue, and green water. The Five-Color Pool, at the summit at 3,576 meters, is the trail's high point—its waters shift hue with the angle of light.

Jiuzhaigou Valley ("Valley of Nine Villages") covers 720 km² in the Min Mountains, between 2,000 and 4,764 meters elevation. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1992, it takes its name from nine Tibetan villages. Its 114 lakes in turquoise, blue, green, and violet waters owe their colors to high calcium carbonate concentration and algae. Lake bottoms are visible at depths of several meters.

Five-Flower Lake, in the Rize Valley, is considered the most beautiful: its translucent water reveals a bottom of submerged logs between travertine beaches, in gradients of turquoise and emerald. Further up, Panda Lake owes its name to the giant pandas that once came to drink here—around 20 pandas still live within the park boundaries.
Nuorilang Waterfall, 270 meters wide, is China's widest travertine waterfall. Partially damaged by the magnitude 7 earthquake of August 2017, it was restored and the park reopened in 2019.

Long Lake (Chang Hai), in Zechawa Valley (left branch of the Y), is the largest, deepest (103 meters), and highest lake in Jiuzhaigou, at 3,150 meters. Stretching 7.5 km, surrounded by forested mountains, it has no outflow stream—water seeps through the karst bottom. Local legend speaks of a monster in its depths.

Five-Color Pond (Wucai Chi), despite its modest size, offers Jiuzhaigou's most colorful waters—blue, green, yellow, turquoise, and violet mingle according to depth, minerals, and algae. It's one of the smallest lakes but the most intense in color.

Mirror Lake (Jing Hai), in Shuzheng Valley, perfectly reflects the surrounding mountains and forests in its motionless waters. The Tibetan village of Shuzheng, one of the original nine villages, preserves traditional houses and prayer wheels.

Free morning in the town of Zhangzha, at the exit of Jiuzhaigou Valley. Tibetan shops offer turquoise jewelry, incense, thangkas, and ornate knives. The food market features plateau products: matsutake mushrooms, goji berries, wild honey, and dried yak cheese.

activities.text: Ascend on foot or by cable car to the summit of Huanglong (3,576 m), then descend on foot along the travertine terraces (3.6 km, roughly 3 hours). Oxygen bottles are sold at the entrance for visitors sensitive to altitude. The trail crosses conifer forest, waterfalls, and Taoist temples before reaching the pools.

Shuttle and walking route through Rize Valley (right branch of the Y): the bus climbs to the primeval forest at the valley's end, then the descent is on foot along wooden boardwalks that skirt the lakes. Allow 4 to 5 hours for the complete valley.

Route through Zechawa Valley and return via Shuzheng Valley (main trunk of the Y): Pearl Shoal's cascading waterfalls, Shuzheng Tibetan village, Rhinoceros Lake. Wooden boardwalks cross conifer forests and bamboo groves—habitat of the giant panda and Sichuan golden monkey.

Transfer to Jiuzhai-Huanglong Airport (1h30 drive, altitude 3,448 m) for a flight to Chengdu (45 min), Xi'an, or Beijing. The airport, perched on a mountain, offers a final panorama over the snow-capped peaks of the Min Mountains.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou
Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou
Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou
Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou

visits.text: Kaifeng served as the capital of the Northern Song dynasty (960–1127), a city of one million inhabitants—the largest in the world at the time. The Iron Pagoda of Youguo Temple, built in 1049 under Emperor Renzong, stands as its symbol. Octagonal, thirteen stories, 56.88 meters: it's not made of iron but glazed brown bricks that mimic rust from a distance. Over 1,600 sculptures of Buddhas, dancers, and flowers adorn its walls. It has survived 38 earthquakes and 6 Yellow River floods.

Millennium City Park (Qingming Riverside Landscape Garden) recreates life in 12th-century Kaifeng on a grand scale, based on the famous scroll painting "Along the River During the Qingming Festival" by Zhang Zeduan. It's highly theatrical: actors in Song costumes, horse races, cockfights, merchant streets, puppetry. For kids and enthusiasts of material culture alike, it's an excellent gateway.

Daxiangguo Temple, right in downtown Kaifeng, was founded in 555 and played a pivotal role in the development of Chinese Buddhism under the Song. Its Hall of a Thousand Arms houses a statue of Guanyin carved from a single ginkgo trunk, dated to the 18th century—truly spectacular.

Kaifeng's former Jewish quarter is one of the most surprising chapters in Chinese history. A Jewish community settled here around the 10th–11th century, likely arriving from Persia via the Silk Road. For 800 years, they kept the Torah, spoke Hebrew, and built a synagogue (destroyed in the 19th century). The current memorial, modest, displays a few steles and an exhibition. You need a local guide to truly understand—the site itself is poorly curated.

Longting Park (Dragon Pavilion), north of the city, occupies the site of the former Song imperial palace. The current pavilion dates to the Qing, but it evokes the vanished grandeur. In October–November, it's the heart of Kaifeng's Chrysanthemum Festival (held since the Song dynasty), with over 1,000 varieties on display.

Kaifeng Fu recreates the tribunal of Judge Bao Zheng, an upright 11th-century magistrate who became a folk hero (Bao the Righteous). The visit is highly theatrical, but it's a chance to discover the Song judicial system: prison, interrogation room, instruments. There's a trial reenactment every two hours—in Chinese, but comedic and expressive.

activities.text: In the evening, dive into the Drum Tower night market (Gulou). Kaifeng ranks among China's great culinary cities: try the Kaifeng-style xiaolongbao (juicy steamed dumplings), hu la tang (spicy morning soup), and sesame flatbreads with stuffing. It's lively until 11 pm, with a warm Chinese family atmosphere.

Rent a bike and circle the Kaifeng city walls (12 km), rebuilt under the Qing. The remarkable part: beneath your feet lie five successive layers of buried cities, including Song-era Bianliang, buried under Yellow River silt. At dusk, a 3D show is projected onto the walls.

For authentic street food, venture off the tourist path to Madao Street (马道街) or Shudian Street (书店街, Bookshop Street). Local Kaifeng residents eat xiaolongbao from Diyilou (the city's historic brand, founded in 1922) and drink iced chrysanthemum tea in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Spiritual Henan & Shaanxi – Tracing the Cradle of Chinese Civilization
Spiritual Henan & Shaanxi – Tracing the Cradle of Chinese Civilization

visits.text: Upper Langde Miao Village, 30 km from Kaili, is a hamlet of wooden stilted houses clinging to the mountainside. Visitors are greeted with a welcome ceremony: women in embroidered costumes offer rice wine in water buffalo horns. The village preserves songs, lusheng (bamboo mouth organ) dances, and traditional embroidery without compromising to mass tourism.

Matang Gejia Village, 19 km from Kaili, houses 400 members of an officially unrecognized Miao subgroup—they consider themselves China's "57th ethnic group." According to their legend, they descend from Hou Yi, the mythical archer who shot down nine suns. The women practice beeswax batik with unique geometric patterns.

The Qiandongnan Nationalities Museum, in central Kaili, presents costumes, silver jewelry, musical instruments, and agricultural tools of the Miao and Dong peoples across eleven halls. The silver ornament collection—crowns, breastplates, necklaces—illustrates the metal's importance in Miao culture: a complete bridal set can weigh over 10 kg.

activities.text: Batik workshop in Matang: artisans teach how to draw patterns on fabric using a stylus dipped in hot beeswax, before indigo dyeing. The complete process (drawing, soaking, rinsing, drying) takes about 2 hours.

days_plan.itin_title: Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian

visits.text: Stand in the rice paddies of Zili Village: before you, nine UNESCO-listed diaolou, the largest grouping of the four protected clusters. Built between 1917 and 1949 by wealthy Cantonese emigrants returning from North America and Southeast Asia, these fortified tower-houses blend Byzantine domes, Roman columns, Gothic gargoyles, and Chinese roofs. Peasants served as architectural models from postcards received by mail. An hour and a half on-site is enough for the magic.

The Ruishi Diaolou, behind Jinjiangli Village, is the tallest of the diaolou: nine stories built in 1921 by the Huang family, with a Roman dome and Byzantine roofline. Inside, original furniture remains in place, as if frozen in time. You climb a narrow staircase to the panoramic terrace from which the family surveyed the countryside—and fired, when necessary. Some windows still retain their machinegun crenellations.

Wander through Liyuan Garden, built between 1926 and 1936 by émigré Xie Weili upon returning from the United States. This isn't a diaolou strictly speaking but a residential complex blending Chinese-style pavilions, artificial lakes, and Italian Baroque ornamentation. European-imported furniture, Art Deco stained glass, and local wood carvings make it a striking witness to the era's cultural cross-pollination. Included in the 180-yuan combined ticket.

activities.text: In total, over 3,000 diaolou were built across the region between 1900 and 1940; roughly 1,833 still stand today in Kaiping's countryside. Rent an electric bike to link Zili, Majianglong, and Jinjiangli along the dikes between rice paddies. It's flat, shaded, and you'll discover dozens of abandoned towers—some still inhabited by the great-grandchildren of their builders. Three hours of riding to truly grasp the phenomenon's scale.

In the evening, rural Cantonese dinner in Kaiping: classic sweet-and-sour pork (not the tourist version), steamed fish with ginger and spring onions, Hakka stuffed tofu. Head to Chikan, the old market town 10 minutes by taxi, for dinner in a restored qilou arcade house. The atmosphere rivals the cuisine, and prices run far lower than in Guangzhou.

days_plan.itin_title: Subtropical Southern China in 12 Days: Canton, Hakka Diaspora & Hong Kong Peninsula

visits.text: Today you enter the sanctuary. Kanas Lake, at 1,374 m altitude and 188.5 m deep - China's deepest freshwater lake, formed 200,000 years ago by glaciers from Friendship Peak (4,374 m). The water changes color with the season and weather: turquoise in June, emerald in August, blue-gray in September. The Mongols call it 'God's palette'. First approach via Wolong Bay, where the Kanas River draws an omega loop around a dragon-shaped islet.

One kilometer upstream, Moon Bay forms a perfect crescent in the valley - this is the postcard image of Xinjiang. The color literally changes hour by hour with the light. Wooden walkways let you descend to the water. Avoid midday when the light is flat.

Then climb to Guanyu Pavilion, perched at 2,030 m on Camel Peak, accessible by 1,068 steps or electric shuttle. The viewpoint, built in 1987, overlooks the lake by 660 m. Panoramic view of Kanas's seven curves and, on clear days, the snow-capped Friendship Peak. Allow 2 hours for the ascent and photo stop.

Second day at Kanas, the good one - the photographers' day. Wake up before 6 AM for the morning mist over Moon Bay: a cotton cloud floats at water level, pierced by the first rays. A nearly daily phenomenon in June and September, rarer in midsummer.

In the afternoon, take the shuttle to Fish Watching Pavilion, different from Guanyu. This is where the Tuvas allegedly first spotted 'the monster' in 1980. Plunging view over the main bay, and walkways along the west shore to escape the crowds.

If the season is right (late September-early October), explore the northern park's birch forest. The trees yellow in barely two weeks, and the ground disappears under golden leaves. September is the absolute best month for Kanas.

activities.text: Embark on a 1-hour cruise on the lake from the main dock. Boats pass near the driftwood dam - a kilometer of logs accumulated by valley winds - and run along the birch and spruce forest. Local legend speaks of a lake monster spotted in 1980, nicknamed 'the Chinese Nessie'; fishermen say it's actually giant Hucho taimen, salmon that can reach two meters.

Late afternoon, walk along the Three Bays trail (Wolong, Moon, Fairy Bay) on 7 km of wooden walkways through the larch forest. Doable in 2 hours, little elevation gain, ideal for recovering from jet lag and altitude.

Hike in Heihu Bay valley, wilder than the tourist sections. 5 km round-trip trail through the taiga, with a chance to spot Siberian squirrels and, more rarely, elk. Ask your local guide if they'll take you - the area isn't marked.

Tuva dinner at a farm near the park: boiled lamb with marrow bones, dried yak cheese, buttered milk tea, and throat singing. A few families open their table to visitors by reservation through your accommodation.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Xinjiang - Kanas, Altai, and Ili Steppes in 16 Days
Northern Xinjiang - Kanas, Altai, and Ili Steppes in 16 Days

visits.text: En route to Kangding, stop at Luding to see the Luding Chain Bridge (泸定桥), built in 1706 by order of Emperor Kangxi. Its 13 iron chains stretched across the Dadu River gorges make it one of China's oldest suspension bridges. In 1935, Mao's troops crossed it under enemy fire during the Long March—an episode that founded the Red Army legend. Allow 30 minutes.

Kangding (康定) marks the frontier between lowland China and the Tibetan world of Kham. At 2,560m, it's your first night at altitude—your body begins to adapt. The town is wedged in a deep gorge crossed by two rivers. Lhamtso Ngachu Monastery, in the heights, dates to the 17th century and belongs to the Nyingma school. Climb up in late afternoon: the light on the mountains is exceptional.

Kangding's old town blends Tibetan and Han architecture in commercial alleyways where monks buy yak butter alongside tourists from Chengdu.
