Paoma Square (跑马山), at the foot of the mountain of the same name, is the town's heart—locals gather there evenings to dance in circles, a living Khampa tradition.

activities.text: Paoma Mountain (跑马山, 3,300m) is reached on foot or by cable car from downtown. Panoramic view over the city and, on clear days, of Gongga (7,556m)—Sichuan's highest peak. This is where your physical relationship with altitude truly begins. Walk slowly, drink plenty of water.
Dinner at a Tibetan restaurant in Kangding: order momo (yak meat steamed dumplings) and salted yak butter tea. It's rich, it's hot, it's plateau fuel. Might as well get used to it tonight.

days_plan.itin_title: From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days
Deep Western Sichuan - Kham, Aba, and Forgotten Valleys in 17 days

visits.text: Journey along the Karakoram Highway, one of the world's most spectacular mountain roads connecting China to Pakistan. The route climbs through dramatic gorges and high plateaus with views of 7,000-meter peaks.
Arrive at Lake Karakul, an alpine jewel at 3,600 meters reflecting the snow-capped peak of Muztagh Ata (7,546m) in its crystal waters. Kyrgyz herders graze yaks on the shores of this sacred lake.
Experience the high-altitude landscape where the Pamir, Karakoram, and Kunlun ranges converge. This 'roof of the world' offers some of Earth's most dramatic mountain scenery.

activities.text: Stay in a traditional Kyrgyz yurt by the lakeshore, experiencing nomadic hospitality with fresh yogurt, butter tea, and stories of life in these remote highlands.

days_plan.itin_title: The Silk Road Overland: Xi'an to Istanbul via Uzbekistan & Turkmenistan

visits.text: First major leg from Urumqi: 350 km straight north to Karamay, China's oil capital that sprang from the desert in the 1950s. You'll arrive in early afternoon and head straight to the Devil's City, 100 km northwest. The site, called Wuerhe Yardang, is a vast field of yardangs - rock formations sculpted by millennia of Dzungarian winds. The scenery is Martian, ochre, riddled with canyons. Budget at least 2.5 hours for your visit, and bring water: there's not a shred of shade.

On the way back to Karamay, stop at the Five-Colored Beach (Wucai Tan) along the Irtysh River. Sedimentary layers create red, ochre, green, and gray bands according to the geology. Short but photogenic - allow 45 minutes.

The Karamay Petroleum Park in town traces the history of the oil field discovered here in 1955 - China's first. You'll see restored original derricks and a didactic exhibition on the transformation of a desert corner into a city of half a million people. Not essential, but interesting if you arrive early.

activities.text: Sample Uyghur cuisine at Karamay's night market: charcoal-grilled lamb skewers with cumin, dapanji (big plate chicken with potatoes and hand-pulled noodles), naan bread fresh from the tandoor. On summer evenings, the atmosphere buzzes until midnight.

Sunset walk among the Wuerhe yardangs: the low-angle shadows reveal the rock formations' relief, and golden light transforms the desert into a movie set. Photographers set up around 7:30 PM in July-August.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Xinjiang - Kanas, Altai, and Ili Steppes in 16 Days

visits.text: Id Kah Mosque (Àitígǎěr Qīngzhēn Sì), in Kashgar's heart, is China's largest mosque. Founded in 1442, it can accommodate up to 20,000 worshippers in its courtyard and prayer hall. Its yellow facade flanked by two minarets faces the city's central square, the convergence point of social and commercial life. Friday prayer gathers thousands from across the region.

Kashgar's Old Town (Kāshí Gǔchéng), one of Central Asia's most ancient quarters, is a labyrinth of pisé alleyways, houses with carved wooden balconies, and interior courtyards. Artisans work copper, wood, and pottery in workshops opening onto the street. The potters' quarter, backed against a clay cliff to the northeast, has produced glazed jars and plates in Kashgar's distinctive style for centuries. The old town was partially restored but retains the topography and atmosphere of a Silk Road oasis city.

The Apak Hoja Mausoleum (Xiāngfēi Mù), 5 km northeast of downtown, is Xinjiang's most important Islamic funerary monument. Built in 1640, it houses the tombs of five generations of the Hoja family in a hall topped by a dome tiled in blue and white faience. Chinese legend associates it with the "Fragrant Concubine," a Uyghur princess who supposedly naturally exhaled a musky scent.

Kashgar's Sunday Grand Bazaar (Yèkèshànbā Bāzhā), direct heir to Silk Road markets, is one of Central Asia's largest open-air markets. Thousands of merchants from surrounding countryside and neighboring countries (Pakistan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan) sell carpets, silks, spices, knives, leather, and dried fruits. The livestock market on the outskirts sees sheep, goats, donkeys, horses, and yaks change hands—deals sealed with a handshake and tea.

activities.text: Watch local artisans at work in old town workshops—coppersmiths, knifemakers, and musical instrument makers maintaining skills passed through generations of Uyghur craftspeople.
Meet Uyghur families in surrounding villages.
Wander through commercial districts and observe the Han and Uyghur cultural blend.

Walk through old town artisan workshops: forging Uyghur knives (pìchàk) with bone or horn handles, pottery wheels in the potters' quarter, stringed instrument making (dutar, rawap). Artisans work at street level, allowing you to watch their hands at work. Late afternoon, sample polo (Uyghur lamb pilaf with carrots) and tea in a traditional teahouse.

days_plan.itin_title: The Silk Road Overland: Xi'an to Istanbul via Uzbekistan & Turkmenistan
China's Silk Road: Xi'an to Kashgar via the Hexi Corridor
China's Silk Road: Xi'an to Kashgar via the Hexi Corridor

visits.text: The Shilin Stone Forest rises 90 km south of Kunming: grey-black limestone pillars reaching 30 meters, sculpted by erosion over 270 million years. UNESCO listed it in 2007 as part of the South China Karst. Allow 3 hours for the Greater and Lesser Shilin. Leave early - tour buses arrive after 10 AM.

Yuantong Temple is Kunming's oldest Buddhist temple, founded under the Tang dynasty in the 8th century. Its central pond surrounded by pavilions is peaceful on weekdays. Since 1956, a Tibetan hall and a Theravada hall have coexisted here - a rare syncretism in China.
Dianchi Lake stretches across 330 km² south of Kunming. In winter (November-March), thousands of Siberian gulls overwinter here. The lakefront is accessible by bus or bicycle from downtown.
Arrival in Kunming by train from Jianshui (2h). Cuihu Park (Green Lake) downtown is perfect for the afternoon - in winter, Siberian gulls land here and Kunming residents sell them bread. Free entry.

The Yunnan Nationalities Village (云南民族村), on Dianchi Lake's shore, presents the province's 26 ethnic minorities in recreated pavilions. Useful for understanding differences between Dai, Hani, Naxi, and Yi before meeting them in real life in Xishuangbanna.

activities.text: Wander through Wenlin Jie, Kunming's student quarter: cafés run by Yi minorities, secondhand bookstores, restaurants serving Yunnan stir-fried rice with vegetables. Perfect for a first taste of local cuisine before heading out on the road.

Try clay pot rice with chicken (汽锅鸡, qiguoji) at the covered market in Jinma Biji Fang in the old town. This steam-cooked dish is a Kunming specialty - the chicken sits on a central clay cone that channels the steam. Count on 30 to 50 RMB.
Dinner around Jinbi Lu street: Yunnan grilled skewers (shaokao) - lamb, fermented tofu, corn - and a glass of Lancang beer before tomorrow morning's flight to Jinghong.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Yunnan - beyond the classic circuit
Deep Yunnan - beyond the classic circuit

visits.text: Major stage of this itinerary: the Kizil Buddhist caves, 60 km from Kuqa. China's oldest Buddhist complex - predating Dunhuang by several centuries - carved between the 3rd and 8th centuries in the Tocharian kingdom of Kucha. 339 known caves, 135 still in good condition, and 5,000 m² of preserved frescoes. UNESCO-listed in 2014. The style mixes Greco-Indian (Gandhara), Persian Sassanid, and Indian Buddhist influences - a unique syncretism on the Silk Road.

Tocharian Buddhism spoke an Indo-European language now extinct (Tocharian B), close to Celtic and Italic - an astonishing fact 6,000 km from Europe. The frescoes of the 'Painters' Pavilion' (cave 207, carbon-14 dated 478-536) show painters signing their work in Sanskrit, extremely rare in Buddhist art.

23 km northwest of Kuqa, the Subashi temple ruins testify to the same Buddhist golden age. Built in the 1st century, at its peak under the Tang (618-907), it housed up to 10,000 monks. The Chinese pilgrim Xuanzang, en route to India, stayed here for two months in 630. Today, mud-brick stupas and adobe walls stand in the desert. Melancholic atmosphere, few visitors.

activities.text: Kuqa's Uyghur market on Friday is one of Xinjiang's most traditional. You'll find embroidered hats (doppa), atlas silk fabrics, musical instruments (rawap, dutar), spices, dried fruits. The atmosphere is more authentic than Kashgar (more touristy). Go between 10 AM and noon.

Sample Kuqa polo (pilaf rice), reputed as one of Xinjiang's best: rice cooked in lamb broth with carrots, raisins, and fatty lamb pieces. Traditionally served by hand, but spoons available in tourist restaurants.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Xinjiang - Kanas, Altai, and Ili Steppes in 16 Days

visits.text: The village of Langmusi ("Tiger Temple" in Tibetan—a tiger cave once stood at the monastery site) is split in two by a river marking the border between Gansu and Sichuan. Each province has its own monastery, school, police station, and hospital. Tibetan Buddhists and Hui Muslims coexist in this high-altitude hamlet (3,300 m) with an alpine-village feel.

Kirti Monastery, on the Sichuan side, is the larger of the two: founded in 1413, expanded in 1748, it houses around 700 monks. Its particularity is preserving the intact body of the 5th Kirti Lama, displayed every 3 to 5 days. Behind the monastery, Namo Gorge marks the source of the Bailong (White Dragon) River.

Sertri Monastery, on the Gansu side, dates from 1748. Smaller but recognizable by its golden roofs, it overlooks the town from a hill. Above, a sky burial (jhator) site is still in use—bodies of the deceased are offered to vultures according to Tibetan Buddhist tradition. The place is sacred and visitors must not attend ceremonies.

Namo Gorge, at the back of Kirti Valley, shelters the source of the Bailong River in a cave lined with stalactites. The trail follows the river between moss-covered limestone walls in a primeval forest atmosphere.

activities.text: Hike through the red gorges behind Sertri Monastery. The trail climbs through wildflower meadows toward Red Mountain (Chongpoma), a red sandstone formation visible from the entire village. At the summit, prayer flags flutter facing snowy peaks. Allow 2 hours round trip.

Horseback riding through the grasslands around Langmusi. Local agencies offer outings from half a day to three days through alpine pastures, nomadic camps, and remote valleys. Tibetan horses, small and sure-footed, are accustomed to steep trails.
Meal at a Hui (Muslim) restaurant in Langmusi: hand-pulled noodles, grilled lamb skewers, flatbread. The Tibetan-Muslim coexistence gives the village a dual cuisine found nowhere else.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou
Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou

visits.text: The Gansu Provincial Museum, on the Yellow River's south bank, holds over 350,000 artifacts including the "Galloping Horse" (Mǎ Tà Fēiyàn), a bronze statuette from the Eastern Han dynasty (25-220 AD) depicting a horse at full gallop balanced on a flying swallow—now the emblem of Chinese tourism. The Silk Road galleries display silks, Roman coins, and Buddhist objects found along the Hexi Corridor.

The Zhongshan Bridge (Zhōngshān Qiáo), the first permanent bridge built across the Yellow River, was erected in 1907 with materials imported from Germany and assembled by American engineers. Before its construction, people crossed the Yellow River on rafts made from inflated sheepskins (yángpí fázi), a method used for centuries. From the bridge, climb to White Pagoda Park (Báitǎ Shān Gōngyuán), built during the Yuan dynasty (13th century) to honor a Tibetan lama who died in Lanzhou en route to Genghis Khan's court.

activities.text: Sample Lanzhou beef noodle soup (Lánzhōu niúròu miàn), considered China's most widespread street food—an estimated 100,000+ restaurants nationwide serve it. The clear spiced broth, hand-pulled noodles made before your eyes, sliced beef, cilantro, and chili oil form the traditional recipe's "five colors."

days_plan.itin_title: China's Silk Road: Xi'an to Kashgar via the Hexi Corridor

visits.text: The Chengdu-Lhasa flight takes 2 hours 15 minutes and lands at Gonggar Airport, 60km from the city. Don't visit anything on arrival day. Lhasa is at 3,650m—after Litang and Yading, your body is better prepared than a direct traveler, but flight fatigue and pressure change demand rest. After transfer to the hotel, a short walk in the Barkhor neighborhood does no harm.

Barkhor Street (八廓街) is the circumambulation route around Jokhang Temple—pilgrims have turned clockwise for centuries. Vendors offer prayer beads, thangkas, and yak butter under canvas awnings. It's lively, dense, real. The first evening in Lhasa is spent here, simply, hands in pockets.

The Potala Palace (布达拉宫) rises on the Red Hill at 3,700m, 13 stories for 110 meters height, 1,000 rooms. Begun in 1645 by the 5th Dalai Lama, Lobsang Gyatso, it took 43 years to complete—and this Dalai Lama died before the end, his death concealed for 12 years to avoid interrupting construction. It was the seat of Tibetan government until the 14th Dalai Lama's flight to India in 1959. The Red Palace houses the golden funerary stupas of eight Dalai Lamas. Tickets must be booked online with real registration—no tickets on-site.

Jokhang Temple (大昭寺) is Tibet's spiritual heart. Founded in 639 by King Songtsen Gampo to house the Jowo statue—a golden Buddha brought by his Nepalese wife Bhrikuti—it rises on 4 levels. Its historic core, dendrochronologically dated to the 7th century, is the only surviving ancient Indian wooden architecture in the entire world. It houses 800 metal sculptures and thousands of thangkas. Photography prohibited inside.

Drepung Monastery (哲蚌寺) was, before 1959, the world's largest monastery with 10,000 monks. Founded in 1416, it perches 5km west of Lhasa on a white hill. Only a few hundred monks remain today, but the main assembly hall, thangka chapels, and interior alleyways are intact. In the morning, monks debate in the courtyards.

Sera Monastery (色拉寺) is famous for its monk debates, held each afternoon (except Sunday) in the inner courtyards between 3-5 PM. Monks clash by clapping hands, pointing fingers, laughing—it's a Buddhist dialectic learning method codified for centuries. Sera was founded in 1419 and housed 5,000 monks before 1959.

Norbulingka (罗布林卡), the Dalai Lama's former summer palace, is a 36-hectare park west of the city. Built in the 18th century by the 7th Dalai Lama, it's been a UNESCO World Heritage extension of the Potala since 2001. It's less visited than Jokhang—local Tibetans come to picnic with family under the willows. A haven of calm.

Chakpori Hill (药王山), facing the Potala from across the main avenue, is the best viewpoint of the Potala Palace—no queue, no ticket, no crowd. Climb on foot in 20 minutes. Dusk over the Potala from this hill is the photo everyone seeks.

Return to Lhasa in late afternoon. Last evening walk at Barkhor—you see it differently after experiencing Rongbuk. The pilgrims circling Jokhang with their rosaries sometimes come from regions even more remote than where you went. Sit on a stupa's steps and observe.

activities.text: Rest—that's Day 10's main activity in Lhasa. Hydrate, sleep early, don't push. Tourists who ignore this simple rule find themselves bedridden on Day 11 with severe altitude sickness. Your body needs 12-24 hours to recalibrate its hemoglobin production.

Walk the Barkhor kora at sunrise (from 6 AM): pilgrims circle Jokhang with their prayer wheels, rosaries, full prostrations on the flagstones. The morning's grazing sunlight on the golden roofs, incense smoke, and mantra hum compose a scene that midday tourists won't know.

Dinner at a Tibetan restaurant in the Barkhor quarter: thukpa (yak noodle soup), yak meat momo, chang (fermented barley beer). Some good addresses in the alleyways north of Jokhang, recognizable by red banners and kitchen sounds.

Explore the old Tibetan town on foot or by rental bike: the Shöl and Ramoche neighborhoods, north of Jokhang, still have white-facade houses painted with typical black and red motifs. It's what remains of pre-1950 Lhasa, amid modern buildings.

Travel shopping in shops around Barkhor: sutra scrolls, copper prayer wheels, carved mani stones, singing bowls. Always negotiate—displayed prices are for tourists. Shops run by Tibetans are generally more authentic than big Chinese stores.
Last meal in Lhasa and luggage check. Gonggar Airport is 60km from the center—allow minimum 1.5 hours. Some flights depart at dawn.

days_plan.itin_title: From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days
From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days
From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days
From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days

visits.text: Break the Yichang-Enshi route with a stop in Lichuan, a staging town in western Hubei at 1,100 m altitude. Visit Tenglong Cave ("flying dragon"), one of China's longest limestone caves - 60 km of mapped galleries. You descend 800 m of lit walkways, cross an underground river by boat. Entry 180 yuan, budget 2h. Exit above a waterfall gushing straight from the cliff.

Make a detour to Suofeng Stone Forest, 30 minutes from Lichuan. You'll find 20-30 m karst needles sculpted by erosion, among bamboo forests. Short trail (1h loop), quiet on weekdays, ideal for stretching before the final section to Enshi. Entry 60 yuan.

activities.text: Before leaving, taste Lichuan's specialty: ge xian mi, sweet potato noodle dish served in spicy soup with bamboo shoots and smoked pork. It's a high-altitude dish, hearty, perfect after a day on the road. Budget 30 yuan per portion at any small downtown restaurant.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Hubei & Hunan - 14 days through Wudang, Shennongjia and Miao valleys

places.name: Linyi

visits.text: Short drive to Zoucheng, 23 km south, birthplace of Mencius (Mengzi, 372-289 BC), Confucius's philosophical successor. The Meng Temple and Meng Cemetery form a complex far less crowded than Qufu's—you may be alone here. A few courtyards, stelae, thousand-year-old cypresses: the contemplative atmosphere suits the philosopher who defended the natural goodness of man.

Afternoon, drive to Linyi (about 2.5 hours, roughly 200 km). Upon arrival, rush to the Yinqueshan Bamboo Slips Museum. Here in April 1972, construction workers accidentally unearthed two Han tombs. Verdict: 4,942 bamboo slips containing the oldest known manuscripts of Sun Tzu's Art of War (13 chapters) and the 16 chapters of Sun Bin's Art of War, lost for 1,400 years. A discovery ranked among the 100 greatest of the 20th century in China.

If you still have energy, visit the former residence of Wang Xizhi (303-361), considered the greatest calligrapher in Chinese history—he wrote the Preface to the Orchid Pavilion, the absolute model of cursive calligraphy. Born in Linyi, he spent his childhood here. The site is more modest than one might imagine but charges the air with a certain emotion.

activities.text: Dine at a small eatery in Linyi's pedestrian quarter. The local specialty is sujian bing (crispy fried flatbread) accompanied by chuanxiang dumplings. Budget 30-40 yuan for a decent meal.

days_plan.itin_title: Shandong: 11 Days from Confucius to Mount Tai and the German Coast

map_keywords: Yinqueshan Han Tombs Bamboo Slips Museum Linyi

visits.text: On the way, stop in Yajiang, clinging to the cliffs of the Yalong valley at 2,600m. You'll have lunch here, fill up on gas, snap a photo of the bridge over the river. Just after, you'll cross Gaoershi Pass (4,412m) then Jianziwankou Pass (4,659m)—prayer flags, 360° views on clear days. Photo stop 15 min max; the altitude hits hard.

Litang welcomes you at 4,014m elevation—one of the world's highest towns. Short of breath, you quickly understand why. The town, bordered by infinite grassland, is the birthplace of several Dalai Lamas, including the 7th and 10th. Sleep early tonight—no alcohol, lots of water, and acclimatize gently.

Step through the gate of Litang Monastery, founded in 1580 by the 3rd Dalai Lama—you enter one of Kham's largest Gelugpa sanctuaries. You'll encounter 1,500 monks who still live here. Red and white buildings sprawl across the hillside overlooking town. At 2pm, watch philosophical debates in the courtyard: hand clapping and chanted voices—it's riveting.

Walk Litang's old town, under restoration, where you'll discover a few authentic alleys south of the monastery. Tibetan houses with painted facades, small family temples, merchants of turquoise and coral. The Khampa of Litang are renowned for their height and long hair tied with red threads—you'll understand when you cross paths.

Head back north—Daocheng to Litang, 200 km, 4 hours. You'll cross Tuoba Pass (4,685m) then traverse the Haizi Shan plateau, dotted with turquoise glacial lakes and over 1,000 Tibetan "sacred lakes" between 4,000 and 4,500m. Mandatory photo stop at the main viewpoint—lunar landscape, yaks in the distance. Count on 30 min on-site; it blows hard.

You return to Litang late afternoon for an overnight stop. This time, settle on the grassland south of town: at 4,014m, the sky here is clearer than anywhere else. At night, with no light pollution, the Milky Way crashes down on you. Refuel with a bowl of hot thukpa (Tibetan noodle soup) before sleeping early.

activities.text: If your visit falls the first week of August, you'll catch the Litang Horse Festival—a week of races, acrobatic equestrian demonstrations (standing on a galloping horse, archery at full gallop), Khampa songs, collective prayers. Tens of thousands of pilgrims. Book your accommodation two months ahead; it's Kham's major event.

Taste salted butter tea in a real nomad's tent on the grassland. Five km from town, herders welcome you in season. 30 yuan for tea and a bit of tsampa, more if you want fresh yak yogurt. The black yak-hair tent, the stove in the center, the smell of smoke and rancid butter—you're in the heart of Kham.

Wander the market alleys of Litang in the morning. Khampa nomads from surrounding villages—women with coral and turquoise-adorned braids, men in burgundy chubas with their large knives at the waist—come here to trade. Don't pull out your camera immediately: watch first, earn their gaze, then ask.

Walk slowly through town—at 4,014m, even climbing 50 steps leaves you breathless. This is your first real night at very high altitude: rest this afternoon, hydrate abundantly, don't force it. Your body is producing extra red blood cells—give it time.

If you're there in early August, the Litang Horse Races are among the Tibetan plateau's biggest: Khampa riders gallop full speed while picking up silk scarves from the ground. Outside this date, the festival vibe isn't there—but the town's soul is always palpable.

Preparation for departure to Daocheng: check the condition of the G227 road with your driver. After June rains, some sections are cut by landslides. The Litang-Daocheng journey takes 3-4 hours in the best conditions.

days_plan.itin_title: From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days
From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days
Deep Western Sichuan - Kham, Aba, and Forgotten Valleys in 17 days
Deep Western Sichuan - Kham, Aba, and Forgotten Valleys in 17 days
Three Parallel Rivers & Eastern Tibet - 14-Day Yunnan to Kham Extension

visits.text: The Ping'an rice terraces in the Longji massif (Dragon's Backbone), about 80 km from Guilin, spread thousands of stepped plots between 600 and 800 meters altitude. Construction of these terraces began under the Yuan dynasty (1271–1368) and concluded in the early Qing dynasty (1644–1911)—over 400 years of work by Zhuang communities. The "Nine Dragons and Five Tigers" viewpoint shows nine ridges undulating like dragons bending to drink from the Jinsha River, while five tiger-shaped rocks frame them. The second panorama, "Seven Stars Surrounding the Moon," shows seven small hills encircling a crescent-shaped rice paddy.

Ping'an Zhuang village was the first Longji terraces site opened to visitors. Its traditional wooden stilt houses, the oldest dating from the Qing dynasty, cling to the slope between terraces. The Zhuang, one of China's 55 ethnic minority groups, perpetuate their architecture, textile crafts, and mountain cuisine—taste rice cooked in bamboo tubes (zhútǒng fàn), fragrant and smoky.

The Jinkeng Dazhai terraces, Longji's most extensive, cover 40 to 50 km² between 300 and 1,100 meters altitude. Dazhai village is inhabited by Red Yao people, recognizable by their spectacular hairstyles—women cut their hair only once in a lifetime and coil it into buns that can exceed a meter long. Three viewpoints mark the ascent: viewpoint #2, "Thousand Layers to Heaven" (Qiāncéng Tiāntī), closest to the village, shows terraces stacked like a giant stairway; #1, "Music of West Mountain" (Xīshān Sháolè), at 1,180 meters, is the highest and offers the best sunrise view; #3, "Golden Buddha Peak" (Jīnfó Dǐng), is accessible by cable car from Dazhai's parking area.

Huangluo Yao village, below the Dazhai terraces, is listed in the Guinness Book of Records for the highest concentration of long-haired women. The Red Yao perpetuate the tradition of the "Clothes Drying Festival" (Shài Yījiē), an annual celebration where brightly embroidered costumes are displayed on wooden balconies. You'll discover embroidery techniques, indigo dyeing, and local hot pepper sauce making.

activities.text: Hike from Ping'an to the old Zhuang village of Longji (Gǔzhuāng) via a flagstone trail descending through the terraces. Allow 40 minutes to 1h30 depending on pace. The ancient village preserves the region's best-kept stilt houses, some over 150 years old. The return can be made on the same path or by shuttle.

Hike from Dazhai to Ping'an crossing through Zhongliu village—a 4 to 5 hour route through the most remote and least-visited terraces. The trail alternates between ridges and valleys, with changing views over the rice fields at each pass. Bring good shoes—the path is narrow and slippery in wet weather.

days_plan.itin_title: Karst Heartlands: Guilin, Yangshuo & Longji Rice Terraces
Karst Heartlands: Guilin, Yangshuo & Longji Rice Terraces

places.name: Longsheng

visits.text: Drive from Fenghuang to Longsheng (~5h). Longji rice terraces ('Dragon's Backbone'), 100 km north of Guilin. Sculpted by the Zhuang and Yao starting from the Yuan dynasty (13th century), 650 years of labor. 66 km², altitudes from 300 to 1,100 m. In spring the flooded terraces reflect the sky; in autumn, they turn golden.
Zhuang village of Ping'an: stilt houses, 'Nine Dragons and Five Tigers' and 'Seven Stars Around the Moon' viewpoints.

activities.text: Hike between Ping'an viewpoints (~1h30) through terraces and villages. At Huangluo village, encounter with Yao women whose hair exceeds 1.80 m in length.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie

map_keywords: Nine Dragons Five Tigers Viewpoint Longsheng

visits.text: The Xi'an–Luoyang train takes just 1.5 hours and drops you at Luoyang Longmen, ten minutes from the grottoes. Head straight there in the morning: 2,345 niches carved into the limestone cliffs along the Yi River over a 1 km stretch, with nearly 110,000 Buddhist statues sculpted between 493 and the mid-8th century, inscribed as a UNESCO site in 2000. This is China's most breathtaking stone sculpture ensemble, rivaling Mogao and Yungang.

The Fengxian Temple, on the west bank, is the crown jewel of the site. The central statue of Vairocana Buddha stands 17.14 meters tall, carved between 672 and 675 during the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, who is said to have lent her own features to the face. The heads of secondary statues were decapitated in the 1920s–1930s by Western collectors or during the Cultural Revolution. Many now reside at the Met in New York or the Atkinson Museum in Kansas City.

Cross the bridge to the east bank, far less crowded. The Xiangshan Temple, rebuilt in 1707 under the Qing, and the garden of Bai Juyi (a Tang poet who spent his final days in Luoyang) offer panoramic views of the western cliffs. If you can, stay for the evening visit: the site reopens from 7 pm to 11 pm April–October, separate ticket at 90 yuan, far fewer people, and the lighting brings the Buddha to life.

According to tradition, the White Horse Temple is China's first Buddhist temple, founded in 68 AD under Emperor Ming of the Eastern Han, 12 km east of Luoyang. The emperor is said to have dreamed of a golden being and sent emissaries to India; they returned with two monks—Kasyapa Matanga and Dharmaratna—and sutras carried on a white horse, hence the name. The current buildings date mainly from the Ming and Qing, but the aura remains intact.

Southeast of the temple, Qiyun Pagoda is one of China's oldest brick pagodas. Rebuilt in 1175 during the Jin dynasty, it rises 35 meters. Clap your hands at the south side of the pagoda: the echo sounds uncannily like croaking frogs—an unexplained acoustic phenomenon that has delighted visitors for centuries.

The Luoyang Museum, opened in its new building in 2011, showcases one of the country's finest collections of Shang and Zhou bronzes, along with Tang tricolor (sancai) ceramics emblematic of the city. Plan on two unhurried hours—it's free with your passport.

Guanlin Temple, south of the city, houses the presumed tomb of General Guan Yu's head. A hero of the Three Kingdoms era (3rd century), he was deified as Guandi. This is one of the three major Guan Yu worship sites in China, full of soldiers and police officers coming to pray before his statue.

activities.text: In the evening, stroll through Luoyang's old town (Laocheng), around Lijing Men Street. It's less touristy than Xi'an, more authentic, and you can sample the famous Luoyang Shuixi (the twenty-four water-based dishes), an imperial cuisine descended from the Tang court. The illuminated Lijing Men gate is stunning after dark.

If you're here between April 5 and 25, don't miss the Peony Festival—the flower has been Luoyang's emblem since the Tang dynasty. Wangcheng Park (National Peony Garden) showcases over 1,200 varieties. It's become a national event, so plan extra time for the crowds.

Try a full Luoyang Shuixi meal at a traditional restaurant like Zhen Bu Tong on Zhongzhou Road. Twenty-four successive water- or broth-based dishes derived from Tang imperial cuisine: it's copious, fascinating, and best enjoyed with a group. Budget 150–300 yuan per person.

days_plan.itin_title: Spiritual Henan & Shaanxi – Tracing the Cradle of Chinese Civilization
Spiritual Henan & Shaanxi – Tracing the Cradle of Chinese Civilization

visits.text: A local proverb says: 'You don't know Mount Lu's true face, because you're inside it' (Su Shi, 11th c.). Mist rises so quickly at Lushan that contours disappear. The mountain, UNESCO-listed in 1996, is also a political place: altitude station developed by British missionaries in the 19th century, became summer residence for Chiang Kai-shek then Mao.

Meilu Villa housed Chiang Kai-shek then Mao. Small, modest, heavy with history - it was here that the 1959 Lushan Conference took place, where Peng Dehuai dared criticize the Great Leap Forward and was purged. Period furniture is in place. Entry 10 yuan. Allow 45 min. Panels in Chinese, phone translation via Google Translate useful.

Five rocky peaks lined up above Poyang Lake: the Wulaofeng, or Five Old Heads. Clear view on clear days, thick fog otherwise. Sandiequan Falls tumbles in three stages, celebrated by Li Bai in the 8th century. Allow 2h round trip on foot. Well-marked trails, sustained elevation gain, closed-toe shoes recommended.

activities.text: If you come in November, check high-altitude trail conditions before departure: frost can close Wulaofeng and Sandiequan. Guling tourist office (the summit village) posts info in Chinese; ask your hotel to verify. In case of closure, stick to Meilu Villa and the village, which are worth the detour alone.

Allow two hours round trip to Sandiequan Falls from the main trail. Good shoes, water, altitude rises to 1474 m. Stay in Guling for sunrise if you want magical light on the sea of mist - Lushan Hotel guesthouse stays open year-round. Otherwise, sleeping down in Jiujiang is more comfortable.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi

visits.text: Markham (Mangkang in Chinese) is the official crossroads between the G214 (Yunnan-Tibet) and G318 (Sichuan-Tibet) highways. At 4,371 m altitude, you'll find a modest town: local market, monastery, and registration of your permit to continue through the TAR. Without a permit, you'd have been stopped at Yanjing—here they simply confirm your passage.

Stop at Hongla Pass, 4,376 m—here you cross from the Mekong valley to the Yangtze (Jinsha Jiang) valley. From there, the G318 plunges toward Batang over 110 km. Prayer flags, stupas, 360° mountain views. You'll understand then that you're truly on the roof of the world.

Slip into Markham's Gelugpa monastery on the town outskirts—it hosts around twenty monks. Small, unassuming, but you'll attend morning chants at 6 AM without a single tourist. Ask your driver to drop you there the evening before.

activities.text: Eat Tibetan at a Markham canteen: thukpa (yak noodle soup), steamed momos, butter tea. Budget 30-40 yuan per person. It's rustic but filling after the drive.

Turn in early tonight: altitude is starting to wear you down (4,371 m), drink 3 liters of water throughout the day, absolutely avoid alcohol, and prep for a full driving day tomorrow to Batang (170 km, 4-5 hours on the G318).

days_plan.itin_title: Three Parallel Rivers & Eastern Tibet - 14-Day Yunnan to Kham Extension

visits.text: Begin your day at the Hakka Museum in Meizhou, housed in a replica tulou (traditional round house) inaugurated in 2008. The museum traces 1,700 years of migrations from the Yellow River plains and gives you the keys to everything that follows: over 80 million Hakka live worldwide, many having passed through this city. Statues of famous Hakka: Sun Yat-sen, Lee Kuan Yew, Deng Xiaoping (Hakka mother).

Drive 30 km south of Meizhou to Yanyang Lou, a traditional Hakka horseshoe-shaped house (weilong) built around 1730 by the Luo family. You immediately see Hakka social organization rendered as architecture: communal rooms at center, bedrooms at the periphery, ancestral altar in the dominant position. The building housed up to 200 people from a single clan. An hour and a half on-site, 50 yuan ticket.

Push on to Songkou, an old Hakka market town on the banks of the Mei River built during the Ming. Its Confucian academy (Yuanchang Lou), where young Hakka studied the classics before imperial examinations, has been restored and is open to visitors. Cobbled lanes and gray-brick houses recall a prosperous 19th-century rural Hakka. Quiet, lightly touristed, perfect for understanding the diaspora's other face.

activities.text: Taste Hakka cuisine: yong tau foo (tofu stuffed with ground meat), salt-baked chicken cooked in a thick salt crust for 90 minutes, braised pork with pickled mustard greens (mei cai kou rou), bouncy fish balls. Hakka cuisine, shaped by centuries of migration, relies on preservation: salting, drying, fermentation. Mei Xian Wei restaurant among locals, roughly 60-100 yuan per person.

Participate in a traditional Hakka tea ceremony, lei cha (pounded tea): green tea blended with herbs, peanuts, and seeds pounded in a mortar, served as a savory porridge—one of China's rarest tea preparations. Several teahouses in Meizhou offer the experience for 50-80 yuan. It's disconcerting but authentic, and practiced since the Han dynasty by Hakka ancestors.

days_plan.itin_title: Subtropical Southern China in 12 Days: Canton, Hakka Diaspora & Hong Kong Peninsula

visits.text: The Jingmai Mountains harbor the world's largest ancient tea tree forest: 2,800 hectares of tea trees planted over a thousand years ago by the Bulang and Dai peoples. UNESCO inscribed this landscape in 2023. These tea trees grow in the shade of other trees - the agroforestry protects the soil and complexifies the tea's aromas.

The Bulang village of Manpo is partially listed to preserve its bamboo and teak architecture. Bulang women still wear traditional black embroidered clothing during festivals. Outside harvest season (March-May), access is quiet.
Menghai weekly market gathers Dai, Hani, Lahu, and Akha vendors every Sunday: pu'er tea cakes, rare spices, and medicinal herbs unavailable in Kunming. Arrive before 8 AM.

activities.text: Join a pu'er tasting at a Jingmai producer: the comparison between a young sheng and a 10-year sheng is spectacular. Some also offer manual picking in March-May. Count on 150 to 300 RMB for a serious session.
Hike through the ancient tea forest at dusk: light filters between 8 to 10-meter trees. Local guides know the reputed tea trees over 800 years old - some bear tracking numbers.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Yunnan - beyond the classic circuit

places.name: Menghan

visits.text: Menghan (Ganlanba, 'olive plain' in Dai) is a Dai village on a Mekong meander 50 km from Jinghong. The Wat Luang in Ban Suan Men village is decorated with murals depicting the Ramayana in Dai version. The welcome is simple and warm.
Ganlanba Tropical Rainforest Park, along the Mekong, is a 400-hectare secondary forest: wild banana trees, wine palms, teak. Signs are in Chinese and Dai - plan for a guide.

If you visit in April during Dai Songkran (water festival, April 13-15), Menghan is one of the best places to experience it: the festival lasts 3 days and pirogues parade on the Mekong. Off-season, Dai dance shows are organized for groups.

activities.text: Cross the Mekong by motorized boat to reach villages on the opposite bank, inaccessible by car - count on 20 RMB round-trip. Residents still cultivate sticky rice and tobacco under conditions close to those of 50 years ago.
Lunch in a Dai stilt house: sticky rice cooked in banana leaf, pork larb, bamboo shoot soup. Xishuangbanna Dai cuisine is one of the closest to northern Thai cooking - sour, herbal, not sweet.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Yunnan - beyond the classic circuit

map_keywords: Dai Village Ban Suan Men Menghan

visits.text: Wangtianshu Park is the only place in China where Parashorea chinensis grows - a tree whose canopy reaches 70 meters. A 2.5 km suspended walkway runs 30 meters high between the crowns. In the morning, you're in the clouds.

Nabanhe Nature Reserve protects a tropical forest corridor between Yunnan and Laos. White-cheeked gibbons are observed at daybreak, Asian elephants, and over 500 bird species. 4×4 access only - local guides offer outings departing from Mengla.

The Mohan border crossing, 25 km from Mengla, is the northern terminus of the China-Laos train inaugurated in December 2021. If continuing to Luang Prabang, a Lao visa on arrival is available at the post.

activities.text: Dawn bird outing in Nabanhe Reserve: the great hornbill (Buceros bicornis), Asia's largest hornbill with its yellow and red casque, is present in the area. Rent binoculars in Mengla (30 RMB/day) and go with a local ornithologist guide.

Dinner in Mengla at an Akha restaurant: smoked dried pork, wild vegetables gathered from the forest, and artisanal rice wine. Akha cuisine is less known than Dai but worth the detour - look for restaurants frequented by locals.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Yunnan - beyond the classic circuit

visits.text: Welcome to Hailuogou ("Conch Gully"), at the foot of Mount Gongga (7,556m), Sichuan's highest. You'll discover Hailuogou Glacier, 14.7 km long, descending to 2,850m—the lowest modern glacier in East Asia. Unique phenomenon: it plunges into primary subtropical forest. You stay in Moxi (1,600m), park entrance, brutal descent after so much altitude.

Enter the park at 8am, take the mandatory shuttle (70 yuan) to Camp 3. You'll follow a 30-min trail to the great icefall: 1,080m high, 1,100m wide—China's highest icefall. The thunder of avalanches echoes several times daily. At 2,960m, you see 45 peaks over 6,000m surrounding you. Stay cautious near edges; local guide mandatory to venture onto the ice.

Ride the cable car (3,500m long) to Camp 4 at 3,600m, above the glacier. If weather cooperates, you see the golden summit of Gongga through the cabin's glass windows. Panoramic observation platform, red rock bank (caused by algae trentepohlia jolithus), cable car return. Count on 2 hours on-site. Freezing cold, bring fleece jacket.

activities.text: Soak in Hailuogou's hot springs that evening, right facing Mount Gongga. A world-unique phenomenon: springs up to 80°C gushing at the glacier's foot. Several hotels exploit these waters (Gongga Shentang Hot Spring Hotel, 5-star, 15 min from entrance). 100-200 yuan per session. Soaking in hot water with glacier views—surreal moment.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Western Sichuan - Kham, Aba, and Forgotten Valleys in 17 days

visits.text: Built in 653, destroyed and rebuilt 29 times, today's Tengwang Pavilion overlooking the Gan River dates from 1989, on a Song model. Poet Wang Bo composed his *Preface to the Pavilion of Prince Teng* there in 675, a text every Chinese schoolchild learns by heart. Ticket 50 yuan, climb to the top floor. River view, especially at dusk.

Nanchang lacks the charm of southern old towns: it's a gray, functional metropolis, marked by militant communism. But Bayi Square (八一, eight-one) commemorates August 1, 1927, day of the uprising that marks the birth of the People's Liberation Army. To understand state mythology, it's the useful stop. Vast square, statue, vertical monument.

Bayi, pronounced 'eight-one', thus commemorates the 1927 uprising; the adjoining museum retraces the episode (free, closed Mondays). One hour suffices to grasp the official angle. Explanations are in Chinese with partial English, period photos, weapons, letters. Exit toward Renmin Square side, nearby restaurants for lunch.

activities.text: Go up to the pavilion at dusk: warm light on the curved roofs and river compensates for the modern reconstruction. Avoid weekends after 5 p.m., 45-min queue. On weekdays, allow 15 min. Audio guide in Chinese only, but panels give the essentials in English. Floating restaurants on the Gan River below for dinner.

Sesame oil and rice wine form the base of *three-cup chicken* (三杯鸡, sanbei ji), Jiangxi's signature dish. Try it in the Honggutan district, chosen for your night. Gan cuisine is milder than Hunan but still spicy - stick with 微辣. Allow 60-90 yuan per person, decent local restaurant.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi

places.name: Nanjing

visits.text: The Nanjing Massacre Memorial (侵华日军南京大屠杀遇难同胞纪念馆) is a difficult but essential stop for understanding contemporary China. On December 13, 1937, the Japanese army took the city; for six weeks, official Chinese estimates count 300,000 dead. Built in 1985 on the site of the Jiangdongmen mass grave, the memorial displays 208 bones exhumed in situ and over 1,000 objects. Six million visitors per year.

Ming Xiaoling Tomb (明孝陵), UNESCO-listed in 2003, is the burial site of Zhu Yuanzhang, founder of the Ming Dynasty (reign 1368-1398). The Spirit Way is one of China's most impressive: 800 meters lined with 24 monumental stone statues - elephants, lions, camels, tigers. These guardians have been watching for over 600 years.

Sun Yat-sen Mausoleum (中山陵) imposes with its scale: 392 steps from the entrance arch to the tomb at the summit of Purple Mountain. Built between 1926 and 1929, it mixes Beaux-Arts architecture and nationalist symbolism. Sun Yat-sen (1866-1925), founder of the Republic of China, rests here.

Nanjing's Ming city walls are the world's best-preserved: 35.3 km on the original layout, with 25 km still standing. Built between 1366 and 1393, their bricks bear the kiln's name and the foreman's - a unique quality control measure. The section around Zhonghua Gate is the most accessible for walking the ramparts.

The Confucius Temple area (夫子庙, Fuzi Miao) runs along the Qinhuai River - the former imperial examination district where thousands of scholar candidates came to try their luck for centuries. The evening atmosphere with red lanterns and boat rides on the Qinhuai is genuinely pleasant.

Xuanwu Lake (玄武湖), at the foot of the northern Ming walls, is a 4.73 km² park in the heart of the city. Its five islands connected by causeways served as training grounds for the Ming imperial fleet. Pleasant walk away from tourist bustle.

activities.text: Stroll through Zhongshan Park (紫金山) between the mausoleum and Ming tomb - forest of plane trees and cypresses, peaceful trails, artificial lake. One of China's finest urban walks, 5 km from downtown.

Dine in the Xinjiekou district. Nanjing specialties: ya xue fen si (vermicelli with duck blood), pan mian (crab stir-fried noodles), salted duck - Nanjing's salted duck has been a national institution since the Ming Dynasty.
Climb onto the Ming wall ramparts in the evening from Zhonghua Gate - the night view over the city and illuminated defenses is striking. The section toward the Qinhuai River takes 45 minutes on foot.

Take an evening boat on the Qinhuai River - the Confucius Temple quarter viewed from the water, with its lanterns and reflected pavilions, is Nanjing's nocturnal postcard. Departs from Fuzi Miao pier.

days_plan.itin_title: Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui
Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

map_keywords: Ming Xiaoling Tomb Spirit Way Nanjing
Ming City Wall Nanjing Zhonghua Gate

visits.text: The Tianluokeng cluster forms the "four dishes and one soup": four round or oval tulou surrounding a square tulou, seen from above. It was by spotting these circular shapes on satellite images in the 1960s that the CIA believed it had discovered nuclear missile silos—an anecdote systematically cited in guidebooks, probably exaggerated but now inseparable from the place.

Taxia village stretches along a stream bordered by stone bridges and 24 ten-meter-high honor poles erected to celebrate clan members who obtained academic titles. The village counts 47 tulou of varied shapes among the mountains.

Yuchanglou, in Xiaban village, is a 14th-century round tulou whose interior pillars lean up to 15 degrees without the structure having collapsed in over 700 years. This "leaning tower" of Fujian intrigues architects.

activities.text: Bike ride between tulou in Nanjing valley. Country roads cross tea plantations, groves of 13 centuries-old banyans (over 300 years old) along the river, and inhabited Hakka hamlets.

days_plan.itin_title: Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian

visits.text: Nanning Museum presents Zhuang culture, China's first ethnic minority (~19 million people). Embroidered costumes, stringed instruments, mythology. Modern architecture opened in 2017, free with WeChat reservation, closed Mondays. Allow 1h30. Useful to close the trip: Zhuang seen at Longji, you find them in museum context here.

Zhongshan Lu mixes Cantonese and Zhuang cuisine in a dense nighttime pedestrian street of street stalls: rice noodles, skewers, seafood. Allow 1h to cross, plus 1h to taste. Point at what you like, allow 30-60 yuan per person for complete meal. Open until 11 p.m., noisy atmosphere, neons everywhere.

activities.text: Take advantage of lunch on Zhongshan Lu before catching high-speed rail back to Guilin North (2h45). If timing tight, skip museum and stick to street stalls. Station luggage storage possible (10 yuan, 6h max). Return to Guilin in mid-afternoon to recover night in city, near airport for next day's departure.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi

visits.text: You arrive at the Mausoleum of Genghis Khan, about 60 km southeast of Ordos in Ejin Horo Banner. Built between 1954 and 1956 by the Chinese government in traditional Mongolian style, the complex covers 5.5 hectares and comprises three grand yurt-shaped halls with yellow roofs. It's a cenotaph: the true tomb of Genghis Khan, who died in August 1227, has never been found. According to his wishes, he was buried without markers, somewhere in the Khentii Mountains.

In the main hall, a 5-meter white marble statue of Genghis Khan stands beside a map of the largest contiguous empire ever assembled. The rear hall houses three yurts: at center, the Khan's silver coffin (empty, containing only caps and accessories), flanked by those of his wives and brothers. The guardians, the Darkhads, are descendants of the Khan's personal guard, passing the role from father to son for 800 years.

Around the complex, the Donglian Scenic Area features the "Long Scroll of Mongolian History"—the world's longest painted scroll, tracing Mongolian history from Genghis Khan's birth to the fall of the Yuan Dynasty. Performance of "an Ordos wedding" and wrestling demonstrations. Touristy, but well executed.

Thirty kilometers away, the ghost city of Kangbashi, Ordos's new district. Built in the early 2000s for one million residents, it now holds only 100,000—dubbed "China's most famous ghost town." Deserted avenues, empty tower blocks, the copper-colored meteorite-shaped Ordos Museum, a library like a giant book. A fascinating dystopian urban walk.

activities.text: If you arrive around 9 AM at the mausoleum, you'll witness the daily shamanic ceremony led by the Darkhads: milk libations, hada scarves, offerings at the cenotaph. Inscribed on China's intangible heritage list since 2006, the ceremony is purely religious, not a tourist show—stay back, don't photograph during chants.

Traditional Mongolian meal in Kangbashi: boiled mutton carved with a knife, beef baozi, buttered milk tea. Restaurants around Genghis Khan Square are most popular with locals. Try the "Khan's vodka" if offered—short but formidable.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner Mongolia: Steppes, Dunes, and Forgotten Empires

places.name: Penglai

visits.text: Morning drive to Penglai, 75 km, about 1 hour 10 minutes. Penglai is the "City of Immortals" in Chinese mythology—said to be where the Eight Taoist Immortals crossed the sea after getting drunk at the pavilion. Perched on a cliff atop Mount Danya, Penglai Pavilion was built in 1061 under the Northern Song. It's one of the "Four Great Pavilions of China" along with Yueyang, Tengwang, and Yellow Crane. Height: 15 meters; area: 328 m².

Inside the pavilion, on the second floor, you'll find a sculpted group representing "The Eight Drunken Immortals," inspired directly from the legend. On the columns, couplets calligraphed by the greatest literati of Song, Yuan, Ming, and Qing—including Su Shi and Dong Qichang. The "Penglai Ge" plaque at the entrance was traced by Tie Bao, Qing Dynasty calligrapher. The panoramic terrace above is the official spot to observe the Penglai mirage.

Next to the pavilion, don't miss Penglai Water City (Penglai Shuicheng), China's oldest still-preserved artificial military port. Its construction began in 1042 and was completed in 1696. The enclosure wall runs 2,000 meters, pierced by two gates—Zhenyang to the south for men and horses, Water Gate to the north for warships. General Qi Jiguang, hero of the fight against Japanese pirates under the Ming, trained his fleet here. Ancient junk wrecks are displayed on the quays.

Afternoon, stroll through Eight Immortals Crossing the Sea Park (Baxian Guohai). Gourd-shaped—one of the Eight Immortals' attributes—it extends seaward with statues, gardens, long corridors, and temples. For Tao fans, guaranteed dive into mythology. For others, it's a pleasant coastal walk with a rare sight: the sharp boundary between the Yellow Sea (yellow-brown) and the Bohai Sea (blue), visible on certain days from Tianheng Shan point.

activities.text: The Penglai mirage isn't a myth: it's a real optical phenomenon, due to temperature difference between cold seawater and warm coastal air. It appears a few times yearly, especially in May-June and after rains. If luck favors you, you'll see the horizon fill with floating islands, boats suspended in the sky, and distant palaces. This phenomenon nourished all Taoist mythology of immortality.

To finish the journey, sit down for an Eight Immortals Banquet (Baxian Yan), Penglai specialty: eight dishes, each symbolizing one immortal and their magical attribute. "Tieguai Li Sea Cucumber," "He Xiangu Mushrooms"… careful presentations, local ingredients (sea cucumbers, Changdao scallops, pectens), pronounced marine flavors. Budget 380 yuan per person at Penglai Pavilion Hotel restaurant. Return to Yantai Penglai Airport, 30 minutes by taxi, for your departure flight.

days_plan.itin_title: Shandong: 11 Days from Confucius to Mount Tai and the German Coast

map_keywords: Eight Immortals Crossing the Sea Park Penglai

visits.text: Longji terraces are carved on mountainside slopes of the Yueliang Mountains since the Yuan (13th century) through the Qing by Zhuang and Red Yao peoples. Three zones: Ping'an (Zhuang, most accessible), Jinkeng/Dazhai (Yao, wildest), and old Longji. The spiral curve of rice terraces impresses whatever the season. Allow 30 min pause at first viewpoint.

Seven Stars and Moon, first Ping'an viewpoint, opens onto the most photographed motif of the terraces: seven small water planes and a moon-shaped rounded terrace. 30 min walk from village. Morning, light from east, view without backlight. Allow 1h on site with photos. Bench, explanatory panel, light crowd on weekdays.

Ping'an remains the most accessible of Longji's three villages: practicable road, restaurants, wooden Zhuang guesthouses. Village clings to ridge, narrow paved alleys, stairs between houses. Rice paddy colors vary radically by season (flooded May-June, green July-August, golden October, snow winter). See When to Go to China blog post.

activities.text: Loop the three viewpoints in 4h30: Seven Stars and Moon (no.1), Nine Dragons and Five Tigers (no.2), Sunrise/Sunset (no.3). Paved trails, light elevation gain, water and hat. Driver-guide waits at main parking. If your legs tire, do first two only, the third is reached by internal shuttle (10 yuan).

Bamboo chicken cooked in its trunk over wood fire, sticky black rice with pandan leaves, five colors of rice dyed with local plants: Zhuang cuisine accompanies any evening in a guesthouse. Communal meal at 6:30 p.m., 50-80 yuan per person. Local rice wine optional, ask before meal.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi

visits.text: You arrive via the Pingtan Strait Bridge, inaugurated December 26, 2020. At 16.34 km long, it's the world's longest sea-crossing road-rail bridge. Upper level: 6-lane highway at 100 km/h. Lower level: railway at 200 km/h. It was built in one of the world's three windiest straits, with waves exceeding 9 meters. Direct train from Fuzhou in 30 minutes.

Wander through Beigang Village, one of Fujian's loveliest surprises. The houses are entirely built from local granite blocks—grey and pink stones interlocked without mortar—to resist typhoons and permanent winds. Many have been restored as guesthouses, cafés, and workshops. Come before 10 AM to have the lanes to yourself.

Walk on Longfengtou Beach, 9.5 km of white sand and turquoise waters. Wide, flat, uncrowded except July-August—it's one of Fujian's few full-scale beaches, ranked among China's best. The sunset walk is superb, with permanent wind in your hair.

activities.text: If you're here between April and August, hunt for 'Blue Tears.' The phenomenon is caused by a bloom of bioluminescent plankton (Noctiluca scintillans) that emits electric blue light when the water is disturbed. Best conditions: moonless night, south wind, high tide. Recognized spots: Changjiang'ao, Beigang, Tannan Bay. Follow the WeChat mini-program 'Pingtan Blue Tears' for forecasts.

Rent an electric scooter (80 yuan per day) to ride the 'Most Beautiful Circular Island Route.' An 80 km coastal itinerary with cliffs, hidden coves, seaside wind farms (the Changjiang'ao park lines up dozens of turbines facing the strait), and views of the islets. Sunset under the wind turbines has become a Chinese Instagram classic.

days_plan.itin_title: Coastal Fujian - Beyond the Tulou, 11 Days on the Maritime Silk Road

visits.text: High-speed train Datong→Pingyao (~3h). Fortified city, UNESCO since 1997, China's last intact walled city. Ramparts erected in rammed earth as early as 827 BC, then reinforced in stone in 1370 under the Ming. Six kilometers in perimeter, 12 m high, 6 gates, 72 bastions. Turtle silhouette seen from the sky.
The Rishengchang Bank (1823), China's first bank. Invention of interbank transfers. Martial arts-trained escorts for money transport.
The old county yamen (26,000 m², rebuilt in 1346). Daily reenactments of ancient trials.
Shuanglin Temple, 7 km away, 1,500 years old. Over 1,000 colored clay Buddhist statues, including a 26-armed Guanyin. UNESCO with Pingyao.
The Confucius Temple, the oldest preserved in China. Dacheng Hall from 1163 (Jin dynasty).

activities.text: Strolling the lanes parallel to Ming Qing Jie. Sample mao'erduo (Shanxi 'cat's ear' noodles).
Sunset from the ramparts—north section, view over the rooftops and Shanxi mountains.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie
Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie

places.name: Putian

visits.text: Stop at Chongwu, China's only maritime fortified city, 50 minutes from Quanzhou. You'll walk along 2.5 km of granite stone ramparts, 7 meters high, pierced by four gates. Built in 1387 by order of Emperor Hongwu to protect the coast from Japanese pirates (wokou), they've remained virtually intact. Climb the parapet walk for a view of the South China Sea.

Tour the Chongwu Sculpture Park outside the ramparts. More than 500 works in local granite are displayed, carved by Hui'an artisans—heirs to a thousand-year-old craft. Buddhas, dragons, fishing scenes: Chongwu supplies sculptures to all of China and even to diaspora temples. Curious but photogenic.

Board the ferry at Wenjia Wharf to Meizhou, the sacred island of the China Sea. The crossing takes 20 minutes. This is where Lin Mo was born in 960 CE, a fisherman's daughter gifted with the ability to predict weather and rescue the shipwrecked. She died at 28 while attempting to save a boat and became Mazu, goddess of the sea. More than 200 million devotees worldwide venerate her today, and over 5,000 temples are dedicated to her.

You arrive before Mazu's ancestral temple, founded in 987 CE, shortly after the goddess's death. Destroyed during the Cultural Revolution, it was rebuilt as early as 1978 thanks to donations from devotees, particularly from Taiwan. It's now the 'Mecca of maritime Asia': on the 23rd day of the 3rd lunar month, tens of thousands of pilgrims gather for the goddess's birthday. Red wood, glazed tiles, permanent incense, processions of palanquins—the atmosphere is intense.

Climb to the giant Mazu statue on the island's summit. Granite, 14.35 meters high, facing the Taiwan Strait. Inaugurated in 1987 for the cult's millennium. An almost identical twin statue stands in Tainan on the strait's other shore—an obvious politico-spiritual gesture. The panorama of the islets and coast is superb at sunset.

activities.text: Spot Hui'an women in the alleyways. Recognizable by their traditional costume—yellow flowered hat, blue scarf tied under the chin, short fitted tunic, silver belt, and wide black trousers—they continue to wear this outfit daily in coastal villages. It's one of the last Han groups to maintain a distinctive dress tradition. Ask before photographing.

Walk on Goose Tail Beach (Goldsand Beach), 9.6 km long with golden sand and clear waters. It's one of Fujian's most beautiful beaches, little frequented by foreigners. Swimming possible from April to October. At the northern tip, Goose Tail Geological Park displays rock formations sculpted by marine erosion into animal figures—locals see lions, tortoises, and even an elephant.

Dine at a local home on Meizhou. Several fishing families run restaurants behind their houses: rice crab, fish steamed in bamboo leaves, ginger clams. Count on 80-150 yuan per person. It's also the right place to try 'Mazu cake' (Mazu bing), a sweet biscuit offered to pilgrims.

days_plan.itin_title: Coastal Fujian - Beyond the Tulou, 11 Days on the Maritime Silk Road
Coastal Fujian - Beyond the Tulou, 11 Days on the Maritime Silk Road

map_keywords: Meizhou Island Mazu Ancestral Temple Putian

visits.text: The Duku Road - 561 km across the Tianshan - is a stage in itself. Built by 168 soldiers sacrificed over 10 years (1974-1983), it crosses five passes over 3,000 m and traverses glaciers, spruce forests, gorges, and high plateaus. Stop at Qiaoerma, a small high-altitude relay town that serves as a bivouac point for drivers along the Duku, with its panorama over the Bogda's snowy mountains. Plan the whole day by car, with several breaks. Road closed October to May due to snow.

The Tianshan Pass, the road's highest point, peaks at 3,200 m. Thin air, alpine meadows, marmots whistling as you pass. Don't linger if you feel dizzy - descending 200 m is enough to dissipate symptoms.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Xinjiang - Kanas, Altai, and Ili Steppes in 16 Days

visits.text: Train from Weifang to Qingdao in exactly 1 hour. Upon arrival, first shock: Qingdao resembles no other Chinese city. Cobbled streets rising and falling, half-timbered facades, neo-Romanesque bell towers—you're in the former German colony of Tsingtau, occupied from 1898 to 1914. The German Empire obtained a 99-year lease on Jiaozhou Bay in March 1898 after the murder of two German missionaries. Start with St. Michael's Cathedral, completed in 1934 by a German architect.

Descend Zhongshan Road, the old main commercial artery, to Zhanqiao Pier. Built by the Germans in 1891 then extended to 440 meters, it ends at the octagonal Huilan Pavilion with its green-tiled roof—the very one that appears on every Tsingtao bottle label. The stroll to the pier's end gives fine views of old Qingdao backed against the hills, and the fishing fleet of the bay.

The afternoon is devoted to the legendary place: Tsingtao Brewery, founded August 15, 1903, by the Anglo-German Brewery Co. under the name Germania-Brauerei. The first beer came out December 22, 1904, in strict compliance with the Reinheitsgebot (Bavarian purity law of 1516). The museum occupies the original buildings—copper equipment, 1903 brewing hall, archives. The ticket includes two tastings: the classic filtered Tsingtao and the far rarer unfiltered raw version.

Morning in the Badaguan quarter ("Eight Great Passes"), upscale 1930s residential zone where each street is planted with a different tree species. Over 200 villas in every European style—English, German, Russian, Spanish, Danish—including the house where Mao Zedong summered. Very few foreign visitors, many newlyweds mid-photo shoot. Long stroll (at least 2 hours) very pleasant in spring when magnolias and lilacs bloom.

Zhongshan Park, the former German public park, shelters the remains of Mount Qingdao's underground fortress. German command headquarters were dug as early as 1899 on five underground levels—2,000 m² of tunnels, mess halls, and gun positions. Today the Mount Qingdao Fort Museum, with uniformed mannequins and artillery battery still pointing seaward. Somewhat creepy atmosphere in the deepest levels—damp, poorly lit—but that's what gives it charm.

Afternoon, descend to Beach No. 1 (Diyi Haishuiyuchang), Qingdao's oldest developed beach, dating from the German era. 580 meters of sand, more than decent water, and dozens of typical beach cabins. For an even finer beach, walk 30 minutes east to Shilaoren Beach, wider and less crowded. Water around 22°C in July-August.

Full-day excursion to Mount Lao (Laoshan), 40 km east of Qingdao. It's China's highest coastal mountain (1,132 m) and one of Taoism's holiest—nicknamed "Mount Tai in the Sea," because unlike other sacred mountains always inland, this one plunges directly into the Yellow Sea. Emperor Qin Shi Huangdi came here in 219 BC seeking the elixir of immortality. He left empty-handed, but the legend made Laoshan a spiritual pinnacle.

Direction Taiqing Palace, the principal Taoist temple, founded under the Han (200 BC) and rebuilt under the Song. The enclosure shelters 155 halls in three ensembles—Sanqing, Sanhuang, Sanguan. Spot the thousand-year-old junipers, one allegedly planted 2,100 years ago. Spectacular sea views from Yu Ying Pavilion. Here Taoist monks still draw the spring water used by Tsingtao Brewery.

Afternoon, ride the cable car to Mingxia Pavilion, then hike toward Jufeng summit via stone stairway trails. If you're less keen on walking, opt for the Yangkou sector on the east side: coves, cliffs, and a very pretty 4-km coastal trail overlooking the sea. Round rocks polished by waves form small natural harbors reachable by boat.

activities.text: In the evening, dive into Beer Street (Dengzhou Road), steps from the brewery. Restaurants line up kegs directly on the sidewalk. Qingdao peculiarity: fresh beer sold in plastic bags with a straw—no, it's not a joke, it's become a local emblem. Order a kilo of clams, a plate of grilled squid, and a bag of beer. Budget 80 yuan per person.

At May Fourth Square (Wusi Guangchang), see the red spiral sculpture 30 meters high "Wind of May," Qingdao's symbol since 1997; it commemorates the May 4, 1919 student movement—triggered precisely by the Treaty of Versailles decision to award the German concession of Qingdao to Japan. Evening seaside promenade, or bike rental along the seawall.

Seafood cooking class at a local's home: Qingdao being a major port, fish markets supply ultra-fresh clams, abalone, sea cucumbers, and sea urchins. Several 3-hour workshops offer a morning market visit followed by a class to learn how to cook the famous clams in Tsingtao beer. Budget 350 yuan per person.

On the return road to Qingdao, stop in a fishing village like Shazikou to taste a pot of Laoshan spring water—mineral, slightly sweet, it's the water that gives Tsingtao beer its signature. A few stalls also sell teas grown on Laoshan's slopes: a rare northern green tea, strongly fragrant.

Seafood dinner at Shazikou facing the harbor. Boats return around 4-5pm, restaurants serve what comes out of the tanks: hairy crabs, clams, abalone, braised sea cucumbers. Tasting Laoshan sea cucumber, considered China's finest, is a small extravagance (120 yuan apiece but unforgettable).

days_plan.itin_title: Shandong: 11 Days from Confucius to Mount Tai and the German Coast
Shandong: 11 Days from Confucius to Mount Tai and the German Coast
Shandong: 11 Days from Confucius to Mount Tai and the German Coast

visits.text: Qinghai Lake (Kokonor in Mongolian, meaning "Blue Sea") is China's largest lake: 4,500 km² of saltwater at roughly 3,200 meters elevation, framed by four mountain ranges peaking between 3,600 and 5,200 meters. Elliptical in shape, it freezes solid in winter. Tibetan Buddhist pilgrims circumambulate the lake—360 km that takes 18 days on horseback or 23 days on foot.

The Erlangjian scenic area on the south shore offers the best lake access. A jetty extends into the turquoise water with views of the snow-capped Datong Mountains to the north. On clear days, you can make out the sand dunes of Shadao Island on the far shore. In July, fields of flowering rapeseed encircle the lake in a brilliant yellow belt.

Bird Island (Niaodao), on the lake's northwest shore, welcomes over 100,000 migratory birds each spring from Southeast Asia—bar-headed geese, whooper swans, cormorants, black-necked cranes. The island itself has been closed to visitors since 2017 to protect habitat, but shoreline observatories allow viewing of the colonies from May through July.
Sand Island (Shadao), on the northeast shore, juxtaposes sand dunes and lake in a striking contrast. The area spans 120 km² with dunes rising several dozen meters high against the turquoise water.

activities.text: Bike tour along Qinghai Lake's south shore. The Tour of Qinghai Lake, a professional cycling race held each July alongside the Tour de France, popularized this route. At 3,200 meters, the effort is real, but the scenery—turquoise lake, grasslands, yaks, snowy peaks—more than compensates.
Visit a Tibetan nomadic camp on the grasslands bordering the lake. Families offer yak butter tea, tsampa (roasted barley flour), and share daily pastoral life—milking yaks, making dried cheese.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou
Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou

visits.text: You're beginning your journey from the most unexpected angle: Marco Polo described 13th-century Quanzhou as the greatest port in the world, and Ibn Battuta confirmed it. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site on July 25, 2021, under the name 'Emporium of the World in Song-Yuan China,' the city gathers 22 sites testifying to a rare cosmopolitanism: Buddhists, Taoists, Muslims, Hindus, Manicheans, and Nestorian Christians coexisted here for centuries. Start at Kaiyuan Temple, founded in 686 CE—the largest Buddhist temple in Fujian.

Look up: in front of Kaiyuan stand the twin pagodas, now the city's emblem. Zhenguo Tower soars to 48 meters, Renshou to 45 meters. Built in wood in the 9th century and reconstructed in stone in the 13th, they've survived multiple earthquakes thanks to remarkable seismic engineering. Every face is carved with bas-reliefs of bodhisattvas and guardians. Allow 30 minutes to circle both and read the details.

Push open the door of Qingjing Mosque on Tumen Street—one of the most moving mosques in China. Built in 1009 CE (year 400 of the Islamic calendar) by Arab merchants, modeled on Damascus mosques, in white granite and diabase. The 12.3-meter portal survived an earthquake that collapsed the main prayer hall roof in 1607—you can still see the pointed arches and domes. A rooftop platform served to observe the moon for Ramadan. Free admission.

Walk down Xijie (West Street), Quanzhou's historic heart. Over one kilometer, you traverse arcaded colonial-era houses from the early 20th century, incense shops, traditional puppet workshops. This is where the mercantile soul of Zayton—the Arabic name for the city that gave the word 'satin' to French—is still palpable. Try the peanut tangyuan at a century-old shop.

Head to Mount Qingyuan, 8 km north. You'll encounter China's largest Taoist rock statue: Laozi carved directly into stone in the 11th-12th centuries, 5.63 meters tall and 8.01 meters wide. The sage sits with ears perked, a smile somewhere between amused and wise—one of the most expressive faces in Chinese statuary. The approach walk crosses a forest of fragrant pines.

Reserve two hours for the Quanzhou Maritime Museum: it recounts a thousand years of exchange via the Maritime Silk Road. The showpiece: the wreck of a late 13th-century merchant ship, discovered in the bay in 1974, 24 meters long, loaded with spices, precious woods, and ceramics at the time of its sinking. You'll also find Islamic, Hindu, and Nestorian funerary steles—witnesses to Song-Yuan religious mixing.

For Islamic history enthusiasts, don't miss the mausoleums on Mount Lingshan. According to local tradition, two companions of the Prophet Muhammad rest here, having come to preach Islam in China in the 7th century—an attribution historically contested but deeply rooted in the Hui community's memory. A carved stele recounts that Admiral Zheng He, himself a Muslim, came to pray here in 1417 before one of his expeditions. A discreet but emotionally powerful site.

If you still have energy, visit Anping Bridge 30 km south in the town of Anhai, inscribed on the UNESCO list in 2021. Built between 1138 and 1151 under the Song dynasty, 2,070 meters long, it was the longest stone bridge in the medieval world. Now standing over a rice paddy rather than water, it retains all its magic. Allow one hour on site.

activities.text: Sample Minnan cuisine, classified as intangible heritage and recognized as a 'UNESCO City of Gastronomy' in October 2025. Dishes to try: oyster omelet (oh-ah-jian), beef soup with Arab-style sauce (legacy of Muslim merchants), sweet potato noodles, and savory zongzi. The Wudianshi Market in the old town gathers the best stalls.

End the day at Guandi Temple, dedicated to the deified General Guan Yu. It's one of the liveliest temples in southern China: merchants, taxi drivers, and even local police burn incense sticks here. The cloud of incense and folk chants create an atmosphere you won't find anywhere else. Not a visit—an immersion.

Immerse yourself in Quanzhou's Hui community. Several families of Arab and Persian origin have lived in the city continuously since the 12th century. The neighborhood around Qingjing Mosque concentrates halal butcher shops, restaurants serving the spicy beef sauce inherited from Muslim merchants, and Arabic-Chinese calligraphy shops. Ask to see the family trees displayed in some homes, sometimes spanning 30 generations.

In the evening, attend a Nanyin performance—traditional music inscribed on the UNESCO list in 2009. This tradition, over 1,000 years old, is played on horizontal pipa, flutes, and percussion. Quanzhou's Nanyin Theatre offers daily performances; tickets around 60 yuan. Melancholic and hypnotic.

days_plan.itin_title: Coastal Fujian - Beyond the Tulou, 11 Days on the Maritime Silk Road
Coastal Fujian - Beyond the Tulou, 11 Days on the Maritime Silk Road

visits.text: Sunrise at Jade Emperor Peak: on the esplanade, you'll be squeezed among hundreds of bundled-up pilgrims, some making this ascent for three family generations. Tradition dictates attaching a heart-shaped padlock to the overlook chains to seal a wish. After the spectacle, descend by cable car on the West side (Tianwai) to discover another less-crowded slope.

Direction Qufu by high-speed train from Tai'an: 25 minutes. You arrive at Qufu East; take a taxi to the historic center (15 minutes). Drop your bags at the hotel and tackle the San Kong complex (the "Three Confucians"), starting with the Temple of Confucius. Built as early as 478 BC, one year after the sage's death, it now extends over 14 hectares with 104 buildings from Jin to Qing dynasties and more than 1,250 ancient trees.

At the temple's heart stands Dacheng Hall ("Great Achievement"), the Confucian equivalent of an imperial throne hall, with its ten stone pillars carved with coiled dragons—a privilege normally reserved for imperial palaces. More than 1,000 stelae are preserved on site, including Han bas-reliefs illustrating the Master's life. Twelve emperors came in person to offer sacrifices here.

Head to the Kong Cemetery (Kong Lin), 1 km north of the temple. Brace yourself for the shock: it's the world's largest family cemetery—200 hectares, over 100,000 tombs accumulated over 2,000 years, from Confucius's own tomb to descendants of the 76th and 78th generations. The enclosure wall runs 7 km long, and over 10,000 ancient trees form a forest inhabited by crows. Rent an electric cart at the entrance to traverse the main avenue.

Confucius's own tomb is surprisingly modest: a simple conical mound, before a stele, in a clearing. Beside him rest his son and grandson Zisi, also a philosopher. A particular zone groups the tombs of the Dukes Yansheng (55th to 64th) with their "spirit ways" sculpted with rams, lions, and horses. Here you measure the historical weight of the longest lineage in Chinese aristocracy.

Afternoon, return to town for the Kong Mansion (Kong Fu). This private residence of direct descendants comprises 463 rooms distributed across 9 interior courtyards. The family lived here and managed their estates for over 800 years. It's a unique case: a private aristocratic palace whose lineage was never interrupted, until the 77th Duke Yansheng fled to Taiwan in 1949. Spot the original murals and the private study cabinet.

If time permits, make a detour to Yan Hui Temple, dedicated to Confucius's favorite disciple, just inside the old wall's north gate. More modest than the main temple, it's also much quieter. The sage Yan Hui died at 41, to Confucius's great sorrow who considered him his natural successor. Entry is very affordable and the place charming late in the day.

activities.text: In the evening, settle into a teahouse in old Qufu, in the shadow of the Ming ramparts. Sample the Kong Family Banquet, refined cuisine codified by Confucius's descendants to receive emperors and officials—196 traditional dishes in total, of which you'll taste a few classics (Qufu-style lacquered duck, imperial tofu, squirrel-shaped fish). Budget 250 yuan per person for a tasting menu.

Before dinner, climb the reconstructed Ming ramparts encircling the old town. A walk of about 30 minutes along the south wall offers the finest perspectives on the gray roofs of the Confucian complex and Drum Tower square. Sunset behind the glazed-tile pavilions is worth it.

Evening show: at Xingtan Theater (named for the "Apricot Terrace" where Confucius taught his disciples), a Ba Yi music and dance performance reproduces Confucian sacrificial dances in period costumes. One hour diving into codified imperial ritual. Evening performances, tickets between 80 and 180 yuan.

days_plan.itin_title: Shandong: 11 Days from Confucius to Mount Tai and the German Coast
Shandong: 11 Days from Confucius to Mount Tai and the German Coast

places.name: Riwa Yading

visits.text: Yading Nature Reserve was sanctified in the 8th century by Padmasambhava and described in 1928 in National Geographic by American explorer Joseph Rock—his 60-page report revealed these landscapes to the Western world. Two years later, writer James Hilton published Lost Horizon and invented Shangri-La inspired by these descriptions. From the entrance at Riwa, take the bus to Chonggu Monastery (3,880m)—starting point for all hikes.

Chonggu Monastery sits at the foot of Mount Chenrezig (6,032m, compassion), the highest of the three sacred peaks. Built about 800 years ago, it's damaged and partially restored—but its location, facing Chenrezig's glaciated wall with green pastures in the foreground, compensates everything. It's one of the reserve's most beautiful viewpoints. Allow 45 minutes.
