Pearl Lake (珍珠海, 4,100m) is 3km from Chonggu Monastery, entirely on wooden boardwalks. In clear weather, it reflects Chenrezig with surreal clarity—the water is turquoise, the shores white as snow. It's the reserve's most accessible sacred lake. Allow 40 minutes walking from the monastery.

Milk Lake (牛奶海, 4,500m) is Yading's second sacred lake—the climb from Luorong Pastures takes 1.5 hours. Its milky white color comes from the reflection of snow and surrounding limestone rocks. Its shape, seen from above, resembles a teardrop. In fine weather, Jambeyang's glaciers dominate it directly.

Five Color Lake (五色海, 4,600m) sits 100m elevation above Milk Lake. It's the reserve's largest lake (0.7 hectares) and highest. Its color changes with the light—from deep green to sky blue depending on time of day. Arrive in early afternoon for the best effect. Aquatic plants on the bottom color the water from the surface.

activities.text: Hike to Luorong Pastures (洛绒牛场, 4,150m): it's the only place in the reserve where you see all three sacred mountains—Chenrezig (6,032m), Jambeyang (5,958m, wisdom), and Chanadorje (5,958m, power)—simultaneously. Yak herds graze in the sparse grass while snow-capped summits reflect in the Gongga River. Allow 2.5 hours round trip from Chonggu.

Observe Tibetan pilgrims doing kora (circumambulation) around the three mountains. Some come from distant provinces, on foot for weeks. The belief holds that one complete kora equals reciting one million mantras. Walk with them, even for a short section.

Complete hike of the Milk Lake - Five Color Lake circuit from Luorong: this is Yading's "long" route—590m positive elevation gain, about 4-6 hours round trip. Start early (7:30 AM) to avoid groups and enjoy morning light on the glaciers. Walk slowly, drink regularly.
Last sunset over Yading from the heights of Riwa village, facing Chenrezig. The mountain's glaciated face turns orange, then pink, then violet in 20 minutes. This is why you're here.

days_plan.itin_title: From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days
From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days
Three Parallel Rivers & Eastern Tibet - 14-Day Yunnan to Kham Extension

map_keywords: Yading Nature Reserve entrance Riwa village
Five Color Lake Yading Wuse Hai
Luorong Pasture Yading three sacred mountains

visits.text: Rongbuk Monastery (绒布寺) stands at 5,019m altitude—the world's highest monastery according to most sources. Founded in 1902 by Nyingma lama Ngawang Tenzin Norbu, it served as starting point and shelter for all major British Everest expeditions in the 1920s. Destroyed during the Cultural Revolution in 1974, it was rebuilt. About twenty monks and nuns still live there. Behind the entrance's white stupa, Everest's north face rises directly—no foreground, no haze, just 8,848 meters of rock and ice.

Rongbuk village and its immediate surroundings are worth exploring on foot from the monastery: meditation caves in the cliff, carved mani walls, prayer flags snapping in the Himalayan's icy wind. Footprints of the founder are indicated in a rock cave above the complex—legend or reality, the place is impressive.

activities.text: Since the north base camp closed to tourists in December 2018 (environmental reasons), Rongbuk is the furthest accessible point. Climbers with ascent permits can go to the real base camp, 8km south. For you, the view from the monastery roof suffices—it's probably the most direct view of the world's highest mountain's north face. Stay until dusk if the sky is clear.

Night at Rongbuk guesthouse (5,000m)—basic, unheated with minimal ventilation, but it's the only available accommodation. Bring a -20°C sleeping bag. The night at this altitude, with Everest in the darkness, is an experience unto itself.

days_plan.itin_title: From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days

visits.text: Chengyang's Yongji Bridge, known as the "Wind and Rain Bridge," completed in 1912, is China's most famous Dong covered bridge. Measuring 64.4 meters long, 3.4 meters wide, and 10.6 meters high, it rests on five stone piers topped with multi-tiered wooden pavilions. The entire structure was assembled without a single nail—only through interlocking fir beams. The Sanjiang region counts more than 160 drum towers and a hundred covered bridges.

The eight villages of Chengyang (Ma'an, Pingzhai, Yanzhai, Dazhai, Pingpu, Pingtan, Jichang, Guandong) form an ensemble of stilted houses, drum towers, and water mills along the Linxi River. From the bridge, tea-planted hills, rice paddies, and water mills compose a preserved Dong rural landscape.

Excursion to Biasha Miao Village (2.5-hour drive each way from Sanjiang via Congjiang), nicknamed the "gunmen's village." The men still wear the topknot shaved on the sides, a knife at the belt, and a flintlock rifle—the only community in China authorized to carry firearms. Burials take place under a tree planted at each child's birth.

activities.text: Hike between Chengyang villages along the Linxi River and tea hills. The short circuit (Chengyang → Pingtan → Guandong) crosses rice paddies and plantations over approximately 12 km.

days_plan.itin_title: Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian

visits.text: Before Sanya, stop in Lingshui for Xincun Bay. You'll see the large floating village of the Tanka - a fishing people who have lived on water for 700 years, descendants of Song dynasty coastal migrants. Houses sit on bamboo rafts moored in neighborhoods. You reach it by boat, visit fish farms, lunch with locals. Remains one of China's last authentic Tanka communities.

Sanya, the island's premier beach resort, awaits you. First discovery: Yalong Bay, considered the island's most beautiful beach - 7 km of fine white sand, translucent turquoise water up to 30 meters from shore. Dubbed "China's first bay." Luxury hotels line their villas along the waterfront, but the beach is free access. Sea at 26°C in winter, ideal for swimming.

Stroll along Sanya Bay, the urban beach in the heart of the city. 22 km of coastline lined with coconut palms, dotted with small family beaches. More lively than Yalong Bay, more accessible. Spectacular sunset over the South China Sea - light shifts from orange to mauve quickly. Drink huts and bars on the sand.

Head southwest of Sanya to Nanshan Cultural Park, founded in 1988 for the 2,000th anniversary of Buddhism in China. You'll find across 30 km² several Tang-style temples, gardens, statues. The Guanyin of Nanshan statue, standing on an artificial island connected to the coast by a 280 m causeway, rises 108 meters high - one of the world's tallest statues. It was inaugurated on April 24, 2005, after 6 years of construction, in the presence of 108 monks from across Asia.

The Guanyin has three faces: one looks inland to bless China, the other two look to sea to protect the world. One face holds a sutra, another a rosary, the third a lotus. Mixed metal structure coated with anti-salt fluoropolymer, roughly 2,600 tons total. Inside, in Yuantong Hall, you'll find a Guanyin statue in gold and jade inlaid with precious stones (the "small" Guanyin set with 100 kg of gold and 120 carats of diamonds). You can climb to the goddess's feet for the view.

Twenty kilometers away, you arrive at Tianya Haijiao ("Edge of Sky, Corner of Sea"), a site that owes its name to large rocks inscribed with Song dynasty calligraphy. Under the Tang, it was an exile site for disgraced officials - they said it was "the end of the world." Chinese couples come en masse to bless their love, it's become an iconic wedding site in southern China.

Luhuitou Park ("the deer turns back"), on the eponymous hill, offers the best panoramic view of Sanya Bay. The bronze deer statue, erected in 1987, stands 12 m high. Li legend tells of a hunter who chased a deer to the cliff edge - the animal stopped, turned around and transformed into a young girl, who became his wife. It's the mythical origin of the Li people.

Head to Wuzhizhou Island, 30 km east of Sanya. Despite its name often mistranslated as "Monkey Island," there are no macaques here - common confusion with Nanwan, don't make that mistake. Wuzhizhou is one of the island's best diving and snorkeling spots. Visibility up to 25 m in clear weather, corals, clownfish, turtles. The island covers 1.5 km², ferry from Houhai port (10 minute crossing).

Head to Haitang Bay, northeast of Sanya, which became the island's new chic bay since 2010. You'll see the Atlantis Sanya complex (opened 2018), with its giant aquarium, water park, and 1,314 rooms. Beach stretching 22 km, still wild in places - but urbanization is advancing quickly. Worth seeing for the contrast between preserved nature and XXL hotels.

If you still have energy late in the day, climb Phoenix Mountain for the panoramic view of the three bays (Yalong, Haitang and Sanya Bay). Cable car in 8 minutes, observation platform at 480 m. It's an opportunity to locate all the beaches you've seen these two days, and see the South China Sea at a glance.

activities.text: Boat in Xincun Bay to visit the Tanka floating village. Allow 1 hour navigation, plus a stop at a floating restaurant for lunch - raw fish with chili, live shrimp, ginger clams. Served on bamboo tables directly connected to the pontoons. Fair price, around 100-150 yuan per person.

Evening at Sanya night market at Diyilou ("First Market"), downtown. Skewers of grilled seafood, ginger fish, chili crabs, green mangoes with salt. Locals eat here, it's packed, lively, perfect after a long day. Ice-cold Pearl River beer at 5 yuan a large bottle.

Vegetarian meal at Nanshan Temple, famous throughout China. Refined Buddhist cuisine, no garlic or onion, based on tofu, mushrooms, vegetables and meat imitations. Served in a large quiet hall with sea views. Allow 80-150 yuan per person, it's one of Asia's finest vegetarian meals.

Relaxing evening at a Yalong Bay hotel spa. Traditional Hainanese massage with coconut oil and local aloe, 90 minutes for 400-600 yuan depending on hotels. Most resorts accept non-guests for their spas. Take advantage to recover after this big cultural day.

Snorkeling or introductory dive at Wuzhizhou. Several PADI-certified centers on the island, complete equipment. Diving at -8/-12 m, 30 minutes underwater, allow 600-800 yuan with a dedicated instructor. Water at 25°C even in winter, so no need for thick wetsuit.

Duty-free shopping at Sanya International Duty-Free. Hainan is China's only total free port, and mainland Chinese fly in by the planeload to buy cosmetics, perfumes, watches, alcohol. Annual quotas of 100,000 yuan duty-free per person. For foreigners, it's less advantageous than Hong Kong, but simpler if you're already here.

days_plan.itin_title: Tropical Hainan: 10 Days Around China's Southern Island
Tropical Hainan: 10 Days Around China's Southern Island
Tropical Hainan: 10 Days Around China's Southern Island

visits.text: Sayram Lake, perched at 2,070 m, is nicknamed 'the Atlantic's last tear' - it's the easternmost point reached by moist winds from the ocean, after a 4,000 km journey. At 458 km² and 92 m deep, it's Xinjiang's largest and highest alpine lake. The color, in June-July, oscillates between sapphire and turquoise depending on light. Kazakhs say it was born from the tears of two lovers separated by the steppe.

The Guozigou Bridge, just before the lake's south entrance, is an engineering feat: Xinjiang's highest cable-stayed bridge, opened in 2011, spanning a dizzying valley. Spectacular view while crossing, and also from the trail descending northward. Photographers fly their drones here (prior declaration required).

The 'Genghis Khan Command Point' (Dianjiangtai) is a rocky platform where, according to Mongol tradition, the Khan reviewed his troops during the western campaign of 1219. Small commemorative stele, 360° view over the lake and peaks. Allow 45 minutes.

activities.text: Bike around the lake: the 79 km circular road was completed in 2005 and hosts an international race each year. Ride over two days for athletes, or in 15-20 km sections for half-day trips. Rental at the main entrance (50 yuan/hour for an electric bike).
Picnic on the flowery meadows of the less crowded west shore: daisies, yellow poppies, gentians in June. Kazakh yurts open to the public offer milk tea, cheese, and fresh bread.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Xinjiang - Kanas, Altai, and Ili Steppes in 16 Days

visits.text: Flight from Xi'an to Shanghai (about 2.5 hours). The Bund (Waitan), an Anglo-Urdu term meaning 'muddy embankment,' stretches for one mile along the west bank of the Huangpu River. Before the British arrived in 1845, there were only muddy shores here. The Opium Wars opened the port to Westerners, and 52 buildings in various styles—Romanesque, Gothic, Neoclassical, Beaux-Arts, Art Deco—gradually lined the avenue. The most imposing is the former HSBC Building (No. 12), completed in 1923, considered at the time the most sumptuous building between the Suez Canal and the Bering Strait. Next door, the Customs House (No. 13), built in 1927, sports a clock inspired by Big Ben. Across the Bund, on the opposite bank of the river, the Pudong skyline bristles with futuristic skyscrapers.

Yu Garden (Yuyuan), built starting in 1559 by Pan Yunduan—a high-ranking Ming dynasty official—to offer his father a peaceful retreat. Construction took nearly 20 years. Across 5 acres, the garden distills the landscape art of Jiangnan: pavilions, rockeries, lotus ponds, and winding corridors, separated by 'dragon walls' whose ridges undulate and end in a dragon's head. The Great Rockery, designed by master gardener Zhang Nanyang, is a 39-foot-high artificial mountain composed of 2,000 tons of yellow Wukang stones—it's the only surviving work by this master. In front of the entrance, the Nine-Turn Bridge leads to the Huxinting Teahouse in the middle of a pond: its zigzags are meant to block evil spirits, who can only travel in straight lines.

The former French Concession of Shanghai retains its plane trees planted in the early 20th century, Art Deco villas, and shikumen—stone-gate townhouses typical of Shanghai, which appeared in the 1860s and once represented over 60% of the city's housing stock. The Tianzifang quarter, a former residential complex of narrow lanes, has been transformed into art galleries, design studios, and cafés.

The Lujiazui district in Pudong, a special economic zone opened in 1990, concentrates the three towers that define Shanghai's silhouette: Jin Mao Tower (1,380 feet, 88 floors, completed 1999), Shanghai World Financial Center (1,614 feet, 101 floors, 2008), and Shanghai Tower (2,073 feet, 128 floors, 2015)—the world's second-tallest building. The latter's elevator reaches the 119th floor in 55 seconds. From the top, on a clear day, the view extends across the entire megalopolis of 26 million inhabitants.

Nanjing Road (Nanjing Lu), a 3.4-mile shopping artery, links People's Square to the Bund. The pedestrianized western section, opened in 1999, concentrates department stores and brand-name shops along a half-mile stretch. At the street's end, People's Square houses the Shanghai Museum, a building shaped like an ancient bronze vessel displaying collections of Shang and Zhou bronzes, ceramics, and calligraphy.

Excursion to the water town of Zhujiajiao, 29 miles west of Shanghai (about 1 hour on metro line 17). Nicknamed 'Shanghai's Venice,' this 1,700-year-old town preserves lanes and buildings from the Ming and Qing dynasties. Thirty-six stone bridges span the canals, including Fangsheng Bridge—the largest five-arch stone arch bridge in the Shanghai region, built in 1571 by monk Xingchao of Cimen Temple in memory of his mother. Rising 19 feet, it's adorned with a relief of eight dragons surrounding a pearl on the central arch and stone lions at the ends. North Street (Bei Dajie), stretching half a mile, is lined with old buildings and craft shops.

Kezhi Garden, Zhujiajiao's largest private garden, blends traditional Chinese styles with Western influences in an ensemble of pavilions, ponds, and greenery. Its five-story building topped with a pavilion is the old town's highest point.

activities.text: Twilight stroll along the Bund: when lights come on along both riverbanks, the contrast between the colonial architecture and the illuminated Pudong towers is striking.
Wander through the Yuyuan Bazaar surrounding the garden, among souvenir shops, teahouses, and xiaolongbao restaurants (soup dumplings), a Shanghai specialty.
Ascend to the Shanghai Tower observatory (119th floor) for a 360° panorama of the city, then walk down through the Lujiazui financial district.
Traditional boat ride on the canals: boatmen in local costume glide wooden craft under stone bridges and along white houses with black-tiled roofs.
Sample local specialties: zongzi (sticky rice dumplings wrapped in bamboo leaves), braised pork trotters, and sesame cakes from the North Street stalls.

days_plan.itin_title: Classic China: Beijing, Xi'an & Shanghai
Classic China: Beijing, Xi'an & Shanghai
Classic China: Beijing, Xi'an & Shanghai
Classic China: Beijing, Xi'an & Shanghai
Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui
Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

visits.text: Touch down in Shangri-La (formerly Zhongdian) at 3,280 m altitude—this is your base camp for the journey ahead. If you're flying in from Kunming, dedicate the day to gentle acclimatization and preparing your 4x4 for the 13 days to come. Walk minimally, hydrate constantly, eat light—your body needs 24 hours to adjust.

Head 5 km north to Songzanlin, nicknamed the "Little Potala" of Yunnan. Built in 1679 on the orders of the 5th Dalai Lama, it's Yunnan's largest Gelugpa monastery and one of the thirteen great monasteries of Kham. Today it houses over 600 monks. Arrive at opening (8 AM) to catch the tranquility and morning chants before tour buses flood the staircases.

Wander into Dukezong Old Town, devastated by a 2014 fire that destroyed two-thirds of its wooden houses. It's since been rebuilt to match the original. Climb to the giant golden prayer wheel perched on Guishan Hill—21 m tall, 60 tons—it takes several people to turn, and it's the quintessential local experience.

If you've got energy to spare, loop through Pudacuo National Park, 22 km east. China's first IUCN-standard national park (2007), it shelters two alpine lakes (Shudu and Bita) above 3,500 m, virgin forests, and a 13 km boardwalk circling the lakes. Feel free to skip if you arrive late from Kunming.

activities.text: Come evening, descend to Dukezong's main street for yak hot pot (Mongolian-style fondue with yak meat), spicy Tibetan potatoes (tudou si), and salted butter tea. One bowl will suffice if it's your first encounter—it's an acquired taste.

Drink plenty of water and avoid alcohol tonight: altitude is beginning to bite, and poor sleep here will handicap the days ahead. If you're taking preventive medication like acetazolamide (Diamox), follow your doctor's dosage and timing—not a guidebook's.

days_plan.itin_title: Three Parallel Rivers & Eastern Tibet - 14-Day Yunnan to Kham Extension

visits.text: Walk through the old Xiaogongyuan quarter, nicknamed the "Little Paris of the Orient": across 2 km², hundreds of Dutch-Chinese qilou arcade houses built between 1860 and 1930 by wealthy Teochew merchants returning from overseas. Shantou was the embarkation port for the Teochew diaspora to Thailand, Cambodia, and Singapore in the late 19th century. Many buildings are crumbling, but renovation has been underway since 2014. A striking sight.

Drive 35 km north of Shantou to Qianmei Village to discover the Chen Cihong Residence, a 506-room Hakka-Teochew palace built between 1870 and 1922 by a magnate from the diaspora returning from Thailand. Interior courtyards, gilded wood carvings, European stained glass, frescoes painted in gold leaf—it's the south-China version of the diaolou, even more Baroque. Allow 2 hours, 50 yuan ticket.

Step into Shantou's Tianhou Temple, built in 1879 by Teochew sailors: the main hall, its permanent incense, and votive statues offered by overseas Chinese recall that the Teochew diaspora financed the region's religious life for a century. Mazu, goddess of seafarers, occupies a central place in eastern Guangdong's coastal culture.

activities.text: For your final meal, Lao Ma Gong restaurant in Xiaogongyuan or Xinghu night market: Teochew seafood congee, marinated goose, steamed dumplings stuffed with bamboo shoots, bingfen (chilled fruit jelly). The Teochew are obsessed with product freshness—an obsession shared with the Cantonese but carried to superior refinement. Expect 80 yuan per person.

If you have time before your flight, take the 11-km bridge to Nan'ao Island, accessible since 2015: deserted beaches, Song-era Song Dading Temple, Ming anti-pirate fortifications, and tea plantations. The Teochew hid their treasures here during wars. Allow half a day. Shantou Jieyang Airport serves Beijing, Shanghai, Bangkok.

days_plan.itin_title: Subtropical Southern China in 12 Days: Canton, Hakka Diaspora & Hong Kong Peninsula

visits.text: Drop your bags in Muyu, the gateway town to the reserve, nestled at 1,200 m altitude. Stroll the main street late afternoon, spot the medicinal plant sellers - the region has over 1,000 recorded medicinal species, earning it the nickname "natural medicine park". Sample dried black mushrooms and polygonatum roots, sold in bags for a few yuan.

Dive into the Shennongjia Natural Museum in Songbai - the only regional natural history museum in central China. You'll see 2,000 plant specimens, 400 animal specimens, including the famous albino white monkeys and Roxellane's snub-nosed monkey. Ideal for understanding the ecosystem before heading into the forest. Free entry, closed Mondays.

Head next to the Tianyan scenic zone - "Sky's Eye" - a funnel-shaped karst depression in the heart of the primeval forest. You'll follow a suspended wooden walkway allowing canopy observation from above. You'll probably spot golden monkeys (Roxellane's snub-nosed monkeys) - 1,550 remain in the reserve, exclusively here. Budget 2 hours on site.

Climb to the summit of Shennongding (3,105.4 m) - the "roof of central China". You'll be above the clouds nine days out of ten. Six peaks in the reserve exceed 3,000 m. The ascent combines shuttle, wooden stairs and aerial walkways. If the Yeren legend - "wild man", China's Bigfoot equivalent - intrigues you, official research was conducted here in the 1970s-80s. No proof to date. Bring warm clothes: it's 10°C cooler than below.

Ask your hotel to arrange a trip to Banbiyan, "mid-height cliff", the likeliest observation point for wild Roxellane's snub-nosed monkeys. Rangers know the groups habituated to human presence. Budget 200 yuan for 3h with a local guide. Patience required, binoculars useful. Best window: 7-10am.

Descend next to Dajiuhu National Wetland Park, at 1,700 m altitude. You'll find nine small connected lakes across 36 km², spectacular morning mists and rich biodiversity. A 5 km boardwalk trail. Local horses graze freely among the ponds.

Venture into Yantian scenic zone, northwest of the reserve: 926 km² of virgin forest almost entirely empty of visitors. You're walking where researchers collected the first specimens of Hubei's subtropical flora in the 1980s. Frozen waterfalls in winter, streams and karst caves. Wild area, minimal facilities, don't go alone.

Reach Honghua Lake mid-day, nestled in a basin at 1,600 m. You'll discover turquoise water, perfect reflections of the peaks, and in May the rhododendrons in bloom that gave the lake its name. 8 km loop hike, quiet on weekdays, perfect for a picnic. Accessible in 1h drive from Muyu.

Climb late afternoon to Shennong Temple, on the peak of the same name - a small sanctuary rebuilt in the 2000s but a pilgrimage site for 2,000 years. It honors the "Divine Farmer" who supposedly invented agriculture and Chinese medicine. According to mythology, Shennong tasted 365 medicinal plants here - and died poisoned by the 366th.

activities.text: For your first steps in the reserve, follow the Tianshengqiao trail - "heavenly bridge" - a natural limestone arch with an 80 m span overlooking a torrent. Easy 4 km loop, 2h round trip, accessible without a guide. Gorgeous late afternoon when light passes under the arch.

Dine on Hubei-style hot pot - spicy broth, wild mushrooms, mountain lamb. The specialty here is Shennongjia black chicken, an endemic free-range breed. The best restaurants cluster around Muyu's main square.

Hire a naturalist for a 3-4h walk in the primeval forest - the world's only subtropical evergreen ecosystem so well preserved. You'll see dove trees (Davidia involucrata), endemic species discovered in 1869 by French missionary Jean-Pierre Armand David, and wild ginkgos, a living species for over 270 million years. Guide rate: 400 yuan half-day.

In the evening, some Muyu lodges organize stargazing sessions - light pollution is zero in the reserve. If the sky is clear, the Milky Way is visible to the naked eye. Chat with locals too: almost everyone has a Yeren story to tell, true or not.

A more committed day hike awaits on the Banbiyan-Jinhouling trail - 12 km, 600 m elevation gain, 6h round trip. Route through giant bamboo forest, observation points for snub-nosed monkeys. Guide essential (500 yuan per day). Bring 2 L water and snacks.

Before leaving the region, visit a local beekeeping operation. Cliff beekeeping has been practiced here for 3,000 years: wooden hives are hung 50 m above ground to protect the swarms. Taste wild high-altitude flower honey - dark, dense, mineral notes. Budget 80-150 yuan per jar.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Hubei & Hunan - 14 days through Wudang, Shennongjia and Miao valleys
Deep Hubei & Hunan - 14 days through Wudang, Shennongjia and Miao valleys
Deep Hubei & Hunan - 14 days through Wudang, Shennongjia and Miao valleys

visits.text: You start strong with the Shenyang Imperial Palace (Mukden Palace), often nicknamed the "Little Forbidden City." Built from 1625 by Nurhaci, founder of the Qing Dynasty, it housed the first three Manchu emperors before Beijing took over in 1644. The complex contains 114 buildings and roughly 300 rooms - twelve times smaller than the Forbidden City but far more intimate. Look for the Dazheng Hall, an octagonal structure inspired by a Manchu tent. Inscribed as a UNESCO site in 2004. Closed Monday mornings.

Head to Beiling Park, 9 km north, which shelters the Zhaoling Tomb of Emperor Hong Taiji (died 1643). The burial mound sits within a massive park that freezes over in winter, where locals come to ice-skate on the lake and sled down hills. The avenue of carved stone animals leads to the tomb itself, guarded by snow-dusted stone lions. This is one of the Three Imperial Tombs of Manchuria classified as UNESCO heritage. Allow two hours to explore comfortably.

The Marshal Zhang Zuolin Residence is worth visiting in the city center. This warlord, nicknamed the "Tiger of Manchuria," ruled the region before being assassinated by the Japanese in 1928 in a bomb attack on the railway. His mansion blends traditional Chinese architecture with European style - a rarity. The rooms have been restored with his original furniture.

activities.text: Dive into Xita, Shenyang's Korean Quarter - one of the most authentic in China outside Yanbian. Storefronts are in Hangeul, Korean barbecues smoke at all hours, and vendors sell homemade kimchi. Try naengmyeon (cold noodles) or bibimbap at one of the family-run restaurants. At night, the red neon lighting creates an atmosphere you won't find anywhere else in China.

If you've got the energy, finish on Zhongjie pedestrian street, lit up for Chinese New Year. Try tanghulu (candied fruit skewers) and bingtang chuan'er, meant to be eaten at -15°C - paradoxically, the cold keeps the caramel from sticking to your teeth. This street is home to the city's best traditional Manchu restaurants.

days_plan.itin_title: Winter Manchuria - snow, sacred volcano and North Korean border

visits.text: Take a turn along Lover's Road, Zhuhai's 28-km coastal promenade facing the South China Sea, where the Fisher Girl statue in granite stands—sculpted in 1982 and now the city's modern symbol. You arrive via the Gongbei border crossing from Macau, one of the world's busiest—up to 350,000 crossings per day. Unobstructed views of Macau in the distance and the HZMB bridge stretching toward Hong Kong.

Shenzhen grew from 30,000 inhabitants in 1980, when Deng Xiaoping designated it China's first Special Economic Zone, to over 17 million today—the world's youngest megacity. Lianhuashan Park offers the best overview: climb to the summit to see Ping An Finance Centre (599 m, the world's 4th-tallest tower) rising from a skyscraper forest. At the summit, a bronze statue of Deng faces the skyline he engendered.

Reserve 3-4 hours for Splendid China and the Folk Culture Village, opened in 1989: at 1:15 scale, you traverse the Great Wall, the Forbidden City, the rice terraces of Yunnan, and 100 iconic monuments. The Folk Culture Village adds ethnic performances (Miao, Dai, Tibetans, Uyghurs). It's unabashedly kitsch but a solid synthesis for those who won't cover the whole country. Combined ticket around 200 yuan.

Drop by Sea World in Shekou, the old port district born in the 1980s around the Ming Hua, a French ocean liner purchased by Deng Xiaoping in 1983 and converted into a hotel-restaurant—it presides over the square's center. The district today gathers international restaurants, terraces, English-language bookshops, and expats. It's Shenzhen's other face, more cosmopolitan and relaxed. An hour's stroll at dusk.

activities.text: Take the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge shuttle bus, or step back to view it: 55 km of viaducts and underwater tunnels opened on October 24, 2018—the world's longest sea-crossing bridge-tunnel. The 6.7-km immersed tunnel at 45 meters depth passes beneath navigation corridors for the world's largest container ships. The viewpoint on the Zhuhai side offers the best perspective. Pure engineering prowess. Late afternoon, HSR train from Zhuhai to Shenzhen in 1.5 hours.

OCT-LOFT is a former 1980s industrial complex reconverted into a contemporary art district in 2004. Galleries, independent bookshops, architecture studios, microbreweries, and design cafés occupy the red-brick warehouses. The Shenzhen Hong Kong Bi-City Biennale of Urbanism\Architecture is held here every other year. Ideal for half a day, magnificent light in late afternoon.

Huaqiangbei is the world's largest electronics market, nicknamed China's Silicon Valley. Across several pedestrian streets and entire shopping centers (Huaqiang Electronic World, SEG Plaza), you'll find everything: bare components, drones, smartwatches, unreleased prototypes. Even without buying anything, the experience is dizzying. Avoid smartphone scams, negotiate everything, pay via WeChat or Alipay.

days_plan.itin_title: Subtropical Southern China in 12 Days: Canton, Hakka Diaspora & Hong Kong Peninsula
Subtropical Southern China in 12 Days: Canton, Hakka Diaspora & Hong Kong Peninsula

visits.text: Shigatse is Tibet's second city and seat of the Panchen Lama. Tashilhunpo Monastery (扎什伦布寺) was founded in 1447 by the 1st Dalai Lama, Gendun Drup. Its red and white walls extend over 15 hectares at the foot of Tara Hill. The 10th Panchen Lama resided here until his death in 1989. The main hall houses silk thangkas from the Ming period and the 1st Panchen Lama's ceremonial saddle.

Jamkhang Chenmo Chapel houses the 26-meter golden Maitreya, built in 1914 and covered with 279kg of gold. Pilgrims circle its base three times in silence—the head alone measures 4.2 meters. The building's low ceiling makes any perspective impossible: just this mass of gold rising above you.

Shigatse Dzong (Samdruptse) dominates the city from a hill. Rebuilt in the 2000s on the ruins of the original fortress destroyed during the Cultural Revolution, it's now partly a museum. The view over the Yarlung Tsangpo Valley and Tashilhunpo Monastery from its terraces is remarkable.

Lunch stop in Shigatse on the return from Rongbuk—3 hours drive. One last hour in the Tibetan market alleys behind Tashilhunpo, with yak butter traders and metal artisans, before taking the road back to Lhasa.

activities.text: Shigatse market is more authentic and less touristy than Lhasa's Barkhor. Tibetan merchants sell metal objects, textiles, yak butter in blocks, and spices. It's the circuit's best place to buy real souvenirs at reasonable prices.

Prepare for tomorrow's climb: Rongbuk and Everest Base Camp are 300km away, via Tingri, with passes to cross. Verify with your guide that Alien Travel Permits for the Everest zone are in order—they won't let you go without them.
Last souvenir shopping at Shigatse market—more authentic and less touristy than Barkhor. Good timing before the 3-hour drive to Lhasa eats up the afternoon.

days_plan.itin_title: From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days
From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days

visits.text: Stop in Shiyan, a necessary staging town between Wudang and Shennongjia - without it, you'd face 6 hours of forest roads in one go. Visit the Shiyan Museum, which displays Cretaceous dinosaur fossils discovered in the region plus Han and Chu artifacts. Small, rarely visited by foreigners, but a good cultural breather before the long drive. Free entry, closed Mondays.

Push on to the old town of Yunyang, north of Shiyan: traditional gray-brick houses, cobbled alleys, and above all the 2nd-century BC Han imperial stele, one of Hubei's oldest stone inscriptions. One-hour stop, lunch on a local noodle dish (rou jia mo with Hubei spices).

activities.text: Late afternoon, climb the switchback road toward the pass separating Shiyan from Shennongjia. Stop at Bayan Pass viewpoint: view over the Qinba ranges stretching in blue waves. Photos guaranteed at sunset. Budget 30 minutes before tackling the final section.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Hubei & Hunan - 14 days through Wudang, Shennongjia and Miao valleys

visits.text: Welcome to the "Alps of Sichuan." You arrive facing four peaks standing in a row, including Yaomei Feng at 6,250m, nicknamed the Queen of Sichuan Mountains. Legend tells of four sisters transformed into mountains to fight a demon—one for each peak. You'll stay in Rilong (3,200m), the base village. At sunset, look up: the range glows pink, then purple.

Tackle Shuangqiao (Double Bridge) Valley, the most accessible of the park's three valleys. You'll ride an electric shuttle bus along 30 km with fifteen stops punctuating the route: Mermaid Bridge, Yak Meadow, Five-Color Lake. Budget 4-5 hours to hit the main stops. At 3,800m max elevation, breath comes short—move slowly.

Hike Changping (Long Bridge) Valley, the hikers' playground. You'll follow 18 km of wooden boardwalk tracing the river to Lama Monastery, hidden among centuries-old pines. You'll pass yaks, prayer wheels, and white stupas. The full walk takes 5-6 hours round-trip—you can hire a horse for 200 yuan if needed.

Tucked at 3,550m in the heart of Changping, Lama Monastery welcomes you to a modest but living Gelugpa temple. Dating from the 19th century, Yellow Hat school, a handful of monks still reside here. Murals depict the protective deities Mahakala and Yamantaka. Light a yak butter lamp, lay a white kata on the altar.

For seasoned hikers, tackle Haizi (Lakes) Valley, which climbs to 4,400m toward Dahaizi and Huahaizi lakes. You'll take a 14 km trail one-way, brutal elevation gain—reserved for those with no altitude issues. Clear views of all four peaks on a good day. Better to hire a horse (300 yuan) for the ascent and walk down.

activities.text: Try horseback riding with the Jiarong Tibetan herders of Rilong. For 200-300 yuan per person, you'll ride with a local guide (often a village teenager) into the high pastures up to 4,000m. Sichuan horses are small, sturdy, reliable—even for beginners. Count on 3-4 hours round-trip. Bring warm clothes; the wind blows hard.

If you're there on a festival night, Rilong village hosts guozhuang evenings—Tibetan circle dances around a bonfire, accompanied by songs. Locals will pull you by the hand to join the circle. It's free, usually from June to September. Accept a bowl of warm barley beer (qingke)—it warms you right up.

Taste Jiarong cuisine with a family in Rilong. Book through your guesthouse the day before—for 80-120 yuan per person, you'll be served salted yak butter, momos (Tibetan dumplings stuffed with yak), butter tea, and tsampa (roasted barley kneaded into dough). Hosts eat with you, translation by gestures. Prepare yourself for rancid butter—that's the authentic taste.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Western Sichuan - Kham, Aba, and Forgotten Valleys in 17 days
Deep Western Sichuan - Kham, Aba, and Forgotten Valleys in 17 days

visits.text: Songpan, an ancient Ming-dynasty fortified town at 2,850 meters elevation, preserves three monumental gates and stone ramparts that straddle the Min River. The town was a strategic trading post on the tea route between Han China and Tibet—merchants exchanged tea, salt, horses, and furs here. The streets retain a Han, Tibetan, Qiang, and Hui mix.

The covered bridge at the North Gate spans the Min River between two watchtowers. The encircling walls, 6.2 km long, were built under Emperor Hongwu's reign (14th century) to protect this key position at the empire's edge.

activities.text: Browse Songpan's market: Tibetan medicinal herbs (including the famous caterpillar fungus—yartsa gunbu), wild honey, dried yak cheese, and Qiang products. The town serves as a base for treks to Huanglong and Jiuzhaigou.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou

places.name: Suzhou

visits.text: The Humble Administrator's Garden (拙政园), built in 1509 during the Ming Dynasty by disgraced official Wang Xianchen, is Suzhou's largest classical garden - 5 hectares, two-thirds covered in water. Pavilions seem to float between ponds and lotus flowers. UNESCO-listed in 1997. Arrive at 7:30am to beat the crowds.

The neighboring Suzhou Museum, designed by I.M. Pei and opened in 2006, is itself a masterpiece. Born in Suzhou, Pei reinterpreted garden architecture in refined modernism: white walls, dark angles, geometric roofs. Collections of bronzes, ceramics, and Ming-Qing paintings. Free admission.

Lion Grove Garden (狮子林), built in 1342 as a retreat for Zen Buddhist monks, is famous for its labyrinth of Taihu rock formations - dozens of caves and passages that seem from another planet. Easy to get lost. The painter Ni Zan stayed here in the 14th century and created several scrolls depicting it.

Pingjiang Road (平江路) is the old town's best-preserved lane - one kilometer along Pingjiang Canal, white facades and gray tiled roofs, arched bridges, silk shops and cafes in interior courtyards. Come in late afternoon when the low-angle light plays on the water.

The Master of the Nets Garden (网师园), founded in 1180 during the Southern Song Dynasty, is Suzhou's smallest classical garden - less than half a hectare - and probably the most refined. Its designer Shi Zhengzhi multiplied depth effects so each meter seems doubled. A design masterpiece to be savored slowly.

Tiger Hill (虎丘) rises above the supposed tomb of King He Lü, founder of Suzhou in the 5th century BCE. At the 36-meter summit, Yunyan Pagoda (961 CE) leans 2.3 degrees - the Chinese call it "the Leaning Tower of the East." Come in the morning before the tour groups arrive.

The Lingering Garden (留园, Liuyuan) is one of China's four greatest gardens. Its distinctive feature: a 700-meter covered gallery connected by over 200 openings cut in varied shapes (vase, leaf, fan) that frame the garden like moving paintings.

Shantang Street (山塘街) runs 3.5 kilometers along the canal dredged by poet-governor Bai Juyi in the 9th century. Called "China's first ancient street" by local tradition, it lines up silk shops, shadow puppet theaters, and teahouses. In the evening, red lanterns on the water create the effect of an animated woodblock print.

activities.text: Visit a silk workshop: Suzhou was for centuries China's leading silk-producing city. Several factories offer tours with weaving demonstrations and double-sided embroidery - a technique visible from both sides with no reverse, a national specialty.
Dine on the lanes around Guanqian Street (观前街), Suzhou's gastronomic artery. Try: zhurou tongyuan (glutinous rice balls with pork), shumo (crab dumplings), eight-treasure rice soup.

Attend an evening kunqu opera performance in one of the gardens. Kunqu is China's oldest opera form, born in Suzhou in the 16th century - it preceded and influenced Beijing Opera. Intimate chamber performances in pavilions: intimate setting, natural acoustics.
Take an evening boat ride on Suzhou's canals from Shantang pier. The navigation passes illuminated facades and goes under stone bridges. Duration about 1 hour.

days_plan.itin_title: Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui
Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

map_keywords: Master of the Nets Garden Suzhou

visits.text: Tagong (塔公) sits on a plateau at 3,700m amid grasslands of an almost surreal green. Tagong Monastery dates to the 14th century and belongs to the Sakya school—it houses a replica of Lhasa's famous Jowo statue, offered according to legend by Princess Wencheng during her passage in the 7th century. A dozen white stupas encircle the main building. Monks come and go between buildings with cell phones in their pockets—21st-century Tibet.

From Tagong, on clear days you can see Mount Yala (雅拉神山), a sacred peak rising to 5,820m. For the region's nomads, it's the king of Kham's mountains—no climbing expeditions are permitted. The prayer flags covering the heights around the village are dedicated to it. Sit in the grasslands and look—you probably won't need to do anything else.

Xinduqiao (新都桥), 40 minutes from Tagong on the G318, is nicknamed photographers' paradise. The Tibetan village is surrounded by golden poplars (in autumn) and rolling hills. Stop for 30 minutes: the early afternoon light on the golden roofs and black stone houses is remarkable.

activities.text: Horseback ride through Tagong grasslands—local Khampa guides offer 1-3 hour outings on their plateau horses. It's the only way to truly feel the immensity of these pastures where yak herds graze as far as the eye can see.

Settle into a Tibetan guesthouse in Tagong for the night. Most are run by local families—the welcome is simple, the yak blankets thick. Step outside after dark: at 3,700m, far from any light pollution, the sky is dazzling.

days_plan.itin_title: From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days
Deep Western Sichuan - Kham, Aba, and Forgotten Valleys in 17 days

visits.text: Train from Jinan in 17 minutes—that's how long it takes to reach Tai'an, the gateway city to Mount Tai. Before the ascent, devote your morning to Dai Temple (岱庙). Built under the Han, expanded under the Tang then the Song, this 96,000 m² Taoist complex is the starting point of the imperial pilgrimage. Here emperors came to purify themselves before the Feng Shan ceremony, the ritual through which they communicated with Heaven to legitimize their mandate. Spot the Bronze Pavilion and the Iron Tower.

After the temple, tackle the ascent via the imperial East route, the path emperors climbed on horseback or in sedan chairs. Hongmen Gate (Red Portal Gate) marks the entrance. You'll pass Doumu Palace (Palace of the Mother of the Dipper), stelae, and calligraphic inscriptions carved directly into the rock for over 2,000 years. In total, more than 1,200 rock inscriptions line the ascent. Allow 4 to 6 hours to the summit depending on your fitness.

Midway, pause at Wanxianlou Pavilion ("Ten Thousand Immortals") and Doumu Temple. If your legs give out, the Zhongtianmen cable car (Midheaven Gate) covers the second half in 7 minutes. Otherwise, continue on foot to the famous South Heavenly Gate (Nantianmen)—the finest panoramic view of the climb, very close to the summit.

activities.text: Sleep at the summit for the sunrise—this is THE Mount Tai ritual for the past 3,000 years. Several rustic hotels sit near the Jade Emperor Peak (1,545 m). Prepare yourself: temperature drops sharply at night even in summer. All hotels rent thick military coats for 30 yuan. Wake-up 4-5am to reach the promontory facing east. If the weather is clear, the spectacle is unforgettable; if not, you'll be in a sea of clouds.

Locals say it: you climb Tai Shan only once in your life, but you talk about it forever. Before sunset, stroll the summit between the Temple of the Azure Cloud Princess and the Wordless Stele, erected by Emperor Wudi of Han (140-87 BC) who judged the mountain too sublime for words. It's one of the most moving sites at the summit.

days_plan.itin_title: Shandong: 11 Days from Confucius to Mount Tai and the German Coast

visits.text: Explore the Stone Fortress ruins at Tashkurgan, an ancient citadel perched at 3,200 meters altitude. This frontier outpost guarded the mountain passes connecting China to the kingdoms of Central Asia for over two millennia.

Discover Tashkurgan's Tajik community, an ethnic minority with Persian roots distinct from both Uyghurs and Han Chinese. Their colorful traditional dress, music, and hospitality reflect ancient connections to the Iranian world.

Drive the Panlong zigzag road, an engineering marvel with 600 hairpin turns ascending through dramatic high-altitude scenery near the Pakistan border-one of the world's most spectacular mountain drives.

activities.text: Visit a Tajik village to witness traditional eagle hunting demonstrations, a practice connecting these mountain people to nomadic cultures across the Eurasian steppes.

days_plan.itin_title: The Silk Road Overland: Xi'an to Istanbul via Uzbekistan & Turkmenistan

visits.text: Tingri (仲巴) is a town without particular interest, but it marks the entrance to the Everest zone. From surrounding passes, on clear days, you glimpse Everest's black pyramid (Qomolangma in Tibetan, "Goddess Mother of the World") and Cho Oyu. It's a mandatory stop for acclimatization before ascending to Rongbuk at 5,019m.

activities.text: Lunch in Tingri and observation of the Himalayan panorama from a restaurant terrace. It's the last stage before altitude exceeds 5,000m again. Drink water, sleep if possible during the break.

days_plan.itin_title: From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days

visits.text: Tonghua serves as a logical stopover before ascending to Changbaishan. The city nestles in a steep valley and its mountains are covered with frozen birches. Make a detour to the Yang Jingyu Martyrs Cemetery, a Chinese resistance general against Japanese occupation killed in 1940. His story is famous throughout China: the Japanese found his stomach filled with bark and cotton.

If you're curious, the Tonghua Wine Museum recounts another surprising chapter of history: the valley has produced red wines since the Qing era, and in the 1930s-40s, production was taken over by the Japanese who exported to Tokyo. Tonghua wine remains one of China's oldest labels. Tasting available.

days_plan.itin_title: Winter Manchuria - snow, sacred volcano and North Korean border

visits.text: Tongli (同里) is the region's most elegant water town. Tuisi Garden (退思园), UNESCO-listed in 2000, was designed in 1885 by Ren Lansheng, a disgraced official - its name means "reflecting while retiring." Unique feature: the buildings descend eastward following a progression toward the water, as if the entire composition were sliding into the pond.

Tongli's canals connect five islands - a configuration unique in the region. Three historic bridges (Taiping, Jili, Changqing) span the waters face-to-face: newlyweds would cross them in a precise order to ensure happiness, fortune, and longevity. The tradition lives on.

activities.text: Take a ride in a round-roofed boat through the canals - the navigation passes under bridges and alongside houses built directly on the water. The boatman paddles standing at the stern, traditional style.

days_plan.itin_title: Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

visits.text: Longwu (Rongwo) Monastery, founded in 1301, is the largest monastery in Rebkong and the third most important in the Amdo region. Originally of the Sakya tradition, it was later converted to the Gelugpa school. Around 400 monks reside here in a labyrinth of prayer halls with walls covered in murals and gilded statues.

The upper and lower Wutun villages, 10 km from Tongren, are the birthplace of Regong art—thangka painting, sculpture, embroidery, and architectural decoration inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list since 2009. Nearly every household is a workshop. Artists use mineral pigments (gold, silver, turquoise, malachite, saffron, indigo) that keep colors from fading for centuries.

The Regong Art Museum in Tongren (free admission) displays over 2,000 pieces from the 11th century to the present: thangkas, sculptures, embroideries. The collection traces the evolution of this art form, which blends Indian, Nepalese, and Chinese influences into a style unique to Amdo.

activities.text: Visit a thangka workshop in Wutun village. Master painters work with a two-hair brush for the finest details, following the strict proportions of the Buddhist "Lection of Measurement." A small thangka requires at least a month; large pieces, several years.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou

places.name: Tunxi

visits.text: Tunxi's Old Street (老街) stretches 1.3 km along the Xin'an River: continuous Ming-Qing facades, antique shops, tea and traditional painting merchants. This is where collectors from all over China come to find paintings from the Xin'an school and Huizhou inkstones. A final easy stroll before the return flight from TXN airport (1h to Shanghai).

activities.text: Final stop at an Old Street teahouse to taste Keemun tea (祁门红茶) - Anhui's most renowned black tea, produced 60 km away in Qimen County. Also a good moment to buy Chinese ink, traditional brushes, or Xin'an school paintings.

days_plan.itin_title: Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

map_keywords: Old Street Laojie Tunxi Huangshan City

visits.text: The ancient city of Jiaohe (Jiāohé Gùchéng), 10 km west of Turpan, is the world's largest and best-preserved rammed-earth city. Perched on a 30-meter-high leaf-shaped loess plateau between two ravines of dried rivers, it served as a natural defense—no ramparts needed. Founded in the 2nd century BC as the capital of the Cheshi kingdom, it prospered as a Silk Road relay during the Tang dynasty (7th-10th century), when it housed the Protectorate of the Western Regions, the highest Chinese authority in Central Asia. The complex covers 370,000 m², with a central avenue, residential quarters, a palace, and a Buddhist temple complex. Destroyed by the Mongols in the 13th century, the site was preserved by Turpan's extremely dry climate. UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2014.

The Karez irrigation system (Kǎnérjǐng), over 2,000 years old, is an underground canal network that channels meltwater from the Tian Shan Mountains to the Turpan oasis across the desert, without evaporation. The Turpan area has about 1,100 karez over a total network of roughly 5,000 km of tunnels—considered one of ancient China's three great engineering works along with the Great Wall and the Grand Canal. At a dedicated museum, you descend 10-50 meters underground to see the canals, access wells, and understand this gravity irrigation principle.

The Emin Minaret (Sūgōng Tǎ), 3 km east of Turpan, is China's tallest Islamic tower at 44 meters. Built in 1777 from raw clay bricks, its conical shaft is decorated with 15 different geometric patterns forming diamonds, waves, and flowers—all in earth, without a single nail or piece of wood.

The Flaming Mountains (Huǒyàn Shān), 30 km east of Turpan, are a 100-km-long chain of eroded red sandstone whose gullies and striations give the illusion of flames under the sun. Famous throughout China thanks to the classic 16th-century novel Journey to the West, where the Monkey King Sun Wukong crosses them with a magic fan. Ground temperature can exceed 80°C in summer—Turpan, in a depression 154 meters below sea level, is China's hottest city.

The Tuyok Valley (Tǔyùgōu), nestled in a Flaming Mountains gorge, shelters a traditional Uyghur pisé village where time seems suspended. Raw clay brick houses, grape trellises drying on rooftops, and winding alleys compose an intact landscape. Above the village, Buddhist caves carved between the 5th and 9th centuries preserve fresco fragments. The site is also a Muslim pilgrimage destination—some consider it a "little Mecca."

The Bezeklik Thousand Buddha Caves (Bǎizīkèlǐkè), in a canyon at the foot of the Flaming Mountains, number 77 caves carved between the 5th and 14th centuries. Their frescoes blend Indian Buddhist, Chinese, and Uyghur influences. Many were damaged by Muslim iconoclasts in the 15th century, then cut out and carried off by European explorers in the early 20th century—fragments now reside in Berlin's museum and St. Petersburg's Hermitage.

activities.text: Day 1: Drive through the legendary Flaming Mountains, spectacular red sandstone formations stretching over 100 km—China's hottest place and setting of a famous Chinese legend featuring a monkey king and a wise monk.
Day 2: Hike through the Flaming Mountains gorges from Tuyok, a challenging 5-hour trek crossing the red sandstone massif to emerge into landscapes of extraordinary geological drama.

Day 1: Drive through the legendary Flaming Mountains, spectacular red sandstone formations stretching over 100 km—China's hottest place and setting of a famous Chinese legend featuring a monkey king and a wise monk.
Day 2: Hike through the Flaming Mountains gorges from Tuyok, a challenging 5-hour trek crossing the red sandstone massif to emerge into landscapes of extraordinary geological drama.

days_plan.itin_title: The Silk Road Overland: Xi'an to Istanbul via Uzbekistan & Turkmenistan
China's Silk Road: Xi'an to Kashgar via the Hexi Corridor
China's Silk Road: Xi'an to Kashgar via the Hexi Corridor

visits.text: The Xinjiang Regional Museum in Urumqi displays the Tarim Basin mummies, 3,800 years old, including the famous "Beauty of Loulan" with her Caucasian features—proof of exchanges between eastern and western populations long before the Silk Road's official opening. Collections cover the cultures of Xinjiang's 13 ethnic groups (Uyghurs, Kazakhs, Kyrgyz, Mongols, etc.) through textiles, musical instruments, and everyday objects.

Heavenly Lake (Tiānchí), 115 km east of Urumqi in the Tian Shan Mountains, sits at 1,910 meters altitude. This 3.4-km-long, 1.5-km-wide glacial lake is framed by spruce forests and dominated by Bogda Peak, which rises to 5,445 meters. Kazakh herders pitch their yurts here in summer. The site, important to Taoism and Buddhism, is listed as UNESCO World Heritage as part of the Tian Shan network.

Red Hill (Hóngshān), a small park in Urumqi's center, offers views over the city and the Tian Shan Mountains in the background. At the summit, a 10.6-meter red brick pagoda built in 1788 during the Qing dynasty was meant to prevent the mythical dragon of the Urumqi River from causing floods.

activities.text: Stroll through the Erdaoqiao International Grand Bazaar, Central Asian China's largest covered market. You'll find carpets, Uyghur knives, dried fruits (Turpan raisins, walnuts, apricots), spices, and musical instruments. Food stalls serve laghman (Uyghur hand-pulled noodles), samsa (meat pastries), and nan (tandoor-baked bread).

Final stop at Urumqi's dried fruit and spice market. Xinjiang produces China's finest raisins, dried apricots, walnuts, and figs—the region's dry, sunny climate gives them high sugar concentration. Free time before departure flight.

days_plan.itin_title: China's Silk Road: Xi'an to Kashgar via the Hexi Corridor
China's Silk Road: Xi'an to Kashgar via the Hexi Corridor
Northern Xinjiang - Kanas, Altai, and Ili Steppes in 16 Days

visits.text: Welcome to Wanning, China's surf capital. You arrive at Riyue Bay ("Sun-Moon"), a Chinese stop on the World Surf League Qualifying Series - 2025 edition December 11-17, over 160 surfers from more than ten countries. Consistent waves, sandy bottom, local surf school. Waves are best November through March, when the northeast swell is consistent.

Thirty kilometers north, you'll discover Shimei Bay ("stone beauty"), which draws a perfect crescent of white sand for 9 km. It's one of the island's most beautiful beaches, still relatively unspoiled. Several luxury hotels (Banyan Tree, Le Méridien) have placed their villas here, but beach access remains public. Turquoise water as far as the eye can see, ideal for a morning at the beach before sightseeing.

Nanwan Monkey Island, 50 km south, is home to roughly 1,500 Chinese macaques. You access it via a 2,138 m cable car over the sea, inaugurated in 1996, which was long the longest of its kind in China. The monkeys are semi-wild, very curious, even aggressive if you brandish food. Don't wear sunglasses on your head, they'll steal them. Scientific reserve since 1965.

activities.text: Take a surf lesson at Riyue Bay (2 hours, board and wetsuit provided). Several PADI-certified schools on the beach, English-speaking instructors. Beginner to intermediate level - waves break gently, perfect for learning to stand up. The best session for you is 7:30 am, before wind and crowds.

Late afternoon, walk along Shimei Bay seawall at sunset. Light shifts from gold to pink in fifteen minutes, the sky reflects in the shallow water. A few small grilled fish restaurants set up tables on the sand - order local grouper with salt and lime.

days_plan.itin_title: Tropical Hainan: 10 Days Around China's Southern Island

visits.text: High-speed train Linyi-Weifang in 1 hour 15 minutes. Upon arrival, head to the World Kite Museum. The building itself is worth the detour: its roof reproduces the shape of a dragon-headed centipede kite, in peacock-blue glazed tiles. Twelve galleries trace 2,000 years of kite history—invention attributed to philosopher Mo Zi, who allegedly spent three years making the first wooden model. Over 1,000 kites on display, Chinese and foreign, including a 300-meter centipede prize-winner in Italy.

Direction Yangjiabu Folk Village, 30 minutes by car. Here is still manufactured today a large portion of Chinese kites—about 5 million per year. The village is also famous for its Nianhua woodblock prints (New Year prints) that have adorned peasant house doors since the Ming. You can witness kite-making: stretching, gluing, painting, flying—the "four arts" of the kite-maker.

activities.text: If you travel in April, jackpot: the International Kite Festival has been held annually since 1984, opening the 3rd Saturday of April for a fortnight. It's the oldest Chinese local festival recognized internationally. Over 30 countries participate and you'll see wild kites—50-meter octopuses, 300-m dragons, giant Hello Kittys. The main site is in the Binhai coastal zone, 50 km from the center.

Kite-making workshop at Yangjiabu: for 80 yuan, you leave with your own hand-painted kite. Allow 1 hour between painting, drying, and test-flying above the village. A far more meaningful souvenir than a t-shirt.

days_plan.itin_title: Shandong: 11 Days from Confucius to Mount Tai and the German Coast

visits.text: Take the express train to Wenchang (50 min from Haikou East), birthplace of the Chinese diaspora to Southeast Asia. First stop: Dongjiao coconut grove, China's largest. Over 20 km of coastline planted with coconut palms, some 30-50 years old, alongside shaded bike paths. Free entry. Rent a bike at the entrance, allow 2 hours for the ride. Caribbean atmosphere, flip-flops and fresh coconut mandatory.

Head to Longlou Town, 30 minutes by car, where the Yaoguang viewing platform lets you watch rocket launches from Wenchang Space Launch Center - off-limits to foreigners. Inaugurated in May 2025, the platform covers 19,000 m² and accommodates up to 2,000 spectators during Long March 5 and 7 liftoffs. The center, operational since 2016, is China's only coastal spaceport (just 19° north latitude, ideal for launches). Check the launch schedule before going.

Ten kilometers north, you'll find Tongguling Nature Reserve, home to "eastern Hainan's first peak," an ancient volcano collapsed into the sea. Trails through subtropical forest, sweeping views over the east coast bays. Nearby Wenchang Stone Park features spectacular marine erosion formations along 2 km of coastline. Bonus: it's one of eight recommended sites for watching official launches.

Downtown, detour to Wenchang Confucius Temple, founded under the Song and rebuilt in 1042, one of the best-preserved in southern China. You'll find red courtyards, lion statues, and red lacquered wooden gates that still host spring examination rituals today. Wenchang historically produced more imperial officials than any other Hainan city.

activities.text: Lunch on Wenchang chicken in its home village. The chicken is free-range raised in coconut groves, fed on fermented coconut and small fish - hence its particularly rich meat. A typical Tianci district restaurant (part of Wenchang) has served the dish for three generations.

If luck is with you, witness a rocket launch from Yaoguang platform. A Long March 5 liftoff - roughly 869 tons at lift-off - produces a deep rumble felt for several kilometers. White light, orange trail, vibrations in your chest according to eyewitnesses. Rare spectacle in China, not to be missed if your visit coincides with a launch.

days_plan.itin_title: Tropical Hainan: 10 Days Around China's Southern Island

places.name: Wolong

visits.text: Make your first stop at Yingxiu, epicenter of the May 12, 2008 earthquake—magnitude 8.0. You'll walk through the ruins of Xuankou Middle School, which collapsed at precisely 2:28pm; the frozen clocks bear witness. More than 87,000 dead or missing across the region. The visit is hard, silent, necessary. Plan 1-1.5 hours. Free entry, open 8am-5pm. Don't photograph local visitors who come to pay their respects.

Head next to Shenshuping in Gengda—you'll find over 90 pandas in 59 enclosures spread across 150 hectares of forest. Far more authentic than Chengdu: the base, opened in 2016, replaced Hetaoping, which was destroyed in the earthquake. Don't miss feeding times: 9:30-10:30am and 2-4pm, when pandas climb trees or roll in the grass.

Finish with the Wolong Panda Museum, 24 km from Shenshuping. You'll trace the species' history over three million years and the impact of the 2008 earthquake. One hour is enough, but the exhibit on captive breeding (between 1986 and 2000, 46 cubs were born here) is fascinating. The section on post-quake reconstruction puts everything you've just seen into perspective.

activities.text: If you've got energy left, lace up your hiking boots for the Five Pavilions Trail (Wuyipeng), at 2,400m elevation. This is where the first WWF research station was installed in 1978. The trail cuts through bamboo forest where wild pandas actually roam—you probably won't see one, but tracks and droppings are common. Two hours round-trip from the road.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Western Sichuan - Kham, Aba, and Forgotten Valleys in 17 days

map_keywords: Xuankou Middle School Earthquake Ruins Yingxiu

visits.text: Enter quietly into Zixiao Palace's main hall (Purple Cloud Palace) - it's Wudang's most complete and best-preserved Taoist sanctuary. You'll find the only fresco of monk Zhang Sanfeng, the legendary founder of taichi, found nowhere else on the mountain. Built under the Ming in the 15th century, expanded under the Qing, the palace still serves as a worship and teaching center. Around a hundred monks live on the massif. 15 yuan entry.

Don't miss Nanyan Palace, literally clinging to the cliff on the south face of Tianzhu Peak - 150 buildings gripping the rock, including 460 rooms from the Ming era. Look for the "stone dragon" jutting 3 meters into the void: pilgrims would place an incense burner on it, a feared balancing challenge for centuries. The valley view is one of Wudang's finest. Plan 90 minutes on site.

Escape the tour groups by climbing to the Five Dragons Temple, Wudang's oldest sanctuary - dating back to the Tang Dynasty (618-907). This is where the first Taoist community settled, under Emperor Taizong. Today's ruins mostly date from the Ming, but the silence of the place is worth the trip. Combine with a walk on the ancient trails - 2 hours round trip from town.

Rise before dawn to reach the Golden Hall atop Tianzhu Peak (1,612 m) - the sea of clouds at sunrise is reason enough. You'll discover a gilded bronze pavilion weighing over 80 tons, built in 1416 under Emperor Yongle, entirely prefabricated in Beijing then hoisted piece by piece up the mountain. The hall measures 5.54 m high, 5.80 m wide and 4.20 m deep. Inside, the bronze statue of Emperor Zhenwu weighs about 10 tons. Entry 27 yuan on top of park ticket.

Walk the complete circuit of Wudang's Forbidden City, which encircles Tianzhu's summit with a 1.5 km stone wall built in 1419 - commissioned by Emperor Yongle who was simultaneously erecting Beijing's Forbidden City. You'll pass through the four cardinal gates, each opening onto a different panorama. The wall runs along vertiginous cliffs. Doable in 45 minutes on foot.

Descend late afternoon toward Yuxu Palace, once Wudang's largest complex with over 2,200 buildings under the Ming. You'll mostly see ruins today, but what remains - the great imperial stele from 1413, massive foundations, the portals - gives an idea of the scale of Yongle's project. Free entry, few people, perfect in evening light.

activities.text: Book an intro taichi session with a local master - Wudang is officially recognized as the birthplace of internal martial arts (neijia quan). Budget a 90-minute session for 200-300 yuan, or two days immersion at Yuan Li Ming's school on the west flank. Early morning (6-7am), young disciples train on Zixiao Palace esplanade: watching the session is free and mesmerizing.

If you're there on a Sunday morning, slip into Zixiao Palace for Taoist rituals - chanting, gongs, incense. Proper attire required, discreet photos, and keep distance from worshippers at prayer. An unforgettable experience, entirely free.

Walk at least one section of the "Sacred Way" linking the main sanctuaries - stone stairs built under the Ming, still used by pilgrims. The most beautiful section connects Zixiao Palace to Nanyan Palace passing through the Stone Gates. Budget 3h up, 2.5h down. Water and walking stick recommended.

In the evening, dine on Taoist vegetarian dishes at a mountain inn. Specialties: Wudang wild mushrooms, bamboo shoots, black soy tofu. Taoist cuisine forbids garlic and onion (thought to stimulate passions). Budget 80 yuan for a full meal.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Hubei & Hunan - 14 days through Wudang, Shennongjia and Miao valleys
Deep Hubei & Hunan - 14 days through Wudang, Shennongjia and Miao valleys

visits.text: Climb the five floors of Yellow Crane Tower right at 8am opening to catch the view over the Yangtze and Wuhan's bridges before the crowds arrive. The pavilion has been destroyed and rebuilt more than twelve times since the 3rd century - today's version dates from 1985. Legend tells of a Taoist immortal who flew away from here on a yellow crane. Skip weekends when it's absolutely packed. Budget 90 minutes to enjoy the panoramic terraces.

Step into the Hubei Provincial Museum for one of the 20th century's major archaeological discoveries: the 65 bronze bells of Marquis Yi of Zeng, cast around 433 BC and still playable today. Don't miss the musical demonstrations (usually at 11am, 2pm and 3pm - confirm at the entrance). The set weighs 2.5 tons and spans five octaves. Free entry but mandatory reservation 24 hours ahead via the museum's WeChat mini-program.

Wander through Wuhan University campus, perched on the slopes of Luojia Hill overlooking East Lake - six times the size of Hangzhou's West Lake, China's largest urban lake. You'll find 1930s Sino-Western buildings and, between late March and early April, 1,000 cherry trees in bloom that draw massive crowds. Off-season, the campus stays peaceful. Walk up to Lao Library Pavilion for the best viewpoint.

Stroll the Hankou Bund at early evening when the neoclassical facades light up. You're walking 1.5 km along the late-19th-century foreign concessions - British, French, German, Russian, Japanese. Stop in front of the 1913 HSBC bank and the 1924 French customs house. The contrast with the modern towers across the river is striking.

Enter Guiyuan Temple, founded in 1658, and hunt among the 500 life-size arhat statues for the one matching your age - local tradition says its expression predicts your year ahead. The temple is still active; you'll pass real monks in meditation. 10 yuan entry, open 8am-5pm. Avoid Chinese public holidays when it's unmanageable.

Head next to the Museum of the Revolution, housed in Sun Yat-sen's former republican government headquarters - this is where the end of the Empire was proclaimed in October 1911. You'll instantly recognize the red colonial building with white columns, now a Wuhan icon. Collections trace the Xinhai Revolution. Free entry, closed Mondays.

activities.text: To taste Wuhan cuisine, head down Hubu Xiang first thing in the morning. The street is famous for re gan mian - "hot-dry noodles" with sesame paste, the iconic local breakfast dish. About 8 yuan per bowl. Also try the dou pi rice fritters and lotus root soups. Skip after 10am when everything's sold out.

Late afternoon, rent a bike near East Lake and loop around Moshan Peninsula - 33 km of shoreline with bike paths separated from traffic. At sunset, local fishermen settle onto the pontoons. Budget 2 hours for the full loop. Bike-share available through the Hello Bike app.

Board an early evening cruise on the Yangtze: 90 minutes between the illuminated banks, passing under bridges with skyline views. Departures from Wuhan Pier every 30 minutes after 7pm. Budget 90 yuan. Bring a windbreaker - the river gets breezy.

The Hubu Xiang quarter hosts a lively night market after 6pm. Specialties to try: fried fermented tofu, Xinjiang lamb skewers, black sesame bao. A real Chinese street food experience - loud, greasy, delicious. Budget 50 yuan for two to sample five or six things.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Hubei & Hunan - 14 days through Wudang, Shennongjia and Miao valleys
Deep Hubei & Hunan - 14 days through Wudang, Shennongjia and Miao valleys

visits.text: Wuhu (芜湖) marks the gateway to deep Anhui on the southern bank of the Yangtze. Guangji Temple (广济寺), founded in 894 during the Tang Dynasty, is nicknamed "Little Jiuhua" - its 88 steps lead to the halls of Maitreya, the Great Pagoda, and the Medicine Buddha. River view from the hill. A brief but solid stop between Jiangsu's China and Anhui's mountains.

Zheshan Park (赭山公园) is Wuhu's emblematic hill, overlooking the Yangtze. The trails that circle it pass historical remnants and offer a panorama of the river and city. Pleasant for a 45-minute walk between drives.

activities.text: Lunch on the Yangtze banks in the Jinghu district - fresh freshwater fish, a regional specialty. Wuhu has been nicknamed "the city of fish and rice" since antiquity.

days_plan.itin_title: Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

visits.text: Bailong Elevator swallows 326 m in 1 min 32 sec against the canyon wall, world's tallest outdoor elevator. 72 yuan for the ascent, alternative to the 2h trail. In high season (Golden Week, Chinese New Year, summer), allow 2-3h queue - arrive 7:30 a.m. or book through guide. Exit leads directly to Yuanjiajie plateau.

Avatar Hallelujah Mountain pillar was officially renamed in 2010: Yuanjiajie inspired James Cameron for the film's Hallelujah Mountains, and China seized the marketing opportunity. 3h loop, First Bridge under Heaven platform spanning two pinnacles. Crowds concentrated midday, plan early or late.

Tianzi Mountain cable car opens panoramic view over Suoxi Canyon from the upper plateau. 67 yuan one way. Allow 30 min queue off-season, 1h30 in high season. Summit plateau covers 2h on foot, flat trails, multiple observation platforms. Red sandstone pillars glow at sunset, exit 6 p.m.

Huangshizhai offers the park's widest panoramic platform, accessible by cable car (72 yuan) or on foot in 2h30 ascent. The most widely distributed photo of Zhangjiajie is taken from the Six Wonders Pavilion viewpoint. If you climb on foot, plan 3L of water and walking sticks. Cable car is cost-effective in high season to save two hours.

Golden Whip Stream winds 5.7 km along a torrent, pinnacles on both sides, flat trail. The park's most contemplative moment: dense forest, green water, little noise. Allow 3-4h round trip. Macaque monkeys at trailside, don't feed them, they steal. Golden light around 4 p.m. in the first half of the gorge.

Six Wonders Pavilion overlooks the true postcard panorama. Platform built on stilts, packed midday. Early morning (8-9 a.m.), sparse crowds, residual mist softening the pinnacles. Allow 30 min on site. Bench, shelter in case of rain, starting point for several secondary trails.

activities.text: Arrive at Bailong at 7:30 a.m. in high season, it's the only slot without a 2h queue. Local guide knows the park's internal buses that avoid saturated cable cars. Hat, water, snacks - interior park restaurants are expensive and mediocre. Bring meal from hotel or Wulingyuan town. Allow 8h on site.

Tujia call *Sansheng pork* their signature dish: fatty belly cooked three times, melt-in-your-mouth, served with scallion and pepper. Try it in Wulingyuan town at dinner, accompanied by sweet potato noodles. Allow 80-120 yuan for two. Restaurants lined up on main street, little English. Point at menu photos.

Climb on foot to Huangshizhai if weather is clear and legs are good: 2h30 ascent, 700 m elevation gain, paved trail. Cable car is the simple option at 72 yuan. Both lead to the same viewpoint. If the day forecasts rain, do Golden Whip Stream in the morning (more protected cover) and Huangshizhai in the afternoon by cable car.

If your legs hold, descend Golden Whip Stream in late afternoon, return around 6 p.m. Exit via South Gate, internal shuttle (free) to Wulingyuan. Sunset in the gorge remains a great moment. Late dinner in Wulingyuan town, early bed - tomorrow's Tianmen day is just as demanding.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi
Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi

visits.text: Tianyou Peak ("Heavenly Tour") is Wuyi Mountain's iconic summit: 848 steps carved into the cliff lead to a viewpoint above the 5th and 6th bends of the Nine Bend River. The panorama over red Danxia sandstone peaks crowned with subtropical vegetation justifies the climb.

The Da Hong Pao area shelters the six mother bushes of this legendary oolong tea, clinging to the cliff of Jiulongke ravine. These plants, estimated at over 350 years old, are World Heritage listed. Since 2006, all harvesting from these mother plants has been prohibited. The Tea Trail (4.5 km) descends through rock walls where hundreds of varieties of rock tea (yancha) grow to Water Curtain Cave.

Thread of Sky (Yixiantian) is a passage between two walls over 50 meters high, so narrow that the tightest point measures only 30 centimeters. You progress sideways, glimpsing a thin line of sky above your head.

Tiger Roaring Rock (Huxiao Yan) owes its name to wind rushing into a giant cavity in the cliff. The trail climbs steeply along the wall via stairs clinging to the rock, with plunging views over the subtropical forest.

Wuyi Palace, at the mountain's foot, was an 11th-century Neo-Confucian center. Philosopher Zhu Xi developed thought here that would influence all of East Asia for centuries. The UNESCO site covers 999 km² and includes ruins of a 1st-century BC Han city.

activities.text: Bamboo raft ride down the Nine Bend River, 9.5 km in about 2 hours. The raft passes between 200 to 400-meter red sandstone cliffs. The raftsman comments on rock formations and legends. The river owes its name to its nine sharp turns between the peaks.

Tea tasting at a teahouse at the foot of Wuyi Mountain. Tea masters prepare Da Hong Pao using the gongfu method: Yixing clay teapot, water at 95-100°C, successive infusions of 10 to 15 seconds. The aromas evolve from infusion to infusion—roasted notes first, then floral and fruity. Wuyi produces over 300 varieties of rock tea.

days_plan.itin_title: Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian
Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian

visits.text: White walls, black roofs shaped like bird wings (*matou qiang*, horse-head walls), carved beams: Huizhou architecture spans 35 villages scattered across Wuyuan County. These villages grew wealthy from the 14th to 19th centuries thanks to Huizhou merchants who controlled tea and salt trade on the Yangtze. Today, they tell the story of pre-industrial merchant China.

Likeng dates back to 1010 under the Song: canals crossing the lanes, arched stone bridges, houses perched on water. First stop on the classic *Eastern Route*. More touristy than neighboring villages, but it's the ideal introduction to understand the architecture. Allow 1h30 on site. Avoid bus groups arriving around 10 a.m.

Wangkou's Yu Xin Hall is a masterpiece of wood carving, beams and lintels elaborately worked. The village also preserves an ancient wharf, testament to Huizhou river trade. Quieter than Likeng, real houses still occupied by their owners. Allow 1h. In the morning, oblique light ideal for photographing interior courtyards.

activities.text: Book a full-day driver through your hotel for the Eastern Route: Likeng, Wangkou, Jiangwan. Allow 300-400 yuan for the day, negotiable. Public transport exists but wastes 2h. Departure 8:30 a.m., return around 5 p.m. Driver waits during visits, simplifies access to more remote villages. Cash or WeChat Pay.

Steamer baskets of *four steamed delicacies* (蒸菜四宝) stack fish, vegetables, ham and bamboo cooked by steam. Wuyuan specialty, simple and just right. Look for *minsu* offering countryside menu at 80-120 yuan. Rice grows locally, fish come from surrounding rivers. No alcohol required, local tea accompanies.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi

places.name: Wuzhen

visits.text: Wuzhen West (西栅, Xizha) is the most successful part of the water town: renovated in the 2000s, it has preserved rare architectural coherence - gray wooden houses, black tiles, covered galleries above the water. In the evening, red lanterns light up along the entire length of the main canal and the water surface becomes a mirror. This is the hour not to miss - ticket valid until 10:30pm.

Writer Mao Dun's house (茅盾故居), in the East Quarter, is the birthplace of the revolutionary novelist (1896-1981), author of Midnight. Opened to the public in 1985, the rooms preserve period furniture - a window into early 20th-century bourgeois Jiangnan life.
