Wuzhen's indigo dyeing workshop is one of the region's oldest. Hundreds of indigo fabric squares dry hanging in garlands above the East Quarter lanes - an unmissable photographic image. The artisan demonstrates the tie-resist process before vat dyeing.

activities.text: Take a covered boat (乌篷船) on Wuzhen's canals in late afternoon to navigate between the houses before sunset. Depart from Xizha's central pier, less crowded than the main dock.
Watch the shadow puppet show (皮影戏) and opera performance in the East Quarter - 20 to 30-minute shows, repeated several times throughout the day. Schedules are posted at the entrance.

days_plan.itin_title: Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

map_keywords: Wuzhen Xizha West Quarter night lanterns

visits.text: Head to Wuzhishan, the "Five Finger Mountain," sacred mountain of the Li, an aboriginal people present here for over 3,000 years. You'll cross China's last primary tropical forest, in a national reserve opened in 1985. The main peak reaches 1,867 m. Marked hiking trail, rare wildlife (Hainan gibbon, among the world's most endangered primates, fewer than 40 surviving individuals).

Shuiman village, nestled in the valley at 600 m altitude, is a Li cultural center. You'll see traditional "upturned boat-shaped" houses (chuanxing wu), thatched roofs. A few rare Li grandmothers still wear ancestral facial tattoos - a practice banned since 1949, dying out with them. The last tattooed generation is now 80-95 years old.

Fifteen kilometers away, Maona village offers a dive into Li daily life: brocade weaving, drying medicinal herbs, raising black-footed pigs. Li brocade (Li jin) has 3,000 years of history. Inscribed in 2009 on the UNESCO List of Intangible Cultural Heritage in Need of Urgent Safeguarding (not the Representative List), it has since paraded at Paris Fashion Week in 2024 and 2025 thanks to Dalishen patterns and Gangong bird motifs.

Rise early for the Yahu terraced rice fields, in Maoyang township, considered Hainan's most beautiful. They climb the mountain slopes, water mirrors in spring, golden waves in autumn. More modest than Yuanyang's in Yunnan, but much more accessible and less crowded. Morning light is finest.

activities.text: Li brocade weaving lesson with a local artisan in Maona or Shuiman. You learn the basics - foot loom, shuttle, cotton threads dyed with bark and roots. Li women traditionally weave sitting on the ground. Allow 2-3 hours to make a small sample (bracelet or bookmark) that you'll take home. Fair rates, around 200-300 yuan per session.

Li meal in a Shuiman family: ant chicken (the "ant chicken" is a local breed), pandanus leaf rice, black Shanlan rice wine (sacred wine served in bamboo), wild forest vegetables. Taste the "jianlan jiu," sweet cardamom wine. A cuisine very different from mainland China.

days_plan.itin_title: Tropical Hainan: 10 Days Around China's Southern Island

visits.text: Flight from Beijing to Xi'an (about 2 hours). The Terracotta Army, discovered on March 29, 1974, by farmers digging a well, lies 19 miles east of Xi'an. Commissioned by Emperor Qin Shi Huang—who unified China in 221 BC and launched construction of the Great Wall—this funerary army was meant to protect him in the afterlife. Pit 1, the largest (755 feet × 203 feet), contains over 6,000 warriors and horses arranged in a rectangular battle formation across 11 corridors. Each statue, standing 5.9 to 6.6 feet tall, has a unique face: expressions, hairstyles, armor, and ranks differ from soldier to soldier. The statues were fired at 1,650°F in kilns, then painted with mineral pigments to distinguish units. In total, excavations have uncovered about 8,000 warriors, 130 chariots, 520 horses, and over 10,000 bronze weapons. The site has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1987.

Pit 2, to the northeast, contains about 1,000 warriors with greater variety: crossbowmen, infantry, cavalry, and war chariots in a combined unit. Display cases let you observe details up close—seven variants of Qin armor have been identified. Pit 3, the smallest (5,380 square feet), U-shaped, holds 68 high-ranking officers and a ceremonial chariot drawn by four horses: this is the underground army's headquarters. The emperor's tomb itself, covered by a 377-foot-high mound, has never been opened—archaeologists fear the artifacts would degrade upon contact with air, as happened with the first warriors' paint when they were unearthed.

The Xi'an City Wall, built between 1374 and 1378 under the first Ming emperor Zhu Yuanzhang, forms a rectangle with an 8.5-mile perimeter around the old town. First constructed of rammed earth mixed with lime and glutinous rice juice, then covered with bricks in 1568, it rises 39 feet high, 49 to 59 feet wide at the base, and 39 to 46 feet wide at the top. A moat 66 feet wide and 33 feet deep encircles the wall. Four monumental gates pierce the ramparts at the cardinal points; 98 watchtowers, spaced 394 feet apart—a distance calculated based on half an arrow's range—line the route. Enter through the South Gate (Yongning), the most ornately decorated, whose main tower rises 105 feet.

The Muslim Quarter (Huimin Jie), at the foot of the Drum Tower, has been home to Xi'an's Hui community since the Tang dynasty (7th century), when Xi'an—then called Chang'an—was the starting point of the Silk Road. The alleys overflow with stalls selling hand-pulled noodles (biang biang mian), grilled lamb skewers, and flatbreads (roujiamo). The Great Mosque, founded in 742 during the Tang dynasty and rebuilt in the 14th century, blends traditional Chinese architecture with Islamic elements across a 129,000-square-foot garden.

The Giant Wild Goose Pagoda (Dayan Ta), built in 652 during the Tang dynasty to house Buddhist scriptures brought from India by the monk Xuanzang after 17 years of travel. The current pagoda, renovated under Empress Wu Zetian, has 7 stories and stands 210 feet tall. It's part of an ensemble inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2014 as part of the Silk Road corridor sites.

activities.text: Wander the alleys and sample local specialties
Sunset over the old town from the city walls
Visit a cave restaurant and learn to cook spicy local dishes
Watch the fountain show at the pagoda in the evening
High-speed train Xi'an → Beijing
Free stroll through downtown shopping streets

Explore Tang Paradise and Furong Lake, a vast theme park southeast of the Giant Wild Goose Pagoda. This recreated Tang dynasty garden offers lakeside walks and nightly water shows celebrating China's golden age.
Explore Xi'an's Muslim Quarter and its traditional markets.
Walk through the historic center and visit the Bell Tower and Drum Tower.

Bike the city walls: rentals are available at each gate. Allow 1.5 to 2 hours to complete the 8.5-mile circuit, with contrasting views of the old town inside and modern towers outside. The walkway, 39 to 46 feet wide, is perfectly flat.
Tasting in the Muslim Quarter: biang biang noodles hand-pulled before your eyes, cumin lamb skewers, pao mo lamb soup (bread crumbled into broth), sesame cakes, and fresh-pressed pomegranate juice.

days_plan.itin_title: Classic China: Beijing, Xi'an & Shanghai
Classic China: Beijing, Xi'an & Shanghai
The Silk Road Overland: Xi'an to Istanbul via Uzbekistan & Turkmenistan
China's Silk Road: Xi'an to Kashgar via the Hexi Corridor
Spiritual Henan & Shaanxi – Tracing the Cradle of Chinese Civilization
Spiritual Henan & Shaanxi – Tracing the Cradle of Chinese Civilization

visits.text: Labrang Monastery, established in 1709, ranks among the six great monasteries of the Gelugpa school (Yellow Hats) of Tibetan Buddhism and stands as the largest outside the Tibet Autonomous Region. Around 1,600 monks study here across six colleges covering philosophy, medicine, astronomy, and tantra. The Grand Prayer Hall, supported by 140 massive pillars, accommodates up to 3,000 monks for collective chanting. At its early 20th-century peak, Labrang housed nearly 4,000 monks.

The Labrang kora—the circumambulation circuit—stretches 3.5 km around the monastery, lined with over 1,700 hexagonal painted prayer wheels. This is the world's longest prayer wheel corridor. Tibetan pilgrims walk it clockwise from dawn, spinning each wheel as they pass. A complete circuit takes about an hour.

The golden Gongtang Stupa, within the monastery complex, houses relics of a revered lama. Its gilded roof gleams in the sunlight above the monastery's whitewashed roofs. From the hill behind, the panorama embraces the entire monastery, the Daxia River, and the surrounding grasslands.

Sangke Prairie, 14 km from Xiahe, unfolds at the foot of rolling hills at 3,000 meters elevation. Tibetan nomadic families graze yaks and sheep here. In summer, wildflowers paint the steppe in color and yurt camps welcome overnight visitors.

Ganjia Prairie, quieter than Sangke, preserves the ruins of the octagonal Bajiao fortress, a rammed-earth citadel visible from the road. Nomads here live in black yak-hair tents, raising horses and sheep as their ancestors did.

activities.text: Hike up the hill south of the monastery for a sweeping view over Labrang, the golden rooftops, and the Daxia River valley. The trail starts behind the white stupa to the left of the complex and takes roughly 45 minutes uphill.

If your visit coincides with a monastic debate day at Labrang (typically afternoons), monks square off in the courtyard, clapping their hands to punctuate philosophical arguments—a striking auditory and visual spectacle.
Sample yak yogurt at the shops along Xiahe's main street. The local yogurt, thick as custard and slightly tangy, is served in ceramic bowls with sugar or honey.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou
Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou

visits.text: Gulangyu Island, 600 meters from Xiamen's shore, has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2017. The 2 km² island was an international concession from 1903 to 1943, explaining its 1,000 buildings mixing colonial, Art Deco, and traditional Chinese styles. Cars are prohibited. The island once counted up to 500 pianos in the early 20th century, earning it the nickname "Music Island."

Nanputuo Temple, at the foot of Mount Wulao, is a Buddhist monastery founded during the Tang dynasty and rebuilt under the Qing. Dedicated to Guanyin, it serves an active monastic community and houses a Buddhist school. The four main halls climb up the hillside.
Sunlight Rock (Riguang Yan) is Gulangyu's highest point at 93 meters. At the summit, a 360° panorama embraces the island, the Taiwan Strait, and Fujian's mountains.

Shuzhuang Garden, created in 1913 by an exiled Taiwanese scholar, integrates the sea into its landscape composition—coastal rocks are part of the garden. The Piano Museum, within the grounds, displays over 100 antique pianos from Europe and America, from the 17th to the 20th century.

Zhongshan Road, Xiamen's main shopping artery, stretches 1.2 km under 1920s-1930s colonial-style arcades (qilou). These covered galleries, typical of southern Fujian and influenced by Southeast Asian architecture, blend European and Chinese elements across three to five stories.

activities.text: Ferry crossing to Gulangyu from Dongdu terminal (20 minutes). Free stroll through pedestrian alleys, between colonial facades covered with bougainvillea and local pastry shops.
Seafood tasting at Xiamen harbor market. Fujian is renowned for oyster omelets (haolicuijian), clam vermicelli, and crab spring rolls. The market bustles in late afternoon.

days_plan.itin_title: Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian
Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian

visits.text: You fall for Xiangcheng the moment you arrive. At 2,900m, in a valley of apple and peach orchards, Tibetan dwellings are entirely white, flat-roofed, narrow at the base and wide at the top—a silhouette unique in China. Facades are whitewashed fresh every year. Drive slowly through Qingde and Wari hamlets, step out of the car, walk.

Climb to Sangpiling (Sampeling) Monastery, planted on a hill overlooking the valley. You enter a sanctuary founded in the 17th century, Gelugpa school, 600 monks. Legend says there are 99 hills around, and the hundredth is symbolic. The main assembly hall is covered in frescoes of the Buddha's life. Donations welcome, photos forbidden inside.

Stroll through the town of Xiangcheng, alongside the Shouqu River. You'll find a morning market for vegetables and yak meat, a small Hui mosque (surprising at this altitude), a people's hall with a Tibetan facade. No tourists, Tibetan families in fur hats who greet you. One hour is enough.

activities.text: Taste Xiangcheng apples. The valley offers famous orchards—small, very sweet red apples, harvested September-October. In season, farmers sell them roadside for 5 yuan per kilo. Peaches arrive in July, apricots in June. Munch while walking through the orchards; ask owners' permission first.

Check into a Tibetan family guesthouse. Several white houses in town rent rooms with meals (200-250 yuan per night). Dinner is taken around the central hearth: barley soup, dried yak meat, cheese. The family stays to chat—little English, but lots of smiles and laughter.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Western Sichuan - Kham, Aba, and Forgotten Valleys in 17 days

visits.text: Head to Xiangshawan, the "Resonant Sand Bay," at the eastern edge of the Kubuqi Desert. Kubuqi is China's 7th largest desert, covering 18,600 square kilometers. Its name means "bowstring" in Mongolian—the Yellow River draws the bow, the sand ribbon forms the string. You arrive at the reception station; a 1-km cable car drops you into the heart of the dunes.

The crescent dunes reach 90 meters tall, with a 45-degree slope. It's the "singing sand" phenomenon that gave the place its name: in dry weather, slide down from a dune crest and you'll hear a deep rumble, compared variously to a jet engine or a bronze bell. The sound can reach 105 decibels and last over a minute.

The Lotus Hotel, designed as a giant lotus flower resting on the dunes, is an architectural feat: no conventional foundation, but steel panels that float the 30,000-square-meter building on sand. Awarded the Iconic and A'Design Silver prizes, listed by CNN among China's most beautiful hotels. Even if you don't stay overnight, see the illuminated lobby at dusk.

activities.text: Sandboarding descent from the summit of a 100-meter dune. Thrills guaranteed—the sand is fine as powdered sugar. Several packages in the park; choose the morning session (9-11 AM), before the sand burns bare feet. Allow 45 minutes to climb, 30 seconds to descend.

Camel caravan through the dunes to reach Yuesha Island or Xiansha Island. The ride lasts 30-45 minutes, the camel's gait slow and rocking, viewing the desert from above. The camel drivers, Mongolian, speak little but smile often. For thrill-seekers, there are also buggies, archery, and a zipline over the dunes.

In the evening, bonfire party with traditional Mongolian dances, an Ordos wedding reenactment, and fire spinning. Desert nights are cool even in summer—bring a fleece. If the sky is clear, climb a dune at 10 PM to watch the Milky Way; Kubuqi is far enough from urban light.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner Mongolia: Steppes, Dunes, and Forgotten Empires

visits.text: Xiaoqi is the most beautiful village in the county according to local consensus, divided into Upper and Lower Xiaoqi. Look for wood carvings in the ancestral halls, mixing pine flowers, qilin and scenes from Han mythology. Morning light falls perfectly on the central square. Allow 1h30, early departure mandatory to beat the groups.

Huangling is only accessible by cable car: village suspended on a mountainside, famous for its *shaiqiu* (晒秋, autumn drying) in October-November - rooftops covered with round baskets filled with red peppers, yellow squash, corn. The most viral image of Wuyuan. Off-season, architecture alone justifies the ascent. Ticket 110 yuan cable car included.

activities.text: If you're traveling in April, replace Huangling with Jiangling: terraced rapeseed fields cascade in golden waves, the springtime photo of Wuyuan. The choice between the two depends strictly on season, see the When to Go to China blog post. Outside April and October-November, Xiaoqi alone suffices, save the half-day to rest your legs before Jingdezhen.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi

visits.text: You land in Xiapu, in northeastern Fujian, a pilgrimage site for photographers worldwide for two decades. The mudflats—40 km² of tidal flats—are sculpted by the tides into geometric patterns of bamboo poles for seaweed farming, fishing nets, oyster beds. High-speed train from Fuzhou in 1 hour. The town itself has nothing spectacular, but its surroundings are striking.

Rise before dawn and head to Beiqi Village, 10 km east—the sunrise spot. At low tide, the wet sand takes on a tiger-skin texture, and hundreds of bamboo poles for seaweed farming create a sublime graphic pattern at daybreak. Farmers lay out nets around 6 AM, creating the human silhouettes you see everywhere on postcards.

Head next to Huazhu, 34 km east, for a cliff-top panorama over the Fuyao Archipelago. Red and blue floats mark the oyster beds; grey bamboo lines signal seaweed. At sunrise in clear weather, sky and sea merge into a single tableau. Platform set up for photographers.

Shift scenery dramatically at Yangjiaxi, 35 km northwest: a forest of thousand-year-old banyan trees and red maples tangled above a torrent. One of China's five best sites for autumn foliage. In spring, light mist and first green leaves; in November, the maples redden. Take a bamboo raft descent (50 yuan).

Don't miss the Shajiang S-Bend, Xiapu's most photographed graphic pattern. Bamboo poles for seaweed farming form a perfect S-curve in a sheltered bay, and fishing boats snake between them during harvest. For the photo, you climb onto the roof of an unfinished building turned photo base (20 yuan entry). Spectacle at sunrise and sunset.

Board a boat to Dong'an Floating Village. Hundreds of wooden cabins perched on floating platforms crowd the bay—the largest concentration of fish farms in Xiapu. They raise abalone, grouper, oysters. Guided tour 100 yuan for 1 hour, with tasting at the farmers' homes.

activities.text: For sunset, head to Xiaohao Village, 20 km southeast. The beach is mainly sandy, traced by small streams that draw golden arabesques in backlight. Fishermen work late here and create magnificent silhouettes. It's one of the most versatile spots: each season produces different light.

Hire a local photographer-driver (around 600 yuan per day for a car, negotiable) who knows the tides and farmers' rotations. Many speak basic English. Local agencies also organize group outings. Without this, you'll lose a lot of time searching for spots.

For food lovers, Xiapu is known for its abalone and dried krill. Head to Sansha Fish Market, 30 km northeast—one of the liveliest on the coast. At 5 AM, boats return and unload live crabs, mandarin fish, clams, lobsters. You buy and the adjacent restaurant cooks it for 30-50 yuan.

Rise early for Mount Mantou (the 'Steamed Bun Hill'), 25 km from town. The site offers a plunging view of hundreds of bamboo poles for farming that light up at sunrise. Farmers work below, their silhouettes standing out against the golden sand. Ink-painting atmosphere. Allow 1 hour to shoot before the light hardens.

days_plan.itin_title: Coastal Fujian - Beyond the Tulou, 11 Days on the Maritime Silk Road
Coastal Fujian - Beyond the Tulou, 11 Days on the Maritime Silk Road

visits.text: Xidi (西递), founded during the Song Dynasty and developed by the Hu family from the 14th to 18th centuries, is Anhui's best-preserved Hui village. The Qingyi Memorial Arch (1578), carved on three levels, celebrates a Hu official's success in the imperial examinations. Behind it, 124 Ming-Qing residences organize along lanes paved with blue flagstones. UNESCO-listed in 2000.

Xidi is quieter and less crowded than Hongcun - tour groups favor the Moon Pond. Get lost in the side lanes behind the merchant houses: ochre rammed-earth walls pierced with geometric windows, dead-ends closed by raw wood doors. The atmosphere of a village still alive.

activities.text: Look for a Chinese ink or calligraphy workshop in Xidi's lanes. Huizhou ink has been produced in the region for 2,000 years and remains the reference for traditional calligraphers nationwide.

days_plan.itin_title: Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

visits.text: Shiqiao Miao Village, in Danzhai County, is the last place in China where paper is made by hand using Tang dynasty (7th century) techniques. More than 60 families still practice this millennial craft: local tree bark soaked, boiled in lime, beaten into pulp, then spread on a bamboo screen. The resulting paper can be preserved for 1,500 years—the National Library and National Museum of China use it to restore ancient books and paintings. The process, inscribed on the national intangible heritage list in 2006, involves more than ten steps.

Chuandong Cave, adjacent to Shiqiao village, houses an underground paper mill fed by a spring whose slightly alkaline water is considered ideal for papermaking. Stalactites grow at an unusual rate here, giving the walls a soft texture to the touch.

Xijiang, the "Thousand Household Village," is the world's largest Miao village: 1,432 families, over 6,000 inhabitants of whom 99.5% are Miao. Wooden fir stilted houses climb in tiers on both sides of the Baishui River, linked by Wind and Rain Bridges. The village is divided into ten natural hamlets at the foot of Mount Leigong.

The Miao Nationality Museum, in the village, traces the history of this people through costumes, orally transmitted "old songs," and agricultural tools. Special emphasis is placed on silver jewelry making—hammering, filigree, granulation—practiced by village silversmiths for generations.

activities.text: Flower paper (huacao) workshop in Shiqiao: visitors dip a screen into bark pulp, place dried flowers and leaves on the wet layer, then add a second layer of pulp. Drying takes a week—the paper is then shipped.

Kaili Sunday Market gathers Miao villagers from the surrounding area every Sunday. Embroidered textiles, hammered silver jewelry, medicinal herbs, and sour fish soup (suantangyu) mingle in a rural fair atmosphere.
Climb to Xijiang viewpoint in late afternoon: at dusk, thousands of lights illuminate the valley, transforming the stilted houses into a luminous amphitheater.

Miao song and dance performance on the central square (around 11:30 am and 5:00 pm). The "Miao old songs" are polyphonic vocals without choir leader or instrumental accompaniment, transmitted by word of mouth for centuries.

Long table banquet (changzhuo yan): guests sit around a table several dozen meters long. Pickled sour fish, salted vegetables, and chili-boiled chicken, accompanied by rice wine served in buffalo horns.

days_plan.itin_title: Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian
Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian

visits.text: You leave Hohhot for the Xilamuren Grassland, about 90 km north. The road climbs gently onto the Mongolian plateau, and the landscape shifts abruptly: trees vanish, replaced by immense rolling grasses rippling in the wind. Stop at Xilamuren Temple (Puhui Si), founded in 1769 under Emperor Qianlong. A small monastery still active—the monks' chants carry far across the empty steppe.

Check into a traditional yurt (ger) camp. The yurt is more than accommodation—it's the quintessential nomadic invention, assembled in two hours, transportable by camel. Ask your host to explain the interior arrangement: altar to the west, hearth at center, always facing south. Comfort is spartan, but the experience counts.

Visit an ovoo, these stone cairns topped with blue prayer flags. They're the shamanic altars of the steppe, dedicated to mountain and sky spirits. Circle three times clockwise, add a stone—it's the custom. Many herders still stop here before long journeys.

Second day on the steppe: wake at 5 AM for sunrise over the grassy hills. Raking light transforms the grass to pale gold, sheep flocks emerge to graze. Real herders (not camp staff) start their day at this hour; ask your host to take you to a neighboring family encampment.

Meet a Mongolian herding family in their permanent yurt. They'll serve you salty milk tea, dried cheese (aaruul, hard as stone), and fresh butter. Conversation flows through your guide-translator. Ask about transhumance, winter life, children who've gone to school in the city. This is the most precious part of this stop.

If your visit coincides with late July, the Inner Mongolia Naadam Festival is held here (dates vary, unlike the fixed calendar of independent Mongolia). Otherwise, amateur competitions are organized all summer at the big camps. Gripping spectacle: hundreds of riders, sumo-style wrestlers in leather costumes, mounted archers.

activities.text: An afternoon devoted to the three manly sports of the Mongols (eriin gurvan naadam): archery, horse racing, and wrestling (bökh). Demonstrations at tourist camps are choreographed but authentic. If you ride, ask for a Mongolian pony—small, stocky, tireless, the same breed Genghis Khan rode 800 years ago.

In the evening, a whole roasted lamb feast (khorkhog or Mongolian barbecue) around the fire, accompanied by Mongolian vodka and airag (fermented mare's milk, tangy and fizzy). Then stargazing: Xilamuren has one of the purest skies in eastern China, with no light pollution for 100 km in any direction. The Milky Way is visible to the naked eye.

A 2-3 hour horseback ride across the steppe with a local guide. Ask for a gallop on the real grasslands (the turf is soft, the horses are trained). It's physical—grip with your knees, not the reins; Mongolian horses don't steer like ours.

Quick lesson in traditional Mongolian archery with a composite recurve bow and reed arrows. Genghis Khan's ancient warriors shot 200 meters at full gallop. You'll aim at a fixed target at 30 meters, which is already respectable. A local instructor corrects finger and shoulder position.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner Mongolia: Steppes, Dunes, and Forgotten Empires
Inner Mongolia: Steppes, Dunes, and Forgotten Empires

visits.text: Welcome to Xinduqiao, nicknamed "photographers' paradise." At 3,300m, you cross a wide basin where Tibetan hamlets of wood and adobe houses dot meadows cut by streams. Poplars turn gold in October, yaks graze, slanting light transforms everything into a painting. Drive 10 km in any direction and you'll find your shot.

Make a 25 km detour south to the hamlet of Yulongxi. You'll find a handful of wooden flat-roof houses with carved balconies, a white-pebbled river, a covered bridge. No ticket, no shops—Tibetan farming in the raw. Buy dried yak cheese from a grandmother and carry on.

activities.text: Wake at 6am for the morning photo session. Mist rises from the streams, first rays strike the snow-capped Yala peaks. Your local guides will take you to viewpoints known only to photographers—the hill behind town, the river bend, a herd of yaks facing the sun. Budget 2-3 hours and all your battery stock.

Share butter tea in a Tibetan home. Several families open their main hearth room: central stove, colorful carpets, family altar. For 50 yuan, you're served butter tea, tsampa, and dried cheese. Light enters through the roof—it's almost a prayer. Your driver knows where to go.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Western Sichuan - Kham, Aba, and Forgotten Valleys in 17 days

visits.text: Welcome to Boao, 60 km southeast of Wenchang, transformed in two decades from a small fishing village to headquarters of the Boao Forum for Asia - the "Asian Davos" founded in 2001. You'll discover the Yongchang Pavilion conference center, designed by architect Zhang Yonghe, which hosts regional heads of state every spring. The peninsula where three rivers meet (Wanquan, Jiuqu, Longgun) before flowing into the sea offers a landscape unique in China.

Walk along Yudai Beach ("jade belt"), 5 km from Boao center, which separates the river estuary from the open sea. It's one of the world's longest narrow sand strips - 8 km long and just a few meters wide. Clear water, palm trees, few people on weekdays. The sunset walk between river and sea is stunning.

Head to Xinglong, 50 km south, a former village created in the 1950s-70s to welcome overseas Chinese repatriated from Indonesia, Vietnam, Malaysia, and Burma. Today, you'll still hear Javanese, Vietnamese, and Malay spoken in some families. The Tropical Botanical Garden, founded in 1957, houses over 1,200 plant species of Southeast Asian origin.

Head to Xinglong coffee village, planted in the 1950s by Indonesian repatriates, where Hainanese arabica robusta is cultivated. You'll see old artisans still roasting over wood fires in earthen ovens. Coffee is served very strong, sweet, sometimes with butter - a local tradition derived from Javanese cafés. Small tasting and beans to take home.

activities.text: Coffee roasting workshop in Xinglong, at a family farm. You'll see the complete process: sorting green beans, roasting over open fire, hand grinding, Singapore-style tasting (arm extended, sock filter). Allow about 90 minutes. Leave with a bag of your own roast.

Taste the Malay-Chinese meal in Xinglong, a dish unique in mainland China. On the menu: Hainanese nasi lemak, satay, sambal, coconut curry rice. This Sino-Malay Creole cuisine exists only on this narrow strip of land. A legacy of the 1960s repatriates. Ice-cold Tsingtao beer to accompany.

days_plan.itin_title: Tropical Hainan: 10 Days Around China's Southern Island

visits.text: Xingping Fisherman Cliff overlooks the exact landscape of the 20-yuan bill, 15 min walk from village center. The currency photo is taken from this precise viewpoint. Much less touristy than Yangshuo, old houses on Li bank, paved alleys. Allow 30 min on cliff, plus 1h walk in old Xingping. Entry 20 yuan.

A few elders still work with cormorants in Xingping, at dawn, on the river. The trade is disappearing, transmitted less and less, but those who still practice accept spectators (early morning, around 6 a.m.). Photos possible for tip. Don't pay more than 20-30 yuan, local rate. Magnificent in reality, outside tourist décor.

activities.text: Book the return driver: local buses Xingping-Guilin become scarce after 7 p.m., and you'll want to stay at Fisherman Cliff until sunset. 250-300 yuan for driver waiting plus return. Arrival Guilin around 9 p.m., late dinner at Banyan Lake. Confirmation by SMS the day before via hotel, payment in cash or WeChat Pay.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi

visits.text: Kumbum Monastery (Ta'er Si), 25 km from Xining, was founded in 1583 on the birthplace of Tsongkhapa, reformer of Tibetan Buddhism and founder of the Gelugpa school. Around 600 monks reside here. The site is famous for its yak butter sculptures (created in winter when hands don't melt the butter) and its eight white stupas aligned before the main entrance.

Kumbum's main hall blends Tibetan and Chinese architecture—golden roofs, white walls, red pillars. The Golden Tree Sanctuary houses a sandalwood tree that legend says grew where Tsongkhapa was conceived, bearing an image of Buddha on each of its 100,000 leaves (hence "Kumbum"—"hundred thousand images").

activities.text: Stroll through Xining's Tibetan market: turquoise and coral jewelry, Tibetan knives, incense, tsampa, and brick-compressed tea. Xining, at 2,275 meters, serves as a supply and acclimatization city before the higher-altitude stages.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou

visits.text: Express train Harbin-Yabuli (1.5 hours, about 100 yuan). You arrive at the birthplace of Chinese skiing: the first resort was opened in 1974 by the provincial sports committee to train athletes. Yabuli hosted the 1996 Asian Winter Games, the 2009 Winter Universiade, and again the Harbin 2025 Asian Winter Games. The complex covers 22.5 km² at the foot of the Zhangguangcai Mountains.

Mao'er Shan, the summit at 1,374.8 m, offers China's longest run: 5 km of continuous descent. Even without skiing, take the gondola up for the panorama: boreal forest as far as the eye can see, and on clear days, the snowy peaks of the northeast. Allow 150 yuan round trip.

The National Training Center, legacy of the 2025 Games, is partially open to the public. You see FIS-homologated slopes (812 m drop), the K90 ski jump and biathlon facilities with their electronic targets. It's the technical matrix of Chinese winter sports.

activities.text: Ski. Yabuli has 46 runs (including 15 blacks) and remains, despite competition from Wanda Changbaishan, the reference resort. Season from mid-November to mid-March, abundant natural snow. Day pass 380 yuan weekdays, 680 weekends. Complete ski-boot-pole rental 150-300 yuan. Intermediate and advanced well served; beginners have a vast magic carpet area at the base.

At day's end, head to Yabuli's forest hot spring, rich in selenium, in a wooded 1,000 m² enclosure. The facility combines outdoor and indoor pools. Expect 118 yuan admission. Perfect relaxation after a day of skiing.

days_plan.itin_title: Winter Manchuria - snow, sacred volcano and North Korean border

visits.text: Li River cruise from Guilin to Yangshuo—83 km in 4 to 5 hours of navigation through hundreds of karst pinnacles sculpted by millions of years of erosion. Departure is from Zhujiang pier, 30 minutes from downtown Guilin. The most spectacular section stretches between Yangdi and Xingping, where peaks close in on both sides of the waterway. Nine Horses Fresco Hill (Jiǔmǎ Huàshān) unfolds a cliff over 400 meters high and 200 meters wide, whose natural reliefs trace nine horses in different poses. Shortly after, the Yellow Cloth Shoal (Huángbù Tān) offers the panorama printed on the back of the 20-yuan note: seven peaks reflected in the calm water like a mirror. Arrival in Yangshuo by early afternoon.

Gongnong Bridge, at the confluence of the Yulong and Jinbao rivers, offers one of the region's finest views: bamboo rafts gliding between karst peaks whose reflections ripple on the water. It's the ideal starting point for observing the countryside at dawn or sunset.

The Big Banyan Tree (Dà Róngshù), a few kilometers south of Yangshuo on the Ten-Mile Gallery, is a thousand-year-old tree standing 17 meters tall with a trunk 7 meters around. Its age is estimated at over 1,400 years, making it contemporary with the founding of Yangshuo county. Its cascading branches cover approximately 1,300 m². Scenes from the film Liu Sanjie (1961), a Chinese cinema classic, were shot nearby.

Moon Hill (Yuèliàng Shān) is a 380-meter limestone peak pierced by a natural arch roughly 50 meters high and equally wide—the remnant of an ancient cave carved by karst erosion. From the road, the opening shifts shape with your viewing angle: crescent, half-moon, full moon. A trail of 800 marble steps leads to the arch in 20 to 30 minutes. At the summit, the panoramic view embraces all of Yangshuo county and the Yulong River. Moon Hill is also a climbing mecca: American climber Todd Skinner equipped the first routes here in the early 1990s, launching sport climbing in the region.

Fuli, a thousand-year-old town 8 km east of Yangshuo on the Li River's banks, is nicknamed "the town of painted fans." A large portion of its inhabitants practice the craft of folding fans, made from local mao bamboo and rice paper, decorated with Guangxi landscapes. The main street is lined with workshops where you can paint your own fan. The town also preserves Nuo culture—ritual music and dances inherited from primitive tribes—and a temple dedicated to the goddess Mazu, brought by immigrants from Fujian and Guangdong who make up much of the population.

Xingping, a town founded in 265 CE, is one of the oldest settlements along the Li River, 25 km northeast of Yangshuo. Its lanes paved with blue-gray slabs are lined with white-walled houses. Precisely here, at the bend in the Li River, you'll find the panorama printed on the 20-yuan note. To reach the exact viewpoint from the bill, walk or cycle about 2 km to Yuanbao Hill.

Climb Laozhai Mountain (老寨山) from Xingping: a steep trail of about 30 minutes leads to the summit where you overlook the Li River's meander at 180°, with karst peaks aligned as backdrop. This is the region's most celebrated photographic viewpoint—the same panorama that generations of traditional Chinese painters tried to capture in ink.

activities.text: Wandering through West Street (Xī Jiē) in Yangshuo. This 517-meter pedestrian street, paved with marble slabs, is lined with Ming and Qing architecture converted into shops, cafés, and workshops. After dark, the atmosphere shifts with red lanterns and little street restaurants where you can taste beer fish (píjiǔ yú), Yangshuo's signature dish: Li River fish cooked in local beer sauce.

Bamboo raft drift down the Yulong River, a tributary of the Li. The Jima–Gongnong Bridge section (about 6 km, 90 minutes) traverses the heart of karst scenery in silence: rafts are poled by the boatman with no motor. You pass under centuries-old stone bridges, between rice paddies and water buffalo, with peaks as backdrop.

Cycling the Ten-Mile Gallery (Shílǐ Huàláng), a 10 km scenic corridor linking Yangshuo to Moon Hill. The road, closed to cars during the day, winds between rice paddies, villages, and karst peaks. You pass Butterfly Spring park, the Totem Path, and several stone bridges. Bike or e-scooter rentals are easy to arrange from Yangshuo.

Countryside hike between Fuli and Liugong village along dirt paths following the Li River. The trail crosses hamlets where traditional houses nestle beside rice paddies, pomelo orchards, and resting water buffalo. Allow about 2 hours for a relaxed walk.

Cycling or walking in the countryside around Xingping. A dirt path follows the Li River's bank south to Yucun village (about 2 km), where you observe the 20-yuan panorama from a different angle, from the riverbank. Continuing inland, trails cross orange groves and rice paddies framed by peaks, far from tourist circuits. Allow half a day to explore at your own pace.

days_plan.itin_title: Karst Heartlands: Guilin, Yangshuo & Longji Rice Terraces
Karst Heartlands: Guilin, Yangshuo & Longji Rice Terraces
Karst Heartlands: Guilin, Yangshuo & Longji Rice Terraces
Karst Heartlands: Guilin, Yangshuo & Longji Rice Terraces
Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie
Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie
Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi
Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi

visits.text: The "Ghost City" of Fengdu (Fēngdū Guǐchéng), on Mount Ming along the Yangtze, is a vast complex of temples, shrines, and monasteries dedicated to the afterlife in Chinese beliefs. Blending Confucianism, Taoism, and Buddhism, the site features sculptures and dioramas depicting the judgment of souls, the tortures of hell, and the path to reincarnation. You cross the "Nothing-to-Be-Done Bridge" (Nàihé Qiáo), the "Ghost Torture Passage," and the "River of Blood." Despite these names, the complex is colorful and lively—Chinese sometimes make pilgrimages here to pray for their ancestors.

Shibaozhai Pagoda (Shíbǎozhài), an alternative stop to Fengdu depending on cruise companies, is a 400-year-old temple built against a 200-meter vertical cliff on the Yangtze's edge. Its 12-story wooden tower, painted red, leans against the rock wall without touching it. After the Three Gorges Dam flooded the area, the promontory became an island connected to the bank by a causeway—the temple was barely saved from rising waters.

Qutang Gorge (Qútáng Xiá), the shortest of the Three Gorges (8 km), is also the most spectacular. Cliffs rise over 1,500 meters on each side, narrowing the river to just a few dozen meters at its tightest point. The entrance, framed by the Kuimen Gates, is the most famous image of the Three Gorges—it appears on the 10-yuan banknote. Poet Li Bai set his celebrated verse here: "In the morning I left Baidi amid colorful clouds; a thousand li to Jiangling, covered in one day."

Wu Gorge (Wū Xiá), 46 km long, is known for its twelve peaks whose summits vanish into clouds. The forest-covered cliffs are so vertical that sunlight barely penetrates. Goddess Peak (Shénnǚ Fēng), a rock formation shaped like a female silhouette atop a ridge, is associated with the legend of Yao Ji, daughter of the Queen Mother of the West, who supposedly helped the mythical Emperor Yu tame the Yangtze's waters.

activities.text: Navigation on the Yangtze between Chongqing and Fengjie. The ship traverses wide sections of the river bordered by karst cliffs and rural villages. Onboard: lectures on Yangtze history, calligraphy demonstrations, and morning tai chi classes on the upper deck.

Excursion by sampan on Shennong Stream (Shénnóng Xī) or the Lesser Three Gorges (Xiǎo Sānxiá), depending on cruise company. You leave the main vessel for a smaller boat that ascends a narrow tributary between rock walls. On Shennong Stream, teams of boatmen pole the sampan forward through gorges so tight the cliffs seem to close overhead. You sometimes spot hanging coffins clinging to the walls—a burial practice of the Ba people over 2,000 years old.

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest China: Sichuan, Chongqing & the Yangtze Gorges
Southwest China: Sichuan, Chongqing & the Yangtze Gorges

visits.text: Slender West Lake (瘦西湖) extends over 4.3 km of lakes and canals linked in a chain, landscaped during the Qing Dynasty. Its name comes from an 18th-century poet who saw it as a graceful variant of Hangzhou's West Lake. The Fishing Platform (钓鱼台) is the most recognizable image: its two circular openings frame the Five-Pavilion Pagoda like a double lens.

He Garden (何园), built in 1883 during the Qing Dynasty, is the most refined of Yangzhou's private gardens. Its two-story covered gallery runs 1,500 meters and connects all pavilions while offering framed views at every angle - an invention unique in China.

Ge Garden (个园), founded in 1818, is famous for its rocks organized by four seasons - each section represents a season's landscape with different stones. The "summer mountain" in Taihu rock pierced with a hundred holes symbolizes water and coolness.

Dongguan Street (东关街) - 1.1 km of preserved Qing merchant houses, antique shops, and local specialties. Yangzhou was for centuries one of China's wealthiest cities thanks to the salt trade. The shaobing bakeries (sesame flatbreads) and gansi vendors (shredded tofu) are unmissable.

activities.text: Rise early for breakfast at one of Yangzhou's traditional teahouses (茶馆). The culture of elaborate breakfast has been an institution since the Tang era - the fabulously wealthy salt merchants of the 18th century spent fortunes on morning dim sum. Fu Chun Cha She is the city's oldest.
Walk along the banks of the Grand Canal (大运河) that crosses Yangzhou - UNESCO-listed in 2014, 1,794 km long. This is where the canal met the maritime route south during the Sui and Tang dynasties.

days_plan.itin_title: Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

visits.text: Yanjing ("salt wells," Tsakalo in Tibetan) is one of the most extraordinary sites in eastern Tibet, administratively within Markam County (TAR). More than 3,000 salt basins suspended on wooden scaffolds cascade down both banks of the Lancang Jiang (Mekong) at 2,300 m altitude. Ancestral salt production: at least 1,300 years documented according to records. You'll see Naxi women draw brine from natural wells (saline springs trapped in the plateau when the ocean uplifted) and carry it in baskets to the clay basins.

Rise early to see the women at work from 7 AM onward—by Naxi tradition, men sell, women produce. About 25 families remain active across the 2,775 registered basins. White salt (food-grade) and red salt (livestock and tea) come from the same well but are harvested in different seasons. Annual production: 1.5 million kg, generating roughly 1.3 million yuan in revenue.

Cross the bridge to Jiada, on the east bank—here's where you access the upper basins. The paths between basins are narrow and slippery; ask the Naxi women for permission before walking among their vats. Bring a few yuan to buy red salt in sachets (5-10 yuan)—it's the quintessential local souvenir.

Push open the door of Tibet's only Catholic church, founded in 1855 by two French missionaries: 80% of Upper Yanjing's population is Catholic Tibetan. You'll see residents alternating crucifixes and prayer flags, just like in Cizhong. Small Mass every Sunday at 9 AM.

activities.text: Sample Jiajia noodles, Yanjing's specialty. Small bowls of barley noodles, minced meat, local red salt—the goal being to down as many as possible. The count is kept with pebbles you add to your table with each bowl. The local record exceeds 100. For you, 7-8 will suffice amply.

Your Tibet permit will be checked for the first time here: the border checkpoint between TAR and Yunnan sits at Yanjing's southern entrance. Without a valid permit, you'll be turned back toward Cizhong and Yunnan. With one, you continue toward Markam tomorrow.

days_plan.itin_title: Three Parallel Rivers & Eastern Tibet - 14-Day Yunnan to Kham Extension

visits.text: High-speed train Qingdao-Yantai in 1.5 hours. Upon arrival, rush to Changyu Winery, the cradle of Chinese wine industry. Founded January 1, 1892, by Zhang Bishi, overseas Chinese diplomat and businessman, this is where the first bottle of Chinese wine was produced in 1899 and the first brandy in 1914. The initial investment—3 million taels of silver—equaled a military budget of the era. The museum occupies the original buildings.

The museum's highlight: the underground cellar, Asia's first industrial cellar, completed after 11 years of work (1894-1905). 1,976 m² dug below sea level, equipped with 430 oak barrels imported from Italy and Austria. Foreign engineers visiting at the time called it a "miracle in wine architecture history." The first cellar master was Baron Maximilian von Babo, vice-consul of the Austro-Hungarian Empire in Yantai. The included tasting presents three cuvées including the famous Cabernet Gernischt.

Afternoon, climb Yantai Hill (Yantai Hill Park). The remains of the 19th-20th century international consular quarter cluster here: former American, British, Danish, Japanese consulates, plus the English chapel. Yantai ("Smoke Terrace") became a treaty port in 1862 after the Opium Wars and hosted some twenty foreign consulates for a century. Clear views over the bay from the park summit.

If you still have time, descend to Golden Beach (Golden Beach), 7 km of fine sand that locals prefer to Qingdao's beaches. Far less crowded as few foreign tourists go to Yantai. Fine seaside promenade, with views of Zhifu and Yangma islands offshore.

activities.text: Yantai obtained in 1987 the status of "International City of Vine and Wine" from the International Organisation of Vine and Wine (OIV)—the only Asian city with this distinction. Over 130 wineries operate in the region. A half-day of wine tourism in Nava Valley, 30 minutes southeast, allows visiting 2 or 3 modern châteaux (Domaine de Long Dai created by Lafite Rothschild, Château Junding, Château Reifeng).

days_plan.itin_title: Shandong: 11 Days from Confucius to Mount Tai and the German Coast

visits.text: The Three Gorges Dam (Sānxiá Dàbà), completed in 2006, is the world's largest hydroelectric dam: 2,335 meters long and 185 meters high. Its power station produces about 100 billion kWh annually. The project, launched in 1994, required relocating over one million people and submerging entire cities beneath a 660-km reservoir. The five-stage ship lock allows vessels to traverse a 113-meter elevation change—the complete passage takes about 4 hours. The ship lift, the world's largest, hoists 3,000-ton vessels 113 meters in roughly 40 minutes.

Disembarkation at Maoping port in Xiling Gorge (Xīlíng Xiá), the longest of the Three Gorges at 120 km. Its waters were once the most dangerous on the Yangtze, scattered with rapids and reefs—the dam's flooding calmed this section. The gorge remains bordered by steep cliffs and successive narrow defiles (Dengying Gorge, Bingshu Baojian Gorge). The bus transfer to downtown Yichang takes about an hour.

Gezhouba Dam (Gězhōubà), 38 km downstream from the Three Gorges Dam, was the first major dam built on the main course of the Yangtze, completed in 1988. Spanning 2,606 meters, it served as a "dress rehearsal" before the Three Gorges project. Its lock still allows passage of large-tonnage vessels. From Yichang's developed embankments, you can observe the structure and the passage of boats.

activities.text: Free time in Yichang before departure (flight or train). Yichang, with a history spanning over 4,000 years, was a strategic point during the Three Kingdoms period—the famous Battle of Yiling (222) took place here. The Three Gorges Tourist Center offers a summary of the river's and region's history.

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest China: Sichuan, Chongqing & the Yangtze Gorges
Southwest China: Sichuan, Chongqing & the Yangtze Gorges
Deep Hubei & Hunan - 14 days through Wudang, Shennongjia and Miao valleys

visits.text: You cross into Ningxia and arrive in Yinchuan, former capital of the Tangut kingdom. Head first to the Western Xia Tombs, 30 km west at the foot of the Helan Mountains. Inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list in July 2025, they're nicknamed the "Eastern Pyramids": nine imperial tombs and 271 subsidiary graves cover 50 square kilometers. The Tangut kingdom (1038-1227) was razed by Genghis Khan in 1227, its writing forgotten for eight centuries.

Don't miss Tomb No. 3, attributed to founding emperor Li Yuanhao (1003-1048). The rammed-earth mound, 23 meters high, was originally a seven-story octagonal pagoda. Gobi winds stripped it bare over centuries, leaving only a brown earth cone. The modern museum at the entrance displays nearly 5,000 artifacts and attempts to reconstruct the Tangut language, of which 6,000 characters were created modeled on Chinese.

South of Yinchuan, 25 km out, Najiahu Mosque (in Yongning) is one of the oldest Ming-style mosques in China, reputedly founded in the 15th century. Sino-Islamic architecture: pavilions with curved roofs, green and white colors, no dome or minaret. Still a very active Hui community.

Stroll through Yinchuan's historic center: the Drum Tower and Bell Tower date from early Ming. The night market around them comes alive at 6 PM, with stalls of dried fruits, Yunnan mangoes, cumin lamb skewers. Yinchuan is also China's wine capital, the country's premier wine-growing region—try a local Cabernet.

Head north of Yinchuan into the Helan Mountains to see the Helankou petroglyphs. Over 6,000 rock carvings dating from 3,000 to 10,000 years ago, chiseled by successive nomadic peoples: stylized human faces, hunting scenes, animals. The most famous depict silhouettes of tigers and cervids. The canyon is dry, wild, the ground covered with black shards.

Fifty-six kilometers northwest of Yinchuan, Sand Lake (Sha Hu)—a surprising combination of a 22-square-kilometer freshwater lake at the foot of the Tengger Desert. Reed beds shelter over 60 species of migratory birds (swans, herons, pelicans). A boat ride, then climb the neighboring dunes. Rare contrast of water and sand.

The Ningxia Museum in Yinchuan complements the visit to the Xixia tombs. Halls dedicated to the Tanguts, Hui culture, calligraphy, and the region's prehistory. Well designed, free, air-conditioned—a welcome refuge in midsummer when it's 38°C outside.

activities.text: Wine tasting from the Helan Shan vineyards at one of the many châteaux dotting the foothills. The region enjoys a semi-arid climate, loess soils, and 3,000 hours of sunshine per year—ideal conditions for Cabernet and Marselan. A guide takes you on a half-day tour of 2-3 estates.

Halal dinner in the Hui quarter: lamb with hand-pulled noodles (lamian), Yellow River fish in vinegar, beef and cumin baozi. Hui-mei cuisine is dry and robust, heavily garlicked. Finish with "eight treasure tea": tea leaves, longans, jujubes, rock sugar crystals.

Ningxia cooking class with a Hui family: learn to hand-pull noodles (the gesture, the rhythm), prepare cumin lamb stew, and shape baozi. Tasting and discussion about Ramadan, holidays on the Chinese Islamic calendar. About half a day.

In the evening, climb to a rooftop bar terrace to see Yinchuan light up. The modern city is surprisingly polished, with its canals and parks. Cocktails made with local wines, fusion cuisine. Rest before the long desert stages ahead.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner Mongolia: Steppes, Dunes, and Forgotten Empires
Inner Mongolia: Steppes, Dunes, and Forgotten Empires

visits.text: Yining (or Ghulja in Uyghur) is the capital of the Ili Kazakh Autonomous Prefecture. Start at the Baytulla Mosque, built in 1773 and rebuilt in 1999, one of Xinjiang's principal mosques. Carved wood architecture, white minaret, paved courtyard. Men at prayer remain discreet toward respectful visitors. Modest dress required, no photos during prayers.

Yining's old Uyghur quarter is organized around the mosque. Courtyard houses, Ili poplar framework, doors painted turquoise and green. Wander at random through the lanes: children playing soccer, elders playing Uyghur chess, smell of bread emerging from the tandoor.

Hanbin Park, along the Ili River, welcomes residents each evening: spontaneous Kazakh and Uyghur dances, rose ice cream vendors, carnival rides. View of illuminated bridges at nightfall. Good place to understand the city's ethnic diversity (Uyghurs, Kazakhs, Han, Hui, Sibe).

activities.text: The Hami melon market near the Kazakh border (50 km, Khorgos sector) is one of China's most colorful. 15 kg watermelons, honey-flavored yellow melons, white peaches, seedless Turpan grapes. Kazakh truck drivers stop here between cross-border convoys. Allow 2 hours round trip from Yining.

Traditional Uyghur dinner in a mehmankhana (guesthouse): laghman (pulled noodles with lamb sauté), polo (pilaf rice), samsa (flaky meat pastries), sweet milk black tea. A few old quarter addresses offer festive menus with musicians.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Xinjiang - Kanas, Altai, and Ili Steppes in 16 Days

visits.text: Transit day between Guangxi and Fujian. From Sanjiang, drive to Guilin (about 2 hours, 130 km). Guilin → Xiamen flight (1.5 hours) then drive to Yongding tulou (3 hours), or Guilin → Longyan high-speed train (5 hours) then minibus to Yongding (1 hour). Arrival at tulou late afternoon.

The Hongkeng tulou cluster gathers Fujian's most famous earthen fortresses. Zhencheng Lou, nicknamed the "Prince of tulou," is a four-story circular structure built according to the Eight Trigrams plan. Its outer rammed-earth walls mixed with glutinous rice and brown sugar reach 1.5 meters thick at the base. The interior combines Chinese and Western elements—neoclassical columns in the courtyard testify to the influence of Hakka emigrants to Southeast Asia.

Chengqi Lou in Gaobei village is the "King of tulou": four concentric rings, 400 rooms across four floors, capable of housing 600 people from the same clan. The central courtyard shelters the ancestral temple. Each family has the same number of rooms on each level—a Hakka principle of strict equality.

In 2008, 46 tulou were inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list, including 23 in Yongding district alone. These communal dwellings, built from the 12th to the 20th century, could shelter up to 800 people behind walls 1 to 3 meters thick and up to 18 meters high. Single door, no ground-floor windows, loopholes on upper floors: fortresses against bandits.

activities.text: Overnight in an inhabited tulou, with a local family. Hakka families offer rooms on the upper floors. Dinner follows Hakka recipes: braised pork with pickled mustard greens, stuffed tofu, and local tea.

days_plan.itin_title: Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian
Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian

visits.text: Yuanyang's rice terraces cover 16,000 hectares on the flanks of Mount Ailao, shaped by the Hani people over more than 1,300 years. UNESCO listed them in 2013. The Duoyishu viewpoint, at 1,940 meters altitude, is best for sunrise: flooded terraces (November-March) transform into mirrors catching the first rays. Wake by 5:30 AM minimum.

In the afternoon, descend into an authentic Hani village - Pugao Laozhai or Azheke are the least touristy. The mushroom houses (conical thatched roofs) are still inhabited. Don't photograph without asking.

If you're there on a market day (12-day calendar, not weekly), Xinjie market gathers Hani, Yi, and Miao vendors from dawn. Embroidered textiles, medicinal plants, and live pigs mingle in a mountain bazaar atmosphere.

Laohuzui - Tiger's Mouth - is Yuanyang's third major viewpoint, 17 km west of Xinjie. Here terraces cascade fan-like from 1,800 meters down to the Red River valley. It's the most impressive panorama for its verticality. Best time: late afternoon.

The Hani sacred forest above the villages has been protected for centuries - the Hani believe it shelters ancestral spirits. Access is free but demands respect: no noise, no litter. The dense canopy creates a particular atmosphere.
Mengpin terraces, 8 km from Xinjie, are less visited and offer the same landscapes from a northern angle. Perfect for photography without crowds. The access road is unpaved - car or moto-taxi.

activities.text: Hike between the terraces from Duoyishu downslope: trails follow Hani irrigation canals (a 4,653 km network also listed). Count on 2 to 3 hours to descend to Qingkou village and climb back up by tuk-tuk.
At sunset, head to Bada viewpoint, 15 km south of Xinjie: west-facing terraces glow red in the last sun. The site is less crowded than Duoyishu and offers a different angle on the terraces' curves.

Join a Hani street banquet (长街宴, changjieyuan) if you're there during Kuzhaza (June or July by lunar calendar): hundreds of tables join into a single line in the main lane. Off-season, some hotels offer a reduced version for groups.
Before leaving, buy Hani red rice at Xinjie market - a local variety cultivated in flooded terraces, unavailable outside the region. It keeps for 6 months.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Yunnan - beyond the classic circuit
Deep Yunnan - beyond the classic circuit

visits.text: Launch the big trek from Xidang (2,700 m), an hour's drive from Feilai Si. Depending on road conditions, you'll hike in on foot (5-7 hours, 18 km, 1,100 m elevation gain) or catch an official SUV shuttle to Lower Yubeng (45 minutes, 30 yuan). The trail passes through Nanzheng Pass at 3,729 m, the climb's high point. Factor in oxygen breaks—you're above 3,500 m for half the journey.

Welcome to Yubeng, two Tibetan villages revealing themselves: Upper Yubeng (3,200 m) and Lower Yubeng (3,100 m), linked by a 30-minute trail. Roadless until recently, they've preserved traditional Tibetan wood-and-stone architecture. Around thirty families live here, most having opened their homes to trekkers. You're on the kora around Kawagebo, a major pilgrimage circuit.

Detour to the small Yubeng Temple (Yubeng Si), which shelters the tooth-shaped rock (Sacred Tooth Rock) said to have given the village its name. According to Tibetan legend, it represents a divine molar that fell from a protective deity of Kawagebo. Pilgrims touch it before departing for the sacred waterfall the next day—do as they do.

Tackle Yubeng's toughest hike, Glacier Lake (Bing Hu, 3,900 m): 12 km round trip, 700 m elevation gain, 6 to 8 hours with breaks. The trail departs Upper Yubeng, cuts through spruce forest, follows the glacial torrent, and ends in a high-altitude basin. The water is turquoise, the far shore a 200 m ice wall. Start at 7 AM, pack a warm lunch.

Halfway up, you'll reach Xiaonong Base Camp (3,623 m), the former campsite of the 1991 Sino-Japanese expedition. A memorial commemorates the 17 climbers lost in the avalanche. It's also a good spot to catch your breath, eat, and watch marmots emerge into the sun.

At the lake, listen: you'll hear water flowing under the ice. Walk carefully along the edges (never onto the central ice—hidden crevasses lurk). Above, a hanging glacier clings to Kawagebo: occasionally low rumbles echo through the basin—those are seracs collapsing.

activities.text: Once settled, grab a ginger tea at a guesthouse café—"Hi I'm Joe," run by a Hongkonger and a local, even serves Hong Kong-style desserts. Rest early—tomorrow you're climbing to the glacial lake at 3,900 m.
Eat light Tibetan fare tonight: tsampa (roasted barley flour), potato soup, momos. Avoid grease and alcohol—altitude doubles the effect.
Upon return to Upper Yubeng, schedule a hearty, early dinner (6-7 PM). Night falls fast at 3,100 m, and after 8 hours of walking, you'll be in bed by 9 PM without struggle.
If you've got energy left, catch the sunset on Mainri (one of the 13 peaks) from the meadow above the village, a 5-minute walk. Yaks come home at this hour—it's cinematic.

days_plan.itin_title: Three Parallel Rivers & Eastern Tibet - 14-Day Yunnan to Kham Extension
Three Parallel Rivers & Eastern Tibet - 14-Day Yunnan to Kham Extension

visits.text: Assumed transport day, no mid-route visit. Morning, descent from Mount Lushan to Jiujiang station. High-speed rail Jiujiang-Changsha South (2h), 45-min connection, high-speed rail Changsha South-Zhangjiajie West (2h30). The high-speed line to Zhangjiajie opened late 2019 and radically changes access to the old Hunan hinterland. Arrival late afternoon.

Wulingyuan, UNESCO-listed in 1992, opens five entrances onto 4,810 hectares. You'll sleep in Wulingyuan town, near the East Gate, more practical than Zhangjiajie city for the two hiking days that follow. Driver-guide picks you up at the West station, 40-min drive. Light Tujia dinner, rest. Trails begin tomorrow at 8 a.m.

Tianmen Shan peaks at 1518 m, 8 km from downtown - distinct from Wulingyuan National Forest Park. The cable car measures 7,455 m, one of the world's longest, opened in 2005. Flies over Tongtian Avenue, the 99-bend road. Allow 30 min ascent. Buddhist temple at summit, glass walkway glued to the cliff.

A natural arch 131.5 m high pierced through the cliff: Tianmen Cave, the Heaven's Gate. 999 steps climb to the arch. Russian fighter jets flew through it in 1999, event that launched local tourism. Today escalator in the cliff for those who don't climb. Vertiginous views, mineral and misty world.

Glass Bridge measures 430 m long and 300 m high, world's longest glass bridge at its 2016 opening. 50 km east of the main park, in Zhangjiajie Grand Canyon. Strong sensation for those who handle heights; others stay below, equally spectacular panorama from canyon bottom. Massive Chinese crowds.

activities.text: Book the local driver-guide in advance via a local agency or hotel in Wulingyuan: 800-1200 yuan per day for two days in the park. English-speaking difficult to find, Mandarin guaranteed - use Google Translate via Pleco. Guide simplifies multiple tickets and anticipates less crowded cable car time slots. Cash or WeChat Pay.

If you keep both sites in one day, it's exhausting: Tianmen morning (cable car 7:30 a.m.), express transfer to Grand Canyon early afternoon, Glass Bridge 4 p.m. 11-12h day, doable. Simpler: Tianmen alone, relaxed dinner in Zhangjiajie city. Glass Bridge then becomes optional extension.

Choose Tianmen in the morning, base cable car departure at 7:30 a.m. to avoid 1h30 queues at noon. Combined tickets (entry + cable car + escalators) 261 yuan. If yesterday was heavy at Wulingyuan, keep Tianmen alone and rest up for Furong tomorrow. Light meal below, summit restaurant is expensive and average.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie
Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie
Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie
Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi
Inner China: 21-day high-speed rail journey from Hunan to Guangxi

visits.text: Zhangye Danxia National Geological Park, designated a UNESCO Global Geopark in 2020, covers 322 km² in the northern foothills of the Qilian Mountains. Its striped sandstone formations—red, orange, yellow, green—resulted from 24 million years of sediment deposits, folded by the collision of the Indian and Eurasian plates (the same tectonic movement that formed the Himalayas), then sculpted by wind and rain. The colors come from oxidation of different minerals: hematite (red), limonite (brown), goethite (yellow), chlorite (green). Five viewing platforms connected by shuttle buses over 20 km let you explore the site in 3-4 hours. Sunset from Platform 1 brings out the most intense colors.

The Giant Buddha Temple of Zhangye (Dàfó Sì), built in 1098 during the Western Xia dynasty, houses a 34.5-meter-long reclining clay Buddha—one of China's largest. According to local tradition, Marco Polo stayed in this temple for a year during his passage along the Silk Road. The wooden and clay body is hollow: Buddhist objects were concealed inside for centuries.

activities.text: Watch sunset over Danxia's multicolored rock formations.
Drive through the Hexi Corridor, the ancient Silk Road trade route.

days_plan.itin_title: China's Silk Road: Xi'an to Kashgar via the Hexi Corridor

visits.text: Zhaoxing, according to the oral tradition of the Lu clan, was founded around 1160 during the Southern Song dynasty—over 850 years of history. It's China's largest Dong village: more than 800 families, 4,000 inhabitants. The village is divided into five quarters bearing the names of Confucian virtues—Ren (Benevolence), Yi (Righteousness), Li (Propriety), Zhi (Wisdom), Xin (Honesty). Each quarter has its own drum tower, covered bridge, and theater stage.

Zhaoxing's five drum towers, built entirely of fir wood without a single nail, measure from 14.9 to 25.9 meters. Four central columns rise to the top, twelve peripheral pillars symbolize the twelve months. Lintels bear carvings of dragons, serpents, turtles, and tigers—Dong totems. Originally, a drum at the summit served to sound the alarm in case of attack or fire.

Tangan village, 6 km from Zhaoxing, is an ecomuseum created jointly by China and Norway. Its 160 Dong families live in stilted houses surrounded by terraced rice paddies. The village preserves a traditional gateway, drum tower, ancient tombs, wells, rice granaries, and stone pathways—a coherent ensemble of Dong rural life.

activities.text: Dong Grand Song (Dong Da Ge) concert at the drum tower square. Inscribed on UNESCO's intangible heritage list since 2009, this multi-voice polyphonic singing is performed without choir leader, without sheet music, and without instrumental accompaniment. The melodies imitate nature's sounds—flowing water, birdsong, wind in the pines.

"Eight villages and one mountain" hike from Zhaoxing: the trail crosses Tangan, Xiage, Shangdiping, Dengjiang, Jitang, and Jilun, passing through the sacred mountain of Sa Sui, a female deity revered by the Dong. Rice terraces, bamboo forests, and wooden hamlets punctuate the route.

days_plan.itin_title: Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian
Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian

visits.text: The Henan Provincial Museum, in Zhengzhou, is one of China's great museums. Reopened in its new building in 2020, it brings together over 170,000 objects, including exceptional Shang collections (the Lian He You, the miniature Mu Wu Ding) and Zhou bronzes. Since Henan served as the cradle of nine dynasties, it offers a chronological reading of Chinese civilization. Budget 3 to 4 hours—it's free with your passport.

If archaeology has captured you, make a detour to the Erlitou Site Museum, 1 hour by car from Zhengzhou. This is where the remains of the Xia dynasty (circa 1750–1530 BC) were allegedly identified—the first Chinese dynasty according to ancient texts. The building is new (2019), spectacular, but the most prestigious pieces are… in Zhengzhou.

The ramparts of Shang-era Zhengzhou, dated to the 16th century BC, are the oldest surviving city walls in China. You can walk along them—it's free—and you'd never guess you're treading on 3,600 years of urban history. Several sections remain in the southeast of the modern city.

For your last morning, save time for the February 7th Memorial Tower (Erqi), at the heart of Zhengzhou. Built in 1971 to commemorate the great railway workers' strike of 1923, violently suppressed by warlords, it's a twin pagoda of 14 stories that has become the modern symbol of the city. Budget 30 minutes, mainly for the view from the top.

Before your flight, make one last stop at Zhengzhou's jade market (Henan Yu City), one of China's largest for Nanyang jade. Even without buying, it's instructive to see the polishing techniques and stone varieties.

Head to Zhengzhou Xinzheng International Airport (CGO), 35 km southeast. Direct flights to Beijing, Shanghai, Hong Kong, and several international destinations including Paris, Frankfurt, and Bangkok. Budget 45 minutes by taxi, 1 hour by metro (airport line from Zhengzhou East station).

activities.text: In the late afternoon, visit the Yellow River 30 km north of Zhengzhou (Yellow River Scenic Area). The "cradle of the Chinese nation" flows here wide and muddy, and you can see the colossal statue of the two mythical emperors Yan and Huang carved into the cliff (106 m tall, one of the world's largest statues, completed in 2007). It's a bit kitschy, but the river is awe-inspiring.

If your flight leaves in the evening, taste Henan cuisine one last time: hui mian (thick mutton noodles), Zhengzhou's specialty, at a traditional spot like Hesheng or Xiao Ji. It's rustic, generous, and far better than airport food.

days_plan.itin_title: Spiritual Henan & Shaanxi – Tracing the Cradle of Chinese Civilization
Spiritual Henan & Shaanxi – Tracing the Cradle of Chinese Civilization

visits.text: Head to Zhongwei, 220 km southwest of Yinchuan, to reach Shapotou—Ningxia's most spectacular site. The area, 17 km west of town, is the only place where the Tengger Desert plunges directly into the Yellow River. The dividing line is visible from the dunes: yellow sand on one side, ochre water and green oasis on the other.

Before the experience, climb Shapotou's great sand slide—1,000 meters long, 100 meters high, slope at 60 degrees. It's China's largest natural slide, and one of the country's four "singing sand" fields. When you descend, the sand produces a deep rumble, likened to distant bells. An unexplained phenomenon that has fascinated travelers for centuries.

Gao Temple, right in downtown Zhongwei, is a curiosity: one of the rare buildings in the world where three religions coexist—Buddhism, Taoism, and Confucianism. Built during the Ming era, restored under the Qing, the architecture blends the codes of all three traditions. Small but remarkable, see it before or after Shapotou.

activities.text: Drift down the Yellow River on a sheepskin raft (yangpi fazi). The technique is 2,000 years old: assemble around twenty inflated sheepskins on a bamboo frame. Capacity of 1 ton, 3-4 passengers. Inscribed on Ningxia's intangible heritage list since 2009. The raft drifts 30-40 minutes with the current, the rower sings traditional songs.

In the northern part of Shapotou, camel ride into the Tengger Desert. The caravan follows the ancient Silk Road route, which passed precisely here to reach the Hexi Corridor. Allow 1.5 hours for a loop. Bring a face scarf—sand flies when the wind picks up.

At sunset, climb the main dune to watch the sky turn pink then mauve over the Yellow River. One of the most beautiful views in northwest China. Few people after 6 PM, the tour groups having already left. Bring a thermos of tea and a blanket.

days_plan.itin_title: Inner Mongolia: Steppes, Dunes, and Forgotten Empires

visits.text: Zhouzhuang (周庄) owes its fame to the Twin Bridges (双桥) - two adjacent 16th-century bridges forming together the motif of an ancient key. In 1984, painter Chen Yifei created a canvas of them that sold for $550,000 in New York, and Zhouzhuang became famous overnight. Arrive early in the morning before tour buses.

The Shen House (沈厅), built in the early 18th century, is the most complete example of a Jiangnan merchant residence: seven successive courtyards, about 100 rooms, separate kitchen accessible from the river via a water gate - deliveries arrived directly by boat.

activities.text: Explore the village's interior lanes, away from the main circuit. The most authentic houses are in the side alleys where residents bring out their chairs in late afternoon - ideal time for street photography.

days_plan.itin_title: Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

visits.text: Flower Lake (Huahu), in the Zoige wetlands reserve, is a shallow lake surrounded by marshy grasslands at 3,400 meters elevation. From June through August, carpets of wildflowers surround its waters. Wooden boardwalks cross the marshes without damaging them. The reserve protects one of the world's largest high-altitude wetlands.

The First Bend of the Yellow River at Tangke: the river, coming from the Tibetan Plateau, traces a monumental loop through the grassland here before turning north again. At sunset, from the viewing platform, the river gleams like a golden ribbon across the steppe. It's one of the most photographed landscapes of the eastern Tibetan Plateau.

activities.text: Wildlife watching on the Zoige grasslands: black-necked cranes (protected species), Himalayan marmots, Tibetan foxes, and herds of domestic and wild yaks. Binoculars recommended.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou
